acceptance:
- [ ] WorkerManager starts a supervisor goroutine on first worker launch
- [ ] Watchdog reaps workers past `WatchdogDeadline` with no phase transition in `StallDeadline`
- [ ] SIGTERM→SIGKILL escalation works on an intentionally-wedged test worker (unit test with a goroutine that ignores ctx)
- [ ] Reaped worker's bead claim is released (tracker status returns to open or claim-expired)
- [ ] `bead.reaped` event recorded on tracker with reason="watchdog" and duration
- [ ] Watchdog deadlines are configurable via ddx server config
description:
## Residual risk after RC1–RC4 from ddx-0a651925

Even with context propagation (RC1), wall-clock bounds (RC2), tightened breakers (RC3), and provider deadlines (RC4), a goroutine-level deadlock or panic-swallowed handler could still leave a worker alive past any reasonable bound. Today, `server.WorkerManager.Stop` (workers.go:648) only calls `handle.cancel()` — it has no escalation to OS-level signal and no autonomous trigger. ddx-b808df39 adds the *operator* tool; this bead adds the *autonomous* watchdog.

## Scope

1. WorkerManager runs a supervisor goroutine that, every minute, inspects each `workerHandle`.
2. If `time.Since(record.StartedAt) > WatchdogDeadline` (default e.g. 6h) AND `record.CurrentAttempt` has not transitioned in `StallDeadline` (default e.g. 1h), escalate:
   - Send SIGTERM to the worker's process group (requires surfacing PID, aligned with ddx-b808df39).
   - After 30s grace, send SIGKILL.
   - Mark record.State = "reaped"; update claimed bead state so the claim is not leaked.
3. Emit a `bead.reaped` event on the tracker with the full timeline so post-mortems are easy.

## Non-goals

- Per-bead runtime tuning; defaults are enough.
- Replacing ddx-b808df39 — this is defense-in-depth, not duplication. ddx-b808df39 is operator-initiated; this bead is system-initiated.

## Dependencies

- ddx-b808df39 surfaces PID in `ddx agent workers --json` output; this bead reuses that plumbing.

title: execute-bead: autonomous worker watchdog with process-level reaper

acceptance:
- [ ] Audit report (checked-in .md or bead-comment) lists every `http.Client.Do`-equivalent call in agent providers with its current deadline semantics
- [ ] Each main provider call path installs a per-request deadline (default 15m or catalog-configurable)
- [ ] Streaming body reads terminate within a bounded idle period (ReadDeadline or equivalent)
- [ ] Integration test: fake HTTP server accepts the connection, sends headers, then stops responding. Provider call returns within deadline with a distinct error
description:
## Root cause RC4 from ddx-0a651925

After RC1 is fixed (caller ctx propagates), provider HTTP calls still need per-request deadlines. A stalled TCP socket that delivers headers and then stops sending body bytes — particularly during streaming token reads — can hang a goroutine for days.

Grepping the `DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14` module cache shows short timeouts only on discovery probes (`provider/openai/discovery.go:46,174`, `openai.go:123`). The main `Run` call path inside agent providers appears to inherit whatever ctx DDx passes — which today is `context.Background()` (see RC1).

## Scope

1. Audit `openai`, `anthropic`, `virtual` providers in the agent library for per-request deadline usage.
2. Install a per-request deadline (default ~10-15 min) on each provider call; separate from the wall-clock bound in RC2.
3. For streaming bodies, enforce an **idle-read limit** on the response body so a mid-stream stall cannot hold a goroutine indefinitely (e.g. `http.Transport.ResponseHeaderTimeout` + periodic `SetReadDeadline` on the underlying conn).
4. Surface provider-timeout failures as a distinct status in `ExecuteBeadResult.Detail`.

## Non-goals

- Rewriting providers; minimally invasive change only.
- Changing DDx-side timeout defaults (RC2 covers outer bound).

## Prerequisite

Sibling RC1 bead must be resolved first, otherwise adding deadlines inside providers is useless while DDx still feeds them `context.Background()`.

title: execute-bead: audit and enforce per-request HTTP deadlines on agent provider calls

acceptance:
- [ ] `RunOptions` accepts a caller ctx, or `Runner.Run`/`Runner.RunAgent` accept ctx explicitly
- [ ] The internal cancel is derived from the caller ctx (no `context.Background()` seeds inside Run/RunAgent)
- [ ] `agent.ExecuteBead` takes ctx and passes it down
- [ ] `server.WorkerManager.Stop(id)` cancels the running agent provider call within 2s in a unit test (fake provider blocks on ctx)
- [ ] Regression test: runner with a canceled ctx returns promptly (<500ms) regardless of mocked provider latency
description:
## Root cause RC1 from ddx-0a651925

`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (Runner.Run) and `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (Runner.RunAgent) both call `context.WithCancel(context.Background())`, discarding any context from the caller. `RunOptions` exposes no ctx field. `agent.ExecuteBead` likewise takes no ctx, so `server.WorkerManager.runWorker`'s ctx never reaches the agent call.

As a result, `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (server/workers.go:648) cancels only the worker goroutine; the embedded `agentlib.Run` call (and any subprocess it launched) keeps running until SIGKILL. This is the structural defect behind 33–142h hung workers.

## Scope

1. Add `Context context.Context` to `RunOptions` (or change signatures to `Run(ctx, opts)`). Default to `context.Background()` when nil.
2. Derive the internal ctx from the caller's rather than `context.Background()`.
3. Thread ctx through `agent.ExecuteBead` → `runner.Run` → `agentlib.Run` → provider HTTP.
4. Update `server.WorkerManager.runWorker`'s `singleTierAttempt` closure to pass `ctx` into `agent.ExecuteBead`.

## Non-goals

- New wall-clock timeout logic (covered by sibling RC2 bead)
- Operator-facing stop command (covered by ddx-b808df39)

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01ULHZxdBTyh4d5yevDYDzq9","type":"tool_result","content":"diff --git a/cli/internal/agent/runner_context_test.go b/cli/internal/agent/runner_context_test.go
new file mode 100644
index 00000000..4a47e35d
--- /dev/null
+++ b/cli/internal/agent/runner_context_test.go
@@ -0,0 +1,138 @@
+package agent
+
+import (
+	"context"
+	"testing"
+	"time"
+
+	agentlib "github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent"
+	"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
+	"github.com/stretchr/testify/require"
+)
+
+// TestRunAgentCancelledContextReturnsPromptly verifies that when the caller
+// supplies an already-canceled context via RunOptions.Context, RunAgent
+// returns within 500ms regardless of how long the provider would otherwise
+// block. Regression anchor for ddx-0a651925 RC1: runner.Run and
+// Runner.RunAgent previously discarded the caller's ctx by constructing
+// their own context.WithCancel(context.Background()), so WorkerManager.Stop
+// could not cancel the in-flight agent call.
+func TestRunAgentCancelledContextReturnsPromptly(t *testing.T) {
+	provider := &sleepProvider{
+		delay: 30 * time.Second, // far larger than the 500ms AC deadline
+		response: agentlib.Response{
+			Content: "never-returned",
+			Model:   "test-model",
+		},
+	}
+
+	r := NewRunner(Config{SessionLogDir: t.TempDir()})
+	r.LookPath = mockLookPath
+	r.AgentProvider = provider
+
+	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
+	cancel() // pre-cancel so the call returns on entry
+
+	start := time.Now()
+	result, err := r.RunAgent(RunOptions{
+		Harness: "agent",
+		Prompt:  "test",
+		Context: ctx,
+	})
+	elapsed := time.Since(start)
+
+	require.NoError(t, err)
+	require.NotNil(t, result)
+	assert.Equal(t, 1, result.ExitCode, "canceled run should report non-zero exit")
+	assert.NotEmpty(t, result.Error, "canceled run should carry error text")
+	assert.Less(t, elapsed, 500*time.Millisecond,
+		"canceled ctx must abort the provider call promptly; took %v", elapsed)
+}
+
+// TestRunAgentContextCancelledMidRunReturnsPromptly cancels the caller's
+// context while the provider is blocked, then asserts that the runner
+// unwinds within 500ms of cancellation. This exercises the propagation
+// path (caller ctx → internal WithCancel(parent) → agentlib.Run → provider
+// Chat) rather than the short-circuit pre-canceled case.
+func TestRunAgentContextCancelledMidRunReturnsPromptly(t *testing.T) {
+	provider := &sleepProvider{
+		delay: 30 * time.Second,
+		response: agentlib.Response{
+			Content: "never-returned",
+			Model:   "test-model",
+		},
+	}
+
+	r := NewRunner(Config{SessionLogDir: t.TempDir()})
+	r.LookPath = mockLookPath
+	r.AgentProvider = provider
+
+	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
+	// Cancel after a brief warmup so we measure the cancel→return latency.
+	go func() {
+		time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
+		cancel()
+	}()
+
+	start := time.Now()
+	result, err := r.RunAgent(RunOptions{
+		Harness: "agent",
+		Prompt:  "test",
+		Context: ctx,
+	})
+	elapsed := time.Since(start)
+
+	require.NoError(t, err)
+	require.NotNil(t, result)
+	assert.Equal(t, 1, result.ExitCode)
+	assert.Less(t, elapsed, 500*time.Millisecond,
+		"caller cancel must propagate within 500ms; took %v", elapsed)
+}
+
+// TestRunCancelledContextReturnsPromptlySubprocess verifies the same
+// cancellation contract for the subprocess dispatch path through Runner.Run.
+// The OSExecutor honours ctx by sending SIGKILL on ctx.Done, so a canceled
+// ctx must not leave the runner blocked on the child exit.
+func TestRunCancelledContextReturnsPromptlySubprocess(t *testing.T) {
+	// Use the mockExecutor (in-process fake) with a blocking delay. A
+	// canceled context should short-circuit execution before any exec
+	// happens, rather than waiting for the mock.
+	exec := &blockingMockExecutor{block: 30 * time.Second}
+	r := newTestRunner(&mockExecutor{output: "ok"})
+	r.Executor = exec
+
+	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
+	cancel()
+
+	start := time.Now()
+	result, err := r.Run(RunOptions{
+		Harness: "codex",
+		Prompt:  "hello",
+		Context: ctx,
+	})
+	elapsed := time.Since(start)
+
+	require.NoError(t, err)
+	require.NotNil(t, result)
+	assert.Less(t, elapsed, 500*time.Millisecond,
+		"canceled ctx must abort subprocess dispatch promptly; took %v", elapsed)
+}
+
+// blockingMockExecutor is an Executor whose ExecuteInDir respects ctx: it
+// either blocks for `block` or returns ctx.Err() when ctx is canceled.
+type blockingMockExecutor struct {
+	block time.Duration
+}
+
+func (e *blockingMockExecutor) Execute(ctx context.Context, binary string, args []string, stdin string) (*ExecResult, error) {
+	return e.ExecuteInDir(ctx, binary, args, stdin, "")
+}
+
+func (e *blockingMockExecutor) ExecuteInDir(ctx context.Context, _ string, _ []string, _ string, _ string) (*ExecResult, error) {
+	select {
+	case <-time.After(e.block):
+		return &ExecResult{Stdout: "", ExitCode: 0}, nil
+	case <-ctx.Done():
+		return &ExecResult{ExitCode: -1}, ctx.Err()
+	}
+}

+package server
+
+import (
+	"context"
+	"sync/atomic"
+	"testing"
+	"time"
+
+	"github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/agent"
+	"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
+	"github.com/stretchr/testify/require"
+)
+
+// TestWorkerManagerStopCancelsAgentExecution is the regression test for
+// ddx-0a651925 RC1: WorkerManager.Stop(id) must cancel the in-flight
+// executor (and transitively the agent provider call) via ctx propagation,
+// not merely close the poll loop. Pre-fix, runner.Run / RunAgent discarded
+// the caller ctx by using context.WithCancel(context.Background()), so
+// cancelling the worker goroutine left the agent subprocess / HTTP call
+// running until SIGKILL. With ctx threaded into RunOptions.Context, Stop's
+// cancellation propagates into the executor within seconds.
+//
+// The fake executor here blocks on ctx (exactly like a real provider's
+// Chat method), so a successful test proves cancellation reaches the
+// provider boundary.
+func TestWorkerManagerStopCancelsAgentExecution(t *testing.T) {
+	root := t.TempDir()
+	setupBeadStoreWithReadyBead(t, root)
+
+	var executorStarted atomic.Bool
+	var executorCtxErr atomic.Value // stores the ctx.Err() observed when the executor unblocks
+	executorDone := make(chan struct{})
+
+	m := NewWorkerManager(root)
+	m.BeadWorkerFactory = func(s agent.ExecuteBeadLoopStore) *agent.ExecuteBeadWorker {
+		return &agent.ExecuteBeadWorker{
+			Store: s,
+			Executor: agent.ExecuteBeadExecutorFunc(func(ctx context.Context, beadID string) (agent.ExecuteBeadReport, error) {
+				executorStarted.Store(true)
+				// Block on ctx — mirrors what a real agent provider HTTP call
+				// does when ctx is threaded all the way down.
+				select {
+				case <-ctx.Done():
+					executorCtxErr.Store(ctx.Err())
+				case <-time.After(60 * time.Second):
+					// If we get here, ctx cancellation never propagated.
+				}
+				close(executorDone)
+				return agent.ExecuteBeadReport{
+					BeadID: beadID,
+					Status: agent.ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed,
+					Detail: "canceled",
+				}, nil
+			}),
+		}
+	}
+
+	record, err := m.StartExecuteLoop(ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec{
+		PollInterval: 30 * time.Second,
+	})
+	require.NoError(t, err)
+
+	// Wait until the executor has actually started running so we know the
+	// cancellation we exercise is against an in-flight call.
+	require.Eventually(t, executorStarted.Load, 3*time.Second, 20*time.Millisecond,
+		"executor never entered the blocking Chat call")
+
+	stopStart := time.Now()
+	require.NoError(t, m.Stop(record.ID))
+
+	select {
+	case <-executorDone:
+	case <-time.After(2 * time.Second):
+		t.Fatalf("executor was not canceled within 2s of WorkerManager.Stop")
+	}
+	elapsed := time.Since(stopStart)
+	assert.Less(t, elapsed, 2*time.Second,
+		"Stop should cancel the running agent call within 2s; took %v", elapsed)
+
+	observedErr, _ := executorCtxErr.Load().(error)
+	assert.ErrorIs(t, observedErr, context.Canceled,
+		"executor should observe context.Canceled from the caller ctx; got %v", observedErr)
+
+	// Ensure the worker goroutine also exits.
+	_ = waitForWorkerExit(t, m, record.ID, 5*time.Second)
+}","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"8e7910e8-fc0d-48fa-99d0-f3a12ea80392","uuid":"3fe23d3a-4723-45b7-889a-0b90ca59dfda","timestamp":"2026-04-18T05:15:09.240Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"diff --git a/cli/internal/agent/runner_context_test.go b/cli/internal/agent/runner_context_test.go

+package agent
+
+import (
+	"context"
+	"testing"
+	"time"
+
+	agentlib "github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent"
+	"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
+	"github.com/stretchr/testify/require"
+)
+
+// TestRunAgentCancelledContextReturnsPromptly verifies that when the caller
+// supplies an already-canceled context via RunOptions.Context, RunAgent
+// returns within 500ms regardless of how long the provider would otherwise
+// block. Regression anchor for ddx-0a651925 RC1: runner.Run and
+// Runner.RunAgent previously discarded the caller's ctx by constructing
+// their own context.WithCancel(context.Background()), so WorkerManager.Stop
+// could not cancel the in-flight agent call.
+func TestRunAgentCancelledContextReturnsPromptly(t *testing.T) {
+	provider := &sleepProvider{
+		delay: 30 * time.Second, // far larger than the 500ms AC deadline
+		response: agentlib.Response{
+			Content: "never-returned",
+			Model:   "test-model",
+		},
+	}
+
+	r := NewRunner(Config{SessionLogDir: t.TempDir()})
+	r.LookPath = mockLookPath
+	r.AgentProvider = provider
+
+	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
+	cancel() // pre-cancel so the call returns on entry
+
+	start := time.Now()
+	result, err := r.RunAgent(RunOptions{
+		Harness: "agent",
+		Prompt:  "test",
+		Context: ctx,
+	})
+	elapsed := time.Since(start)
+
+	require.NoError(t, err)
+	require.NotNil(t, result)
+	assert.Equal(t, 1, result.ExitCode, "canceled run should report non-zero exit")
+	assert.NotEmpty(t, result.Error, "canceled run should carry error text")
+	assert.Less(t, elapsed, 500*time.Millisecond,
+		"canceled ctx must abort the provider call promptly; took %v", elapsed)
+}
+
+// TestRunAgentContextCancelledMidRunReturnsPromptly cancels the caller's
+// context while the provider is blocked, then asserts that the runner
+// unwinds within 500ms of cancellation. This exercises the propagation
+// path (caller ctx → internal WithCancel(parent) → agentlib.Run → provider
+// Chat) rather than the short-circuit pre-canceled case.
+func TestRunAgentContextCancelledMidRunReturnsPromptly(t *testing.T) {
+	provider := &sleepProvider{
+		delay: 30 * time.Second,
+		response: agentlib.Response{
+			Content: "never-returned",
+			Model:   "test-model",
+		},
+	}
+
+	r := NewRunner(Config{SessionLogDir: t.TempDir()})
+	r.LookPath = mockLookPath
+	r.AgentProvider = provider
+
+	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
+	// Cancel after a brief warmup so we measure the cancel→return latency.
+	go func() {
+		time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
+		cancel()
+	}()
+
+	start := time.Now()
+	result, err := r.RunAgent(RunOptions{
+		Harness: "agent",
+		Prompt:  "test",
+		Context: ctx,
+	})
+	elapsed := time.Since(start)
+
+	require.NoError(t, err)
+	require.NotNil(t, result)
+	assert.Equal(t, 1, result.ExitCode)
+	assert.Less(t, elapsed, 500*time.Millisecond,
+		"caller cancel must propagate within 500ms; took %v", elapsed)
+}
+
+// TestRunCancelledContextReturnsPromptlySubprocess verifies the same
+// cancellation contract for the subprocess dispatch path through Runner.Run.
+// The OSExecutor honours ctx by sending SIGKILL on ctx.Done, so a canceled
+// ctx must not leave the runner blocked on the child exit.
+func TestRunCancelledContextReturnsPromptlySubprocess(t *testing.T) {
+	// Use the mockExecutor (in-process fake) with a blocking delay. A
+	// canceled context should short-circuit execution before any exec
+	// happens, rather than waiting for the mock.
+	exec := &blockingMockExecutor{block: 30 * time.Second}
+	r := newTestRunner(&mockExecutor{output: "ok"})
+	r.Executor = exec
+
+	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
+	cancel()
+
+	start := time.Now()
+	result, err := r.Run(RunOptions{
+		Harness: "codex",
+		Prompt:  "hello",
+		Context: ctx,
+	})
+	elapsed := time.Since(start)
+
+	require.NoError(t, err)
+	require.NotNil(t, result)
+	assert.Less(t, elapsed, 500*time.Millisecond,
+		"canceled ctx must abort subprocess dispatch promptly; took %v", elapsed)
+}
+
+// blockingMockExecutor is an Executor whose ExecuteInDir respects ctx: it
+// either blocks for `block` or returns ctx.Err() when ctx is canceled.
+type blockingMockExecutor struct {
+	block time.Duration
+}
+
+func (e *blockingMockExecutor) Execute(ctx context.Context, binary string, args []string, stdin string) (*ExecResult, error) {
+	return e.ExecuteInDir(ctx, binary, args, stdin, "")
+}
+
+func (e *blockingMockExecutor) ExecuteInDir(ctx context.Context, _ string, _ []string, _ string, _ string) (*ExecResult, error) {
+	select {
+	case <-time.After(e.block):
+		return &ExecResult{Stdout: "", ExitCode: 0}, nil
+	case <-ctx.Done():
+		return &ExecResult{ExitCode: -1}, ctx.Err()
+	}
+}

title: execute-bead: propagate caller context through Runner.Run and RunAgent

acceptance:
- [ ] Compaction-stuck detection fires within 15 minutes when no-op compaction events are the only activity, regardless of event rate
- [ ] No regression on the existing behaviour for healthy agents (normal runs do not trip)
- [ ] Unit test: fake agentlib emits one no-op compaction event every 3 seconds → breaker fires within 15 min
- [ ] Result.json `detail` clearly reports the time-based breaker when it fires
description:
## Root cause RC3 from ddx-0a651925

`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:218-241` trips at `stuckThreshold = maxConsecutiveCompactionFailures` (50 by default). Observed on bundle `20260416T230316-efbf91c5`: the breaker took **2h 8m** to fire (7,720,908 ms). If each iteration takes ~3 min to emit a no-op compaction event, 50 events = ~2.5h. The counter is useful but fires far too late to be a hang-guard.

## Scope

Either:
1. **Time-bounded** — fire after N no-op events **within M minutes** (e.g. 10 within 15m), OR
2. **Threshold lowered** — drop default from 50 to ~10 (only for agent harness where one iteration is already expensive), OR
3. **Wall-clock guard layered on top** — independent breaker fires after e.g. 20 min of consecutive no-op compaction events regardless of count.

Recommend option 1 or 3: time-based thresholds are more robust to provider-speed variance.

## Non-goals

- Changing the compaction algorithm itself.
- Extending breakers outside the compaction path.

title: execute-bead: bound compaction-stuck breaker by wall-clock time

acceptance:
- [ ] New default wall-clock deadline (e.g. 3h) applied to both `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` and `Runner.RunAgent`
- [ ] Wall-clock timeout fires even when stdout/stderr/agent events are flowing (unit test: fake provider emits 1 event/sec for > deadline, expect cancel at deadline ± 1s)
- [ ] Idle timeout behaviour preserved (unit test: no activity for idle timeout → fire)
- [ ] Result.json distinguishes `timeout: wall-clock` from `timeout: idle`
- [ ] Documented in types.go (DefaultWallClockMS constant with comment)
description:
## Root cause RC2 from ddx-0a651925

The `DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000` (2h) in `cli/internal/agent/types.go:363` is advertised as a hard cap but implemented as an *idle timeout*:

- `cli/internal/agent/executor.go:154-178` resets `time.NewTimer(idleTimeout)` on every stdout/stderr byte.
- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:264-285` resets on every non-compaction agentlib event.

A provider that emits any heartbeat, retry line, or periodic event defeats the timer indefinitely — enabling the 33–142h hangs observed on agent projects.

Only the claude streaming path (`cli/internal/agent/claude_stream.go:373-384`) uses a true wall-clock deadline via `time.After(timeout)`. That path is why bundle `20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba` fired at exactly 2h 0m 0.098s — and nothing else does.

## Scope

1. Add an absolute wall-clock deadline in addition to (not replacing) the idle timeout.
2. Default the wall-clock bound to something operators can trust — e.g. 3x `DefaultTimeoutMS` or a new `DefaultWallClockMS`.
3. Apply in: `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` (subprocess path) and `Runner.RunAgent` (embedded agent path).
4. Reflect the wall-clock error in result.json as a distinct `error: "wall-clock deadline exceeded after Xh"` so it is not confused with idle timeout.

## Non-goals

- Lowering or removing the existing idle timeout.
- Per-provider tuning (can be follow-up).

1. EITHER: closed as 'covered by tool-calling gating' with a written justification (e.g., test or comment in the tool-calling bead's deliverables that asserts SupportsTools => SupportsStructuredOutput for all currently routed providers).
2. OR: SupportsStructuredOutput tri-state added to ModelEntry/Surface + capabilities table, with rejection logic in evaluateCandidate, RouteRequest.RequiresStructuredOutput field, and unit tests parallel to the tool-calling bead's tests.
3. go test ./cli/... is green (only applies to outcome 2).
description:
## Lowest-priority sibling of ddx-4817edfd.

Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / response_format=json_object / tool-use-required. If a bead prompt depends on structured output, today routing's blindness means the failure surfaces at dispatch.

Per the parent bead's analysis, this is probably covered incidentally by the tool-calling gating sub-bead — most reasoning-tool-capable models also support structured output. This bead exists to either (a) explicitly add a SupportsStructuredOutput capability, or (b) close as 'covered by tool-calling gating' once that bead lands and we verify the assumption.

## Decision gate (do this first)

Before writing code, verify the 'covered by tool-calling' hypothesis:
- Survey the providers we currently route to (anthropic, openai, openrouter, omlx, lmstudio, ollama) and check whether each one's tool-calling support implies JSON-mode support.
- If yes everywhere we care about: close this bead with a note in the tool-calling bead's tests / docs that asserts the implication.
- If no (any counter-example): proceed with scope below.

## Scope (only if decision gate fails)

- Add SupportsStructuredOutput tri-state to ModelEntry / Surface and to capabilities.go's curated table.
- RouteRequest.RequiresStructuredOutput bool; execute-bead sets it when the prompt requires JSON mode.
- evaluateCandidate rejects RequiresStructuredOutput=true on SupportsStructuredOutput=no. Unknown passes with -v warning.

## Files (only if decision gate fails)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go
- cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go
notes: Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing
title: routing: structured-output (JSON-mode) gating — likely incidental to tool-calling

acceptance:
1. ModelEntry / Surface in catalog.go gain SupportsTools (tri-state: yes / no / unknown).
2. capabilities.go (new file) ships a curated default table for known harness+flavor pairs.
3. Discovery code populates SupportsTools from /v1/models/status when the field is present in the response.
4. RouteRequest.RequiresTools added; execute-bead sets it true for bead execution.
5. evaluateCandidate rejects RequiresTools=true candidates known to be SupportsTools=no, with a debug reason.
6. Unknown SupportsTools does not reject; logs at -v.
7. Unit tests cover: known-no rejection, known-yes pass, unknown pass with warning, RequiresTools=false disables the check.
8. go test ./cli/... is green.
description:
## Reject routes that can't actually run tool-calling work (sibling of ddx-4817edfd).

Some omlx flavors and older local OpenAI-compat servers do not implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Today routing fuzzy-matches them and dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' — or worse, silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop cannot consume.

## Scope

- Per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata: SupportsTools bool. Two layers:
  1. Hand-curated table for known harnesses (claude, codex, opus, etc.) and provider flavors (openai, anthropic, openrouter, omlx, lmstudio, ollama).
  2. Discovery-driven enrichment when /v1/models/status (or equivalent) advertises tool support per model.
- RouteRequest gains RequiresTools bool (default false to preserve current behavior; execute-bead sets true).
- evaluateCandidate rejects candidates where RequiresTools=true and the resolved (provider, flavor, model) combo is known NOT to support tools. Unknown = pass with -v warning, same pattern as context-window bead.
- Capability metadata location: cli/internal/agent/catalog.go on ModelEntry/Surface, plus a curated table in cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go (or co-located).

## Out of scope

- Context-window, structured-output, effort, permissions gating (sibling beads).
- Probing servers actively at routing time (uses cached discovery + table).

## Files

- cli/internal/agent/types.go (RouteRequest.RequiresTools, capability fields on CandidatePlan)
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go (rejection in rejectIfNotViable / evaluateCandidate)
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go (SupportsTools on ModelEntry / Surface)
- cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go (NEW — curated table)
notes: Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing
title: routing: tool-calling support gating with per-flavor capability metadata

acceptance:
1. Capability table tracks SupportsEffort and SupportsPermission per (provider, model_pattern).
2. evaluateCandidate rejects candidates that don't honor a RouteRequest's --effort or --permissions, with a debug reason that names which capability and which provider/model.
3. Unknown capability does NOT reject; logs at -v.
4. Unit tests cover: known-no-effort rejection, known-yes pass, unknown pass with warning, no-effort-requested disables the check (parallel structure for permissions).
5. go test ./cli/... is green.
description:
## Provider-specific honoring of --effort and --permissions (sibling of ddx-4817edfd).

Today, Harness.EffortFlag is checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable, so harnesses without effort support are rejected when --effort is set. But provider-level gating is missing: e.g., openrouter doesn't honor --effort on non-reasoning models, and --permissions unrestricted may not apply to every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and silently no-op the flag.

## Scope

- Add effort/permissions capability fields to the curated capability table (same file added by the tool-calling sub-bead, or co-locate here if that one ships first).
  - SupportsEffort tri-state per (provider, model).
  - SupportsPermission(permission_class) — at minimum 'unrestricted'.
- evaluateCandidate / rejectIfNotViable: when RouteRequest names an --effort or --permissions value not honored by the resolved (provider, model) combo, reject with a clear reason. Unknown = pass with -v warning.
- Document expected behavior for each known provider in capabilities.go comments (openrouter non-reasoning models = no effort, etc.).

## Out of scope

- Context-window, tool-calling, structured-output gating (sibling beads).
- Building a 'downgrade' path (e.g., silently strip --effort and continue). Rejection is sufficient for v1; downgrade is a follow-up.

## Files

- cli/internal/agent/routing.go
- cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go (shared with tool-calling bead — or new file if order differs)
- cli/internal/agent/types.go
notes: Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing
title: routing: effort/permissions gating per provider+model (not just per harness)

acceptance:
1. RouteRequest has EstimatedPromptTokens uint32 field, zero value means 'unspecified'.
2. evaluateCandidate rejects candidates whose known context window < EstimatedPromptTokens; emits a routing-debug reason that names the model and its window.
3. Unknown context window does NOT cause rejection; logs a warning at -v.
4. execute-bead populates EstimatedPromptTokens with at least a char/4 estimate of the assembled prompt.
5. Unit test in routing_test.go covers: (a) below-window rejection, (b) above-window pass, (c) unknown-window pass with warning, (d) EstimatedPromptTokens=0 disables the check.
6. go test ./cli/... is green.
description:
## Smallest useful slice of capability gating (sibling of ddx-4817edfd).

Routing currently does not check whether the resolved model's context window can hold the bead prompt. A 32k-context fuzzy match against an 80k-token bead prompt routes 'successfully' and fails mid-dispatch with a provider 400 or truncated output.

## Scope

- Add optional EstimatedPromptTokens (uint32) to RouteRequest (cli/internal/agent/types.go).
- In evaluateCandidate (cli/internal/agent/routing.go), when EstimatedPromptTokens is set and > 0, reject (or penalize) candidates whose concrete_model context window is below it.
- Source of context window per candidate: v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits first, then catalog ModelEntry.ContextWindow (cli/internal/agent/catalog.go) as fallback. If neither is known, treat as 'unknown' — do NOT reject (avoid false negatives), but log at -v.
- Caller side: pass EstimatedPromptTokens from execute-bead when the bead prompt is assembled (best-effort — can use char/4 heuristic if a tokenizer is unavailable).

## Out of scope

- Tool-calling, structured-output, effort, permissions gating (separate sibling beads).
- Adding a tokenizer dependency. char/4 estimate is fine for v1.

## Files

- cli/internal/agent/types.go
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go
- cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go (or wherever the bead prompt is assembled)
notes: Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing
title: routing: context-window gating in evaluateCandidate

acceptance:
1. fuzzy('qwen3', ['qwen3.5-27b-4bit', 'qwen3.6-35b-a3b-4bit']) returns 'qwen3.6-35b-a3b-4bit', not qwen3.5.
2. fuzzy('qwen3.5', ['qwen3.5-27b-4bit', 'qwen3.5-claude-distilled']) returns the shortest-suffix candidate within the qwen3.5 group (unchanged behavior within a version group).
3. Non-semver inputs still tiebreak by shortest-suffix: fuzzy('gpt', ['gpt-4o', 'gpt-4-turbo']) returns gpt-4o (shorter).
4. At least 6 table-driven tests in discovery_test.go covering semver + non-semver + mixed cases.
description:
## Problem (filed per opus fresh-eyes review 2026-04-17)

After ddx-0486e601 lands normalization, fuzzy matcher still has an inherited tiebreak quirk:

    Input: 'qwen3'
    Canonicalized pool: ['qwen3.5-27b-4bit', 'qwen3.6-35b-a3b-4bit']
    Current tiebreak: shortest-suffix wins -> picks qwen3.5 (shorter suffix '.5-27b-4bit' < '.6-35b-a3b-4bit')

A user asking for 'qwen3' almost certainly wants the LATEST, not whichever happens to have a shorter concrete suffix. Shortest-suffix is a reasonable default for avoiding ambiguity, but for semver-like suffixes it produces counterintuitive results.

Opus: 'Not a blocker — inherited behavior — but add a test asserting the current (possibly-wrong) resolution and flag it for a follow-up. Making this a conscious policy choice for a future change.'

## Fix

Add a semver-aware tiebreak when multiple candidates share a prefix and the first character after the prefix is a digit (indicating a version suffix). Otherwise fall back to shortest-suffix.

Heuristic:

1. Group candidates by the normalized prefix+first-version-segment (e.g. 'qwen3.5' vs 'qwen3.6').
2. Within each group, pick by shortest-suffix (current rule).
3. Across groups, pick the HIGHEST version by semver-like comparison.

Refuse to over-engineer: no full semver parsing, just 'compare version segments as numbers where possible, fall back to lexicographic'.

Corner cases to test:
- 'qwen3' -> qwen3.6-35b-a3b-4bit (prefer qwen3.6 over qwen3.5)
- 'qwen3.5' -> qwen3.5-27b-4bit (exact prefix match, only one candidate)
- 'gpt-4' -> gpt-4o (if present) vs gpt-4-turbo — semver-free, fall back to shortest-suffix (current behavior)
- 'minimax-m' -> prefer minimax-m2.7 over minimax-m2.5

## Files touched

- cli/internal/agent/discovery.go — new tiebreak in fuzzyMatchInPool
- cli/internal/agent/discovery_test.go — semver + non-semver tiebreak cases

## Depends on

- ddx-0486e601 (normalization + per-provider) — this is the file where tiebreak lives
notes: Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing
title: routing: discovery fuzzy match tiebreak — prefer latest version over shortest-suffix

acceptance: New file cli/internal/agent/script.go implements RunScript(opts) that reads a directive file, executes directives against opts.WorkDir using real filesystem and git operations, and returns a real Result. The harness is registered in cli/internal/agent/registry.go as `script` and marked always-available in Discover(). Directive grammar supports at minimum: append-line, create-file, delete-file, commit, set-exit, no-op, sleep-ms, fail-during. Environment variable interpolation supports ${DDX_BEAD_ID}, ${DDX_ATTEMPT_ID}, ${DDX_WORKER_ID}. Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go cover: (1) append-line + commit produces a real commit with the expected content, (2) no-op produces zero commits and exit 0, (3) create-file + commit produces the file and a commit, (4) set-exit 1 makes the run return exit 1, (5) fail-during 2 executes directives 0 and 1 but errors on directive 2, (6) env var interpolation replaces ${DDX_BEAD_ID} with the value from Correlation, (7) invoking via `ddx agent run --harness=script --model=<file>` works end-to-end with a real temp git repo. go test ./internal/agent -run TestScript completes in under 5 seconds.
description:
The execute-loop / execute-bead / land-coordinator system cannot be tested end-to-end today because every "fake" agent in the codebase returns a canned Result{} without actually writing files or making commits. `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go` is a prompt-response dictionary replayer — it returns canned stdout based on prompt hash but does not touch the filesystem or git. `fakeAgentRunner` and `fakeExecuteBeadGit` in `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go` are in-memory mocks that track expected call counts. Neither produces actual commits that the orchestration layer can rebase, merge, or land.

The consequence: every test that claims to cover concurrency, serialization, or git orchestration uses mocked git and therefore cannot catch merge-race, rebase-conflict, or coordinator-serialization bugs. `TestConcurrentWorkersNoMergeRace` at execute_bead_orchestrator_test.go:228 is particularly misleading — it uses separate orchTestGitOps instances per worker so the shared-writer invariant is never exercised. The test passes because no shared state exists to race on.

## What this bead builds

A new deterministic `script` harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go, mirroring cli/internal/agent/virtual.go):

- Registered alongside claude/codex/agent/virtual in cli/internal/agent/registry.go
- Always available (no binary probe required)
- Takes a directive file (passed via RunOptions.Model or a new RunOptions.ScriptFile field — either works)
- Reads the directive file, executes each line against opts.WorkDir as real filesystem + git operations
- Returns a real Result{ExitCode, Output} that the loop and orchestrator can observe

## Directive grammar

Line-based, comments start with `#`. Each directive performs a real side effect:

```
append-line <path> <text>          # append text to file (create if missing)
create-file <path> <content>       # create or overwrite file
delete-file <path>                 # rm -f path
modify-line <path> <regex> <repl>  # sed-style in-place substitution
run <shell-command>                # exec in WorkDir with clean env
commit <message>                   # git add -A && git commit -m <message>
set-exit <code>                    # final exit code (default 0)
no-op                              # no commits, no files, exit 0
fail-during <index>                # synthetic failure at the Nth directive
sleep-ms <n>                       # deterministic delay (for scheduling tests)
```

Directives run in order. Environment variables DDX_BEAD_ID, DDX_ATTEMPT_ID, DDX_WORKER_ID are interpolated into text arguments so directives can be parameterized. Example:

```
append-line tests/smoke.txt "hello from ${DDX_BEAD_ID}"
commit "smoke: ${DDX_BEAD_ID}"
```

## Invocation

From the CLI: `ddx agent run --harness=script --model=./testdata/append-smoke.txt --prompt=/dev/null`

Programmatically from a test:
```go
runner := agent.NewRunner(agent.Config{Harness: "script"})
result, err := runner.Run(agent.RunOptions{
    WorkDir: wtPath,
    Model:   "testdata/append-smoke.txt",
    Correlation: map[string]string{"bead_id": beadID, "attempt_id": attemptID},
})
```

## Implementation notes

- Mirror the dispatch pattern in cli/internal/agent/runner.go:81 where `virtual` is intercepted
- Always-available in Discover() same as virtual + agent
- No external dependencies beyond what the agent package already uses (os, os/exec for git)
- Estimated size: ~200 lines of implementation + ~200 lines of tests
- Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go must prove each directive actually performs its claimed side effect on a real temp git repo

## What this enables

Every test case in beads B (integration tests) and C (server runWorker regression test) depends on this harness existing. Without it, the land-coordinator redesign ships behind whatever real claude happens to do on the day the test runs.
title: Implement deterministic script harness for orchestration stress testing

acceptance:
1. fuzzyMatchInPool returns the correct match for case + vendor-prefix variants: fuzzy('qwen/qwen3.6', ['Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', 'Qwen3.5-27B-4bit']) -> ('Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', true).

2. Negative case: fuzzy('minimax/minimax-m2.7', ['MiniMax-M2.5-MLX-4bit']) -> ('', false). Version-patch divergence does not force a match.

3. Exact match still takes precedence: fuzzy('Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', ['Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit-v2']) -> ('Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit', false).

4. Shortest-suffix tiebreak is unchanged for already-matching cases.

5. cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/ -run Fuzzy passes, plus at least 5 new table-driven cases covering the normalization paths.
description:
## Problem

The fuzzy matcher at cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:62-108 has two defects that compound:

### Defect 1: strict case-sensitive prefix with no canonical-prefix stripping

fuzzyMatchInPool (discovery.go:76) does literal case-sensitive prefix check:

    for _, m := range pool {
        if len(m) > len(input) && m[:len(input)] == input {
            candidates = append(candidates, candidate{...})
        }
    }

Cannot bridge 'qwen/qwen3.6' (canonical ref) -> 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit' (vidar-omlx native). Case mismatch + slash-prefix mismatch at first byte.

### Defect 2: matcher collapses provider identity

FuzzyMatchModel (discovery.go:62-71) pools d.AllModels() — a deduplicated union across EVERY discovered provider. Callers then do a second lookup via ProvidersForModel(match) to pick a provider, but that lookup is not gated on --provider preference. A model name appearing on any provider can leak routing to that provider even under a --provider pin.

These two defects must be fixed together. Per Opus fresh-eyes review: per-provider-pool without normalization matches even less than the current union pool. Splitting them creates a merge-conflict hazard (same file, same test surface, one coherent change).

## Fix

Update the discovery.go fuzzy-match layer as one coherent change:

### 1. Normalize input and pool entries

Add canonicalization helper:

    func canonicalizeModelKey(s string) string {
        // Strip exactly ONE leading 'vendor/' segment if present (preserve
        // path structure for future multi-segment refs — do NOT strip all
        // slashes blindly).
        if slash := strings.Index(s, '/'); slash > 0 {
            s = s[slash+1:]
        }
        return strings.ToLower(s)
    }

Per codex fresh-eyes review: ONLY the leading vendor namespace is stripped. 'openai/gpt-4' -> 'gpt-4'. 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6' -> 'claude-sonnet-4-6'. 'qwen/qwen3.6' -> 'qwen3.6'. A hypothetical 'a/b/c' becomes 'b/c' (not 'c').

### 2. Case-insensitive prefix on canonicalized forms

Apply prefix matching on canonicalized(input) vs canonicalized(pool entry). Shortest-suffix tiebreak remains. Return the ORIGINAL pool entry (preserve casing) when a match is found.

Negative case: 'minimax/minimax-m2.7' vs 'MiniMax-M2.5-MLX-4bit' normalizes to 'minimax-m2.7' vs 'minimax-m2.5-mlx-4bit'. Prefix check: 'minimax-m2.5-mlx-4bit' does NOT start with 'minimax-m2.7' (divergence at '.5' vs '.7'). Returns no match. CORRECT — m2.7 is not m2.5.

### 3. Per-provider variant

Add DiscoveryResult.FuzzyMatchModelOnProvider(input, providerName) that pools ONLY the named provider's models. Exact-match + fuzzy-match semantics identical to the global version, running against the narrower pool.

    func (d *DiscoveryResult) FuzzyMatchModelOnProvider(input, providerName string) (model, provider string, fuzzy bool)

Returns (resolvedModelID, providerName, isFuzzy) tuple. Provider identity is preserved so downstream scoring doesn't need a second lookup.

### 4. Callers

Keep the existing AllModels() path (useful for unpinned routing). Callers with a provider pin should use the per-provider variant first and fall back to global discovery only if the pinned provider has no match (SD-015 'soft preference' semantics: try pinned first, then broader).

### 5. Exact-match precedence

Preserve exact-match precedence (both case-sensitive and case-insensitive). Exact > fuzzy.

### 6. Known tiebreak quirk (track, don't fix here)

Per Opus review: 'qwen3' matches both 'qwen3.5-27b-4bit' and 'qwen3.6-35b-a3b-4bit'; shortest-suffix picks qwen3.5. A user asking for 'qwen3' probably wants the latest. This is inherited behavior from the existing tiebreak rule and is out of scope for THIS bead — add a follow-up bead and a test documenting the current (possibly-wrong) resolution so a future change is a conscious policy choice.

## Files likely touched

- cli/internal/agent/discovery.go — canonicalizeModelKey, updated fuzzyMatchInPool, new FuzzyMatchModelOnProvider
- cli/internal/agent/discovery_test.go — new cases: vendor-prefix stripping, case folding, exact-match precedence, ambiguity tiebreak, negative (version divergence), per-provider pool match, pinned-provider fallback behavior
- New follow-up bead tracking 'prefer latest version on ambiguous tiebreak' (file in the same commit so the link is durable)

## Depends on

None; self-contained discovery-layer change. Unblocks ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/profile discovery integration) and ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier-affinity) once ddx-8610020e (Provider field) is also in place.

acceptance: dxx-agent has dedicated ls, find, and grep tools; stall detector classifies them as read-only; bash is only classified as write-capable; agents on OpenRouter models transition from reading to writing faster
description:
During execute-loop runs with OpenRouter models (GLM-5.1, Qwen 3.6), agents get stuck in read loops — they call read and bash (for ls/find/grep) repeatedly without ever writing. The stall detector cannot distinguish bash ls from bash "git commit" because bash is classified as a write-capable tool, resetting the stall counter on every call.

Adding dedicated read-only tools (ls, find, grep) to ddx-agent would:
1. Let the stall detector accurately distinguish read-only exploration from write activity
2. Reduce token usage by avoiding bash prompt/response overhead for simple file queries
3. Give models clearer affordances — read-only tools for exploration, write/edit/bash for modification
4. Help models transition from reading to writing faster by making the tool boundary explicit

With these tools, the stall detector classifies read/ls/find/grep as read-only (increment stall counter) and write/edit/bash as write-capable (reset counter). Bash is only used when the agent needs commands that might modify the filesystem.
notes: Stall detection exists. Gap: no dedicated ls/find/grep tools registered in agent_runner.go tools slice (only Read/Write/Edit/Bash). Add as distinct agentlib.Tool implementations.
title: Add dedicated read-only tools (ls, find, grep) to ddx-agent for stall detection and efficiency

acceptance:
**User story:** As an operator, clicking Drain queue on a project home page starts a worker that I can immediately see in that project's Workers view, with its state and current bead updating live.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Root-cause fix.** The `workersByProject(projectID:)` resolver returns all workers whose target project matches the requested id. Both Option 1 (id→path resolution) and Option 2 (store project id on record) satisfy this; implementation chooses one with a one-line justification in the PR description.

2. **Regression test — project-scoped query.**
   - Seed a ServerState with two registered projects (different paths and ids).
   - Start one worker for each project.
   - `workersByProject(projectID: <id-1>)` returns exactly worker 1.
   - `workersByProject(projectID: <id-2>)` returns exactly worker 2.
   - `workers` (global) returns both.
   - Unknown project id returns empty list, not an error.

3. **Regression test — backward compat.**
   - A worker record on disk without a `ProjectID` field (simulating a pre-migration record) is correctly matched by the path-resolution fallback. Only required if Option 2 is chosen.

4. **End-to-end validation.**
   - Playwright: navigate to project home, click Drain, confirm, wait 2s, navigate to Workers tab. Assert exactly one new row with `state: running` and matching worker id.
   - Same test run against a second project asserts that project's Workers tab does not show project-1's worker.

5. **No regression to unrelated surfaces.**
   - Global `workers` query still returns all workers.
   - Worker detail page still loads by worker id.
   - Workers live-progress subscription still patches rows in place.

6. **Cross-reference.**
   - Note on `ddx-69789664` (Workers lifecycle controls): that bead's acceptance assumed Workers pane worked correctly. Point to this bead as the prerequisite for those tests to be meaningful.
description:
## Observed

User clicks "Drain queue" on the project home page. Mutation returns success with a worker id. Navigating to `/nodes/.../projects/<id>/workers` shows no workers — an empty table.

Verified on disk: the worker record exists and is running.

```
$ cat .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T205846-f23f/status.json
{
  "id": "worker-20260422T205846-f23f",
  "kind": "execute-loop",
  "state": "running",
  "project_root": "/Users/erik/Projects/ddx",
  ...
}
```

So the dispatch worked, but the per-project workers list filters it out.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:156` in `GetWorkersGraphQL`:

```go
if projectID != "" && rec.ProjectRoot != projectID {
    continue
}
```

- `projectID` is the GraphQL argument — a project **id** like `proj-96d7ea83`.
- `rec.ProjectRoot` is the worker record field — a project **path** like `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx`.

These are different representations and never match. With any non-empty `projectID`, every worker is filtered out.

The global `workers` resolver (`resolver_agent.go:9`) passes `""` and works correctly. Only the per-project path (`workersByProject` → `GetWorkersGraphQL(projectID)`) is broken. The user's flow (dispatch from project home → navigate to project workers) hits exactly this path.

Note: `GetBeadSnapshots` (state_graphql.go:49-105) handles the same concept correctly by iterating `s.Projects` and filtering on `proj.ID != projectID` — comparing id to id. The worker resolver does not use that pattern.

## Proposed direction

Two options; prefer (1) for minimal change, (2) for long-term clarity.

### Option 1 — Resolve id → path at the filter site

```go
expectedPath := ""
if projectID != "" {
    proj, ok := s.GetProjectByID(projectID)
    if !ok {
        return nil // unknown project id → no workers
    }
    expectedPath = proj.Path
}
...
if expectedPath != "" && rec.ProjectRoot != expectedPath {
    continue
}
```

### Option 2 — Store project id on the worker record

Add `ProjectID string` to `WorkerRecord`. Populate at creation time in `WorkerManager.StartExecuteLoop` via `s.GetProjectByPath(effectiveRoot)`. Filter on `rec.ProjectID != projectID`. Cleaner — the worker record carries its own identity and the query doesn't depend on runtime resolution. Requires a tiny migration (for older records missing the field, fall back to path-resolve as in Option 1).

Recommend Option 2. It removes the representation mismatch for good and is cheap.

## Scope note

This is a one-line functional bug with outsized impact: it's the reason the Workers page looks broken whenever used as the dashboard intends. Prioritizing P1.

## Out of scope

- Workers pane lifecycle controls (`ddx-69789664`).
- Cross-project workers view — the global `workers` query already handles that.

      * workersByProject(id=A) returns only worker A

acceptance: 1) TestExecuteBeadAgentErrorWithCommitsPreserves no longer references fakeExecuteBeadGit or fakeAgentRunner. 2) Test dispatches through RealLandingGitOps + newScriptHarnessRepo (or equivalent real-git harness). 3) cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadAgentErrorWithCommitsPreserves$' -count=1 green. 4) No other test in agent_execute_bead_test.go is modified.
description:
Migrate cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go TestExecuteBeadAgentErrorWithCommitsPreserves() from fakeExecuteBeadGit + fakeAgentRunner to real git + script harness.

Pattern:
- Use cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go newScriptHarnessRepo(t, beadCount) to build an isolated repo with real git.
- Use runGitInteg (from cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go) for any supplemental git invocations, so GIT_* env is scrubbed.
- Replace fakeExecuteBeadGit with the RealLandingGitOps flow used in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land_test.go.
- Replace fakeAgentRunner with the script harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go) driven by a per-attempt directive file. When the test needs to simulate 'agent emits no commits' or 'agent emits a dirty worktree', encode that as script directives rather than a canned Result struct.
- Preserve every assertion — the point is that the test now exercises real git + real merge/preserve semantics, not that the surface shrinks.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadAgentErrorWithCommitsPreserves$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.

acceptance: File exists at skills/ddx/reference/beads.md. Contains sections covering: inline context requirement, explicit file scope (in + out), small-bead rule of thumb, command-based acceptance criteria, tests-included requirement, rollback expectations, spec-id guidance, CLI reference. No occurrences of 'phase:(frame|design|test|build|deploy|iterate|review)' in body. No mandatory 'helix' label requirement (grep -E 'helix.*(required|must|required)' returns no matches). Linked from skills/ddx/SKILL.md intent-router.
description:
Create skills/ddx/reference/beads.md — the domain reference file a harness reads when the user asks to create, plan, or update beads.

Content required:
- **Inline context, not links.** Every bead description must be readable cold by an automated execute-bead worker that has no chat history and no open tabs.
- **Explicit in-scope file list.** Repo-relative paths. Bad: 'the auth module'. Good: 'cli/internal/auth/pipeline.go and cli/internal/auth/pipeline_test.go'.
- **Explicit do-NOT-touch scope negation** (non-goals). Prevents scope creep during agent execution.
- **Favor many small beads.** Rule of thumb: if a bead would touch more than ~3 files or more than one subsystem, decompose into child beads under an epic.
- **Acceptance is a command that passes, not a sentence.** 'X works' is not AC. 'cd cli && go test ./internal/foo/... passes and returns 0' is AC.
- **Implementations include tests.** The acceptance criteria must reference a test command.
- **Rollback/cleanup expectations** for partial changes — what 'leave it unclaimed' means in practice.
- **spec-id linkage** when a governing artifact exists (FEAT-*, SD-*, TD-*, ADR-*); omit when it doesn't.
- **CLI reference** at the end: ddx bead create/update/close/list/show/ready/blocked/dep/import/export/evidence.

Source material: existing /skills/ddx-bead/SKILL.md (already contains much of this — especially the top-level "Execution-Ready" section added in an earlier refinement). Strip HELIX-specific rules: remove the mandatory 'helix' label and the phase:frame|design|test|build|deploy|iterate|review enumeration. Keep generic guidance ('label beads with area, kind, and any workflow tags your project uses').

In-scope files:
- skills/ddx/reference/beads.md (new)

Out-of-scope:
- skills/ddx/SKILL.md (separate bead ddx-bd852e86)
- skills/ddx/reference/{work,review,agents,status}.md (separate beads)
title: Create skills/ddx/reference/beads.md — execution-ready bead authoring

acceptance: make build in cli/ succeeds after running make copy-skills (manually or as a prerequisite). ls cli/internal/skills/ shows only embed.go, validate.go, skills_test.go, and ddx/ (no ddx-bead, ddx-run, etc.). diff -r ../skills/ddx/ ./internal/skills/ddx/ reports no differences after make copy-skills. A built ddx binary, when run, embeds the single ddx skill (not 7 separate ones). lefthook.yml has a pre-commit hook wired for copy-skills.
description:
Go embed patterns cannot traverse upward out of the package directory. //go:embed all:../../skills/ddx is illegal and will fail at go build. Commit to copy-at-build approach.

Steps:
1. Add Makefile target copy-skills in cli/Makefile that rsyncs skills/ddx/ into cli/internal/skills/ddx/ before every build. Example:
     copy-skills:
         rsync -a --delete ../skills/ddx/ ./internal/skills/ddx/
2. Wire copy-skills as a prerequisite of make build, make test, make dev (and any other targets that invoke go build or go test).
3. Update cli/internal/skills/embed.go to replace the current multi-dir embed line with: //go:embed all:ddx
4. Delete cli/internal/skills/ddx-{bead,agent,doctor,install,status,review,run}/ — the new embed picks up the copied ddx/ tree instead. (Deletion of top-level /skills/ddx-* is Phase 2; this bead only touches cli/internal/skills/.)
5. Commit cli/internal/skills/ddx/ as a tracked copy so contributors who run go build directly (without make) still get a valid binary. Add a pre-commit hook (lefthook.yml) that re-runs make copy-skills if skills/ddx/ is dirty and cli/internal/skills/ddx/ is stale; this keeps the copy in sync without manual intervention.

In-scope files:
- cli/Makefile
- cli/internal/skills/embed.go
- cli/internal/skills/ddx/ (committed copy — gets populated by copy-skills)
- lefthook.yml (add pre-commit hook)

Out-of-scope:
- skills/ddx/SKILL.md and reference files — separate beads
- ddx init / ddx update changes — separate beads
- Old /skills/ddx-* (top-level) deletion — Phase 2

Prerequisite: skills/ddx/SKILL.md + reference/*.md exist before make copy-skills runs, else the rsync copies an empty dir.
title: Wire Makefile copy-skills target and update embed.go for single ddx skill

acceptance: (1) `ddx agent providers` prints a table matching `ddx-agent providers` output (same providers, same statuses). (2) `ddx agent providers --json` returns valid JSON that a script can parse. (3) Implementation uses Go package imports from github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent/config (and related packages) — grep the new file for ReadLine / exec.Command / os/exec returns nothing. (4) `go build ./...` passes. (5) Manual: run the command against the live config (including vidar, grendel, bragi, openrouter, vidar-coder) and verify the output matches `ddx-agent providers` from the v0.3.5 binary.
description:
Add a new DDx CLI subcommand `ddx agent providers [--json]` that surfaces the live provider list with status. Implementation: use github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent/config's public APIs (agentconfig.Load, cfg.Providers, cfg.ProviderNames, cfg.DefaultName) and the provider package's live-probe helpers. Output format matches ddx-agent's `cmdProviders` exactly — NAME/TYPE/URL/MODEL/STATUS columns for text mode, structured JSON array for --json. Implementation lives in cli/cmd/agent_providers.go (new file) and registers with the existing `ddx agent` command group in cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go.

If ddx-agent's public API does NOT expose a clean "list providers with live status" helper (the internal probeProviderModels and related functions are not exported as of v0.3.5), file an upstream bead on github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent requesting a public `config.ListProviderStatus(ctx, cfg) ([]ProviderStatus, error)` (or equivalent) function before implementing the DDx wrapper. Do NOT shell out to the ddx-agent binary. Do NOT copy-paste probing logic from ddx-agent's cmd/ package into ddx.

acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer reviewing how different harness/provider/model combinations are performing, I expect the Efficacy page to load quickly on a workspace with tens of thousands of sessions, and I expect the rows to reflect every agent invocation — not just those tied to closed beads.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Source change.** `EfficacyRows` resolver reads from the sharded sessions index (`.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, per `ddx-2ceb02fa`). Bead-event read paths (`efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot`'s event loop, `attemptsFromEvidence`, `efficacyMemo`) are removed in the same PR — no dead code retained.

2. **Schema parity.** Row shape and field names stay as they are today (rowKey, harness, provider, model, attempts, successes, successRate, medianInputTokens, medianOutputTokens, medianDurationMs, medianCostUsd, warning). UI does not need to change. Optional new filters (`since`, `until`, `projectId`) are added as nullable args with sensible defaults.

3. **Fixture + baseline recorded.** Fixture seeds ≥10,000 session index rows across 2+ monthly shards with ≥10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups. Baseline latencies (before any optimization beyond source change) written to bead notes with hardware + go-version footer.

4. **First-pass perf targets.**
   - All-time rollup on 10k-row, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
   - Last-30-days rollup on same fixture: p95 ≤ 60ms / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
   - Stretch fixture (50k rows × 24 shards): all-time p95 ≤ 400ms in-process / ≤ 1000ms HTTP, or **user is consulted** before choosing a new substrate.

5. **DB-decision protocol (per user directive 2026-04-22).**
   - If targets in (4) cannot be met with in-place optimization (streaming aggregation, per-shard memoization with correct invalidation, selective field read), the PR author posts a short proposal in the bead notes: measured achievable numbers, proposed substrate from {axon, sqlite, postgres}, and migration cost. Implementation of that substrate requires explicit user approval before landing.

6. **Completeness test.** A fixture seeds sessions that would not appear under the old bead-event path (open-bead sessions, `ddx agent run` sessions, benchmark sessions). The new resolver returns rows for each of them. A matching integration test on the old code path (kept as a golden-output comparison for one cycle) asserts the new output is a strict superset in the cases it should be.

7. **Freshness.** A session written via the sessions index writer (from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) is visible in the Efficacy query within 2s. Integration test writes a row and queries the aggregation.

8. **Playwright smoke.** `/efficacy` opens, renders the rollup table, and clicks into `EfficacyAttempts` for one row — all within normal navigation budgets. Uses the 10k-row fixture; asserts the table has ≥5 rows rendered.

9. **Cross-reference.** This bead is a dependent of `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index must exist). If `ddx-2ceb02fa` is not yet landed when this work starts, bead notes record the stub / interim source chosen and the timeline for switching over.
description:
## Observed

The Efficacy page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../efficacy` loads very slowly. User feedback: efficacy should really be "an aggregation of sessions grouped by provider / harness / model" — which is not what the current implementation does.

## Ground truth (current implementation)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:676-833` —

- `EfficacyRows` builds its rollup from **bead evidence events** (`kind:routing` and `kind:cost` on every closed bead), not from sessions.
- Computation path: `store.ReadAll()` → for every closed bead, read its event file → filter for routing/cost → per-(harness, provider, model) aggregate.
- There is a cache (`efficacyMemo`), keyed by a fingerprint that itself walks every event on every closed bead to compute event count + max timestamp. So cache validation costs almost as much as cache rebuild.
- Cost shape: `O(closed_beads × events_per_bead)` on every request (cache miss) and even on cache hit the fingerprint walk happens.

## Why this is wrong (per user feedback)

- Efficacy is a sessions-view concept: "how is harness/provider/model X doing?" Sessions are the natural row. Bead events are a lossy, indirect proxy — they only exist for *closed* beads, they duplicate session state, and they force coupling between the efficacy feature and bead lifecycle.
- The bead-event path misses: open beads with in-flight sessions, sessions that didn't close a bead, benchmark/quorum sessions, non-execute-bead invocations (e.g., `ddx agent run`).
- The sessions feed is already being reworked into a sharded pointer index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). Efficacy should read from that index — single source of truth.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Reshape the data source

- Rebuild `EfficacyRows` as an aggregation over `sessions.index` (the sharded index from `ddx-2ceb02fa`), not over bead events.
- Grouping key: `(harness, provider, model)` — optionally filterable by project, bead, label, time range.
- Metrics per row: attempts (count), successes (count + rate), median input/output tokens, median duration, median cost. Identical shape to today; only the source changes.
- Shard awareness: time-range queries map to a shard set (same pattern as `ddx-2ceb02fa`); the default view reads current + previous shard.

### Part 2 — Fixture + baseline (honor user's 1,000+ record floor)

- Perf harness target: aggregate ≥**1,000 session rows** (≥ 10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups) within the budget below.
- Stretch fixture: 50,000 session rows across 24 monthly shards. Measures steady-state behavior as the index grows.
- Baseline captured in the bead notes before any optimization.

### Part 3 — Perf targets (first pass, in-place)

- Default Efficacy view (all-time) on a 10k-session, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
- Date-filtered view (last 30 days, 1 shard): p95 ≤ 60ms in-process / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
- If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, memoize-per-shard, streaming aggregation) **cannot hit these numbers** on the 50k stretch fixture, **do not silently change substrate** — raise the decision with the user. Candidate backends named by the user: axon, sqlite, postgres. Bring a recommendation + rationale, not a fait accompli.

### Part 4 — Drop the bead-event read path

- After the rewrite, remove `efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot` event iteration, `attemptsFromEvidence`, and `efficacyMemo`. They become dead weight once the source moves.
- Evidence events on beads remain (they're bead audit trail, not efficacy data); nothing in bead lifecycle changes.

## Out of scope

- The `comparisons` / "compare arms" feature on this page (tracked separately — see companion bead).
- Project-scope filtering UI (the backend support can be added now; the UI widget is a follow-up).
- Cross-machine / federated efficacy rollups.

acceptance:
1. Written incident report (.md or bead comment body) captures at least one observed hang with: process state, timeline, tracker state, and root-cause classification.

2. Follow-up bead(s) filed for each identified root cause. Each with deterministic AC (e.g. 'add 10m wall-clock timeout on bash tool subprocess execution').

3. This bead closes as 'investigation complete; see child beads for fixes'. ddx-b808df39 references this bead's findings in its implementation notes.
description:
## Context

ddx-b808df39 (P0: ddx agent stop/kill command for hung workers) treats hung-worker remediation as an operational need, but does NOT investigate the UNDERLYING cause of the hangs. The feature is a useful escape hatch, but shipping it without understanding WHY workers hang means we'll keep needing the escape hatch.

Opus fresh-eyes review (2026-04-18) flagged: 'Hung workers for 33h–142h are an operational defect independent of root cause' — the feature bead is justified, but a SIBLING root-cause investigation is missing from the portfolio.

## Observed

The referenced 33h and 142h hang durations in ddx-b808df39 imply multiple incidents where an execute-bead worker process remained in-progress past any reasonable upper bound for a single bead. Needs to investigate:

1. Is the worker process alive but stuck in a tool call (bash subprocess that never returns)?
2. Is it alive but mid-LLM-call with no timeout (provider hang + no client-side deadline)?
3. Is it dead but the bead's claimed_at never cleared (tracker-side leak)?
4. Is the worktree locked or fsync'd in a way that prevents cleanup?

## Fix direction

This bead is an INVESTIGATION, not a fix. Deliverable is a short report that:

1. Reproduces or observes at least one hang incident and captures:
   - ps / pgrep state of the worker process tree
   - stack traces via 'go tool pprof' against the worker (if alive)
   - Timeline from .ddx/executions/<attempt>/embedded/*.jsonl (last event type + timestamp)
   - tracker claimed_at and retry_after state

2. Classifies the hang into one of the four categories above (or a new category).

3. Files concrete follow-up beads for each identified root cause:
   - Tool subprocess timeout if bash hangs
   - LLM client deadline if provider hangs
   - Claim expiry / heartbeat if tracker-side leak
   - File-lock / worktree cleanup if fs-side

4. Recommends whether ddx-b808df39's stop/kill mechanism is sufficient as mitigation or needs additional primitives.

## Relation to ddx-b808df39

ddx-b808df39 ships the OPERATOR TOOL to stop a hung worker. This bead finds the BUG that causes hangs. Both are needed; ship ddx-b808df39 first if the operator-tool is more urgent, then this investigation.

## Non-goals

- Fixing the root cause in this bead. Scope is investigation + follow-up bead filing.
- Preventing all possible hangs (impossible). Scope is the actually-observed hang modes.
