@@ -0,0 +1,193 @@
+# Investigation: root cause of 33–142h hung execute-bead workers
+
+**Bead:** ddx-0a651925 (investigates; sibling to ddx-b808df39)
+**Attempt:** 20260418T043148-1346e8a3
+**Scope:** Source-code + execution-bundle analysis. No live hung worker was available
+in this isolated worktree to capture live `ps`/`pprof`, so the incident evidence is
+drawn from `.ddx/executions/*` bundles and the worker/runner source path.
+
+---
+
+## 1. Observed evidence (from `.ddx/executions/`)
+
+| Metric                                               | Count / value                                        |
+| ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
+| Total attempt bundles                                | 328                                                  |
+| Bundles with `result.json`                           | 291 (89%)                                            |
+| Bundles with `manifest.json` but **no** `result.json` | 37 (11%) — executions whose worker never finalized  |
+
+Top five longest *finalized* attempts:
+
+| `duration_ms` | Wall time    | `detail`                                                                    | Bundle                          |
+| ------------- | ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
+| 7,720,908     | **2h 8m**    | `agent: compaction stuck: 50 consecutive failed compaction attempts`        | 20260416T230316-efbf91c5        |
+| 7,200,098     | **2h 0m**    | `timeout after 2h0m0s` (claude streaming wall-clock fired)                  | 20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba        |
+| 6,764,927     | **1h 53m**   | success (agent harness, qwen)                                               | 20260416T230315-f9f46822        |
+| 5,649,987     | **1h 34m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260416T230313-e193942b        |
+| 4,284,347     | **1h 11m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260415T103422-bb3a45fa        |
+
+**Key observation:** the 33h / 142h hang durations cited in ddx-b808df39 are
+~15× and ~65× longer than the longest *finalized* attempt on record. This means
+the hung workers never reached the point of writing `result.json` — they were
+blocked inside `runner.Run` (or deeper) for days. Every code path that
+*should* have terminated them (idle timeout, compaction-stuck, stall detector,
+cancel propagation) failed to fire.
+
+The 37 manifest-only bundles are candidates for hung or externally-killed
+workers; e.g. `20260416T215953-6b515c05` has a manifest from 2026-04-16T21:59Z
+with no result. Those fit the profile of "workers running 33h–142h with no bead
+assigned" reported on agent projects.
+
+---
+
+## 2. Root-cause classification (source code evidence)
+
+The investigation checked the four hypotheses from the bead description.
+
+### RC1 (PRIMARY) — Caller context is discarded inside the Runner
+
+`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (`Runner.Run`):
+```go
+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
+```
+
+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (`Runner.RunAgent`):
+```go
+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
+```
+
+`RunOptions` (types.go) takes no `context.Context` at all. `agent.ExecuteBead`
+likewise does not accept ctx, and `server.WorkerManager.runWorker` passes its
+own ctx into `worker.Run(ctx, …)` but that ctx **never reaches the agent
+invocation**.
+
+**Impact:** `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (workers.go:648) calls `handle.cancel()`
+which cancels only the worker goroutine's ctx. The running agent provider call
+/ subprocess is detached. The only way to interrupt a stuck agent today is a
+SIGKILL from outside the process. This is the structural defect that enables
+33–142h hangs — once the agent is stuck, nothing inside DDx can stop it.
+
+### RC2 — Idle-reset timer, not wall-clock deadline
+
+`cli/internal/agent/executor.go:35-40` creates an *idle timeout* context value:
+```go
+func withExecutionTimeout(ctx context.Context, timeout time.Duration) context.Context {
+    return context.WithValue(ctx, executionTimeoutKey{}, timeout)
+}
+```
+
+`executor.go:154-178` consumes it and **resets the timer on every stdout/stderr
+byte**. `agent_runner.go:264-285` implements the same pattern against agentlib
+events.
+
+The default timeout is 2h (`types.go:363 DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000`). The
+comment claims "2 hours — long enough for any task; supervisor/agent decides
+termination" but the actual behaviour is **"2 hours of silence required to fire"**.
+A provider that emits a heartbeat, a ping, a retry line, or any byte resets the
+timer indefinitely.
+
+The `runClaudeStreaming` path (`claude_stream.go:373-384`) is the only path that
+uses a wall-clock deadline — and it is the only path we see actually hitting
+the timeout (bundle 20260415T015231 exactly 2h 0m 0.098s).
+
+**Impact:** for `harness=agent` (qwen, minimax, openrouter, etc.), no wall-clock
+bound exists. 33–142h hangs are consistent with a provider that dribbles events
+slowly enough to defeat the idle reset.
+
+### RC3 — Compaction-stuck breaker fires late
+
+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:218-241` increments a counter on each no-op
+compaction event and trips at `stuckThreshold = maxConsecutiveCompactionFailures`
+(50 by default). On observed `efbf91c5` it took **2h 8m** to trip. If one
+iteration takes 3 minutes to produce a no-op compaction event, 50 events =
+~150 min — matches the observed tail.
+
+**Impact:** on its own, the breaker is too slow to be a useful hang-guard. It
+should be replaced or complemented by a wall-clock-bounded variant.
+
+### RC4 — Provider HTTP requests likely lack a per-request deadline
+
+`RunAgent` passes the internal `context.Background()`-derived ctx into
+`agentlib.Run(ctx, req)`. The agent library's providers (`openai.Client`,
+anthropic client) do not appear to install a per-request deadline themselves
+(grepping the v0.3.14 module cache shows short timeouts on discovery/probe
+only; the main call path has no WithTimeout). A stalled TCP socket after
+headers are received but before the response body arrives can hang a streaming
+read indefinitely.
+
+**Impact:** consistent with "alive but mid-LLM-call with no client-side
+deadline" — hypothesis (2) in the bead description. Cannot be fully confirmed
+from source alone; needs runtime `go tool pprof http://localhost:<port>/debug/pprof/goroutine`
+against a live hung worker.
+
+### RC-skipped — BashTool subprocess hang (hypothesis 1)
+
+Not the root cause. `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14/tool/bash.go:15`
+installs a 120s default wall-clock timeout (`context.WithTimeout(ctx, timeout)`)
+and calls `exec.CommandContext`. Per-call bash hangs self-terminate at 2min.
+
+### RC-skipped — Tracker-side claim leak (hypothesis 3)
+
+Tracker state is incidental to the hang: the bead's own description notes the
+hung workers exist as processes (`ps aux | grep claude`). The worker process is
+alive; the claim is alive because the worker is alive. Fixing the claim alone
+does not kill the worker. This hypothesis is downstream, not upstream.
+
+### RC-skipped — Worktree file-lock (hypothesis 4)
+
+No evidence in bundles. Execute-bead worktrees are plain git worktrees at
+`/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/*`; no process-held file locks would survive across 33–142h
+without the owning process. If the process is alive, the lock is alive — same
+as RC3.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Relationship to ddx-b808df39
+
+ddx-b808df39 proposes an operator `ddx agent workers stop <id>` command that
+sends **SIGTERM at the OS level**, then SIGKILL after a grace period. That
+design **does** work around RC1 (process-level signal bypasses the broken ctx
+plumbing). So ddx-b808df39 is a *correct mitigation* — but only a mitigation.
+
+Without fixing RC1 (and at least RC2), operators will keep needing to invoke
+`workers stop` manually. The autonomous remediations every other system relies
+on (cancel ctx, idle timeout, circuit breaker) are each broken today in ways
+that require explicit code changes, not just a new operator tool.
+
+**Recommendation:** Ship ddx-b808df39 first (quick operational relief), then
+resolve the child beads filed below in order RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3.
+
+---
+
+## 4. Follow-up beads filed
+
+Each addresses one root cause with a deterministic acceptance test. Priority
+and ID are shown; full details available via `ddx bead show <id>`.
+
+The IDs below carry an `.execute-bead-wt-…` prefix because `ddx bead create`
+was invoked from inside the isolated execute-bead worktree — this is a
+separate DDx bug worth noting but orthogonal to the hang investigation. They
+are parented to `ddx-0a651925` and resolvable by `ddx bead list --parent ddx-0a651925`.
+
+| RC  | Priority | Bead ID (suffix)                                                           | Title                                                              |
+| --- | -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
+| RC1 | P0       | `…-526efaf1` | propagate caller context through Runner.Run and RunAgent           |
+| RC2 | P0       | `…-b203c889` | add wall-clock deadline alongside idle timeout for agent harness   |
+| RC3 | P2       | `…-b0562380` | bound compaction-stuck breaker by wall-clock time                  |
+| RC4 | P1       | `…-3e3913a0` | audit and enforce per-request HTTP deadlines on agent provider calls |
+| —   | P1       | `…-34d039b7` | autonomous worker watchdog with process-level reaper (complements ddx-b808df39) |
+
+**Suggested execution order:** RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3 → watchdog. RC1 is a
+prerequisite for RC4 being meaningful.
+
+---
+
+## 5. What this investigation could NOT do
+
+- Capture live `ps`/`pprof` from a hung worker — none was accessible from the
+  isolated execute-bead worktree. RC4 in particular needs a live `go tool pprof`
+  goroutine dump to confirm that the stuck goroutine is in `net/http` streaming
+  read, not some other path.
+- Confirm whether the agent library's providers install any timeout that was
+  missed in grep. The listed RC4 bead should start with a provider-path audit
+  rather than an immediate fix.","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"7c4457b5-7817-4bd4-9850-94282c2cfdb7","uuid":"b9b08566-e074-49f2-a31f-e547a318b057","timestamp":"2026-04-18T04:51:23.303Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"commit 13cd213e157cd211c48f51796bd62a2fb8b1fcae

+Not the root cause. `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14/tool/bash.go:15`
+installs a 120s default wall-clock timeout (`context.WithTimeout(ctx, timeout)`)
+and calls `exec.CommandContext`. Per-call bash hangs self-terminate at 2min.
+
+### RC-skipped — Tracker-side claim leak (hypothesis 3)
+
+Tracker state is incidental to the hang: the bead's own description notes the
+hung workers exist as processes (`ps aux | grep claude`). The worker process is
+alive; the claim is alive because the worker is alive. Fixing the claim alone
+does not kill the worker. This hypothesis is downstream, not upstream.
+
+### RC-skipped — Worktree file-lock (hypothesis 4)
+
+No evidence in bundles. Execute-bead worktrees are plain git worktrees at
+`/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/*`; no process-held file locks would survive across 33–142h
+without the owning process. If the process is alive, the lock is alive — same
+as RC3.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Relationship to ddx-b808df39
+
+ddx-b808df39 proposes an operator `ddx agent workers stop <id>` command that
+sends **SIGTERM at the OS level**, then SIGKILL after a grace period. That
+design **does** work around RC1 (process-level signal bypasses the broken ctx
+plumbing). So ddx-b808df39 is a *correct mitigation* — but only a mitigation.
+
+Without fixing RC1 (and at least RC2), operators will keep needing to invoke
+`workers stop` manually. The autonomous remediations every other system relies
+on (cancel ctx, idle timeout, circuit breaker) are each broken today in ways
+that require explicit code changes, not just a new operator tool.
+
+**Recommendation:** Ship ddx-b808df39 first (quick operational relief), then
+resolve the child beads filed below in order RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3.
+
+---
+
+## 4. Follow-up beads filed
+
+Each addresses one root cause with a deterministic acceptance test. Priority
+and ID are shown; full details available via `ddx bead show <id>`.
+
+The IDs below carry an `.execute-bead-wt-…` prefix because `ddx bead create`
+was invoked from inside the isolated execute-bead worktree — this is a
+separate DDx bug worth noting but orthogonal to the hang investigation. They
+are parented to `ddx-0a651925` and resolvable by `ddx bead list --parent ddx-0a651925`.
+
+| RC  | Priority | Bead ID (suffix)                                                           | Title                                                              |
+| --- | -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
+| RC1 | P0       | `…-526efaf1` | propagate caller context through Runner.Run and RunAgent           |
+| RC2 | P0       | `…-b203c889` | add wall-clock deadline alongside idle timeout for agent harness   |
+| RC3 | P2       | `…-b0562380` | bound compaction-stuck breaker by wall-clock time                  |
+| RC4 | P1       | `…-3e3913a0` | audit and enforce per-request HTTP deadlines on agent provider calls |
+| —   | P1       | `…-34d039b7` | autonomous worker watchdog with process-level reaper (complements ddx-b808df39) |
+
+**Suggested execution order:** RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3 → watchdog. RC1 is a
+prerequisite for RC4 being meaningful.
+
+---
+
+## 5. What this investigation could NOT do
+
+- Capture live `ps`/`pprof` from a hung worker — none was accessible from the
+  isolated execute-bead worktree. RC4 in particular needs a live `go tool pprof`
+  goroutine dump to confirm that the stuck goroutine is in `net/http` streaming
+  read, not some other path.
+- Confirm whether the agent library's providers install any timeout that was
+  missed in grep. The listed RC4 bead should start with a provider-path audit
+  rather than an immediate fix.","stderr":"","interrupted":false,"isImage":false,"noOutputExpected":false}}

notes:
Investigation complete (attempt 20260418T043148-1346e8a3). Report at .ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md.

Root-cause summary:
- RC1 (PRIMARY): Runner.Run and Runner.RunAgent discard caller ctx (runner.go:239, agent_runner.go:163). WorkerManager.Stop cannot interrupt a running agent.
- RC2: idle-reset timer instead of wall-clock deadline for non-claude paths (executor.go:154, agent_runner.go:264).
- RC3: compaction-stuck breaker fires too late (~2h observed).
- RC4: provider HTTP calls likely lack per-request deadlines (confirm against live hung worker).
- BashTool subprocess hang ruled out (120s default timeout). Tracker-side / fs-lock hypotheses are downstream of RC1.

Follow-up beads filed (parent=ddx-0a651925):
- …-526efaf1 P0 [RC1] propagate caller context
- …-b203c889 P0 [RC2] wall-clock deadline
- …-3e3913a0 P1 [RC4] per-request HTTP deadlines
- …-b0562380 P2 [RC3] bound compaction-stuck breaker by wall-clock
- …-34d039b7 P1 autonomous worker watchdog (complements ddx-b808df39)

ddx-b808df39's operator stop/kill is a correct mitigation but NOT sufficient as sole fix — until RC1 lands, operators will keep needing it. Ship ddx-b808df39 first, then RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3 → watchdog.

Find child beads: ddx bead list --parent ddx-0a651925

description:
'ddx agent execute-loop' help says 'execute-loop scans the current project's execution-ready bead queue.' When submitting to a multi-project server, there is no way to say 'run this loop against project X'. The server picks whichever project is the current server context, ignoring the caller's CWD.

## Observed

From CWD /Users/erik/Projects/axon:

  $ ddx agent execute-loop --once --harness claude --model claude-sonnet-4-6 --json
  {
    "project_root": "/Users/erik/Projects/ddx",  # WRONG
    "state": "running",
    "worker_id": "worker-20260415T011331-d0a4"
  }

I wanted the worker to pick an axon bead. The server ran the worker against the ddx repo instead. There is no --project flag, no env var, nothing in the CLI to redirect the server's project scope.

Currently the only way to target a specific project with execute-loop is --local mode (which spawns a subprocess using the caller's CWD as the project root). This defeats the purpose of having a server in a multi-project deployment.

## Scope

- --project <path> or --project <name> flag on execute-loop, execute-bead, and 'ddx agent run' that tells the server which project_root to use
- Env var fallback: DDX_PROJECT_ROOT or similar
- Default to CWD if unset and no flag
- Server refuses ambiguous/unmatched project names with a clean error
- Integration test: from CWD A, submit a loop targeting project B; assert worker runs in B's project_root

## Why this matters for the user's workflow

Without this, the ONLY way to drive multi-project execution from one shell is --local, which (a) loses the server's queue visibility, (b) ties the parent process to the child, and (c) negates the 'ddx agent execute-loop submits to server by default' ergonomic that execute-loop's --help promises.

## Acceptance

- [ ] --project flag wired through execute-loop/execute-bead/run
- [ ] Env var fallback documented
- [ ] Default behavior: CWD-based project detection
- [ ] Integration test for cross-project submission
- [ ] execute-loop --help updated to describe project targeting
title: exec: execute-loop --project flag to target a specific project when server is multi-project

description: Execute-bead creates a worktree from a git snapshot that predates recently created beads. When execute-loop picks up a new bead and calls execute-bead, the worktree's beads.jsonl doesn't contain that bead, causing 'bead: not found' errors. The snapshot needs to be refreshed to include beads created after the worktree was spawned.

acceptance: ddx agent log --bead axon-abc123 shows all execution attempts for that bead in chronological order with ATTEMPT, STARTED, DURATION, HARNESS, MODEL, OUTCOME, TOKENS, COST, SESSION columns. OUTCOME is resolved from result.json when available, falls back to exit_code. Summary line printed after table. --json emits filtered SessionEntry array with outcome field. Bead with no sessions prints 'no sessions found for bead <id>'. Existing ddx agent log behavior (no --bead flag) is unchanged. ddx agent log <session-id> single-session detail view is unchanged.
description:
ddx agent log is fully implemented (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go lines 745-807) and reads .ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl. Each SessionEntry has a Correlation map (cli/internal/agent/types.go) that includes bead_id when the session was created by execute-bead. However there is no way to filter sessions by bead — you must know the session ID in advance or scroll through all recent sessions.

This bead adds --bead <id> filtering and a per-bead attempt summary view to the existing command.

## Required changes

### 1. --bead flag on ddx agent log (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)
When --bead <bead-id> is provided:
  - Scan all SessionEntry records in sessions.jsonl
  - Filter to entries where Correlation["bead_id"] == bead-id
  - Sort ascending by Timestamp (attempt order)
  - Display in attempt-history table format (see below)

No change to the default behavior (no --bead = show recent N sessions as today).

### 2. Attempt history table columns
ATTEMPT | STARTED | DURATION | HARNESS | MODEL | OUTCOME | TOKENS | COST | SESSION
- ATTEMPT: 1-based index in chronological order
- OUTCOME: merged / preserved / no-changes / error (from ExecuteBeadResult.Outcome via Correlation or result.json)
- SESSION: truncated session ID (8 chars) for use with ddx agent log <session-id>
- COST: formatted as /bin/zsh.0042 or 'local' if cost_usd == 0

### 3. Outcome resolution
SessionEntry does not directly store outcome — it stores exit_code and error. The full outcome is in .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/result.json (ExecuteBeadResult struct). When rendering the attempt table:
  - Use Correlation["attempt_id"] to find .ddx/executions/{attempt_id}/result.json
  - Read ExecuteBeadResult.Outcome from that file
  - Fall back to exit_code == 0 ? 'success' : 'error' if result.json is absent

### 4. Summary line
After the table, print a one-line summary:
  '<bead-id>: N attempts, M merged, K preserved, J errors. Last attempt: <elapsed> ago.'

### 5. --json passthrough
When --bead and --json are both set, emit the filtered SessionEntry array as JSON with an added outcome field resolved from result.json.

## Key files
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — newAgentLogCommand() lines 745-807, add --bead flag and filter path
- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry struct (lines 94-122), Correlation map field
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadResult struct (lines 30-64), artifact paths convention
- .ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl — runtime data source

acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer browsing the DDx web UI, I want the documents list to show only canonical project documents under the configured roots, so I can find `docs/resources/agent-harness-ac.md` without seeing duplicates from agent worktrees or broken absolute-path links.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. `findMarkdownFiles` skips `.claude` (in addition to `.git` and `.ddx`) and does not descend into any `worktrees/` directory regardless of parent.
2. `doc.Path` stored in the graph is relative to `workingDir`; no document path begins with `/`.
3. GraphQL `documents` query returns zero entries whose path contains `.claude/worktrees/` when the repo has active worktrees under `.claude/worktrees/`.
4. GraphQL `document(id:)` resolver handles both the previously-absolute form and the new relative form for a grace period (read-side tolerance) OR all callers are updated atomically.
5. Web UI document route `/documents/:path` renders the correct document content for `docs/resources/agent-harness-ac.md` (no double slash, no 404).
6. Unit test in `cli/internal/docgraph/docgraph_test.go` creates a fixture with `.claude/worktrees/agent-x/docs/foo.md` and `docs/foo.md`, and asserts only the latter appears in the graph with a relative path.
7. Playwright e2e test in `cli/internal/server/frontend/` navigates to the documents list, asserts no listed document has a path containing `.claude/` or starting with `/`, clicks a real `docs/` document, and verifies the content renders.
description:
## Observed

Browsing the web UI to a document URL renders `Document not found` instead of content. The URL contains a worktree path with a leading double slash, e.g.:

  /nodes/<node>/projects/<proj>/documents//Users/erik/Projects/ddx/.claude/worktrees/agent-a0673989/docs/resources/agent-harness-ac.md

Two distinct defects:

1. **`.claude/worktrees/` pollution** — the documents list surfaces markdown files from AI agent worktrees checked out under `.claude/worktrees/`. These are throwaway copies of the repo; they are noise and frequently shadow / duplicate the real `docs/` tree.
2. **Absolute document paths** — document IDs/paths are absolute (`/Users/erik/...`), producing malformed URLs (leading double slash) and failing the reader resolver.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/docgraph/docgraph.go:247` (`findMarkdownFiles`) walks `workingDir` and only skips `.git` and `.ddx`. It traverses `.claude/` (including `.claude/worktrees/<agent>/…`) and records every `.md` it finds.

`cli/internal/docgraph/docgraph.go:128` stores `doc.Path = filepath.Clean(filePath)`. Because `filepath.Walk` hands back paths rooted at the absolute `workingDir`, `doc.Path` ends up absolute. The GraphQL document resolver surfaces this absolute path as the document's id/path, which the frontend then interpolates into a URL.

## Scope of fix

- Exclude `.claude/` (and any `worktrees/` subtree) from doc discovery. `.claude` is a tool-managed directory analogous to `.git`/`.ddx`; it is not a source of canonical project documents.
- Normalize `doc.Path` to be relative to `workingDir` so routes stay clean and portable.
- No change required on the frontend beyond existing URL handling once paths are relative.

description:
Migrate cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go TestExecuteBeadPreserveOnMergeFailure() from fakeExecuteBeadGit + fakeAgentRunner to real git + script harness.

Pattern:
- Use cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go newScriptHarnessRepo(t, beadCount) to build an isolated repo with real git.
- Use runGitInteg (from cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go) for any supplemental git invocations, so GIT_* env is scrubbed.
- Replace fakeExecuteBeadGit with the RealLandingGitOps flow used in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land_test.go.
- Replace fakeAgentRunner with the script harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go) driven by a per-attempt directive file. When the test needs to simulate 'agent emits no commits' or 'agent emits a dirty worktree', encode that as script directives rather than a canned Result struct.
- Preserve every assertion — the point is that the test now exercises real git + real merge/preserve semantics, not that the surface shrinks.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadPreserveOnMergeFailure$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.

description:
<context-digest>
Observed on 2026-04-11 while launching server-managed execute-loop workers against the restarted 8094 server. Worker records remained state=running with current_bead=null even after distinct execution bundles and manifests had already been created for claimed beads.
</context-digest>

Worker API projection lags behind real execution start. When a server-managed execute-loop worker has already claimed a bead and created an execution bundle, /api/agent/workers and /api/agent/workers/:id should expose that current bead immediately instead of waiting for end-of-run summary parsing.

## Scope
- Project current_bead as soon as the worker claims or launches a bead
- Keep worker detail/list views aligned with execution bundle state while a run is active
- Add regression coverage for running workers whose current bead is known before process exit

## Acceptance Criteria
Server-managed execute-loop workers expose current_bead promptly while running, not only after exit or summary parsing, and tests cover the live-running projection path.
title: Project current bead immediately for running server-managed workers

acceptance: File library/personas/test-engineer.md exists with valid YAML frontmatter. Body covers the core stance: favor real e2e tests (no mocks at e2e layer); stubs over mocks at unit layer; contract tests between stub and real backend; zero mocks in integration tests; behavior over implementation; deterministic factories; behavior-naming; testing pyramid as shape not ratios; performance measurements require baseline + explicit boundary + reproducible harness. grep -E "cli/internal/|docs/helix/|orchTestGitOps|fakeExecuteBeadGit|gateTestGitOps|mockExecutor|script\.go|integration_helper_test" library/personas/test-engineer.md returns no matches. No hard numeric coverage threshold presented as a universal rule. No "TDD is the only valid approach" framing. No retry-loop-around-flaky-test as acceptable pattern. No performance claim pattern without baseline + boundary. Sources section cites at least two external works.
description:
The existing library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md is the strongest shipped persona content-wise, but it references DDx-internal paths that only exist in the DDx repo (docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md, cli/internal/agent/script.go, named DDx legacy offenders like orchTestGitOps/fakeExecuteBeadGit/gateTestGitOps/mockExecutor).

Rename to library/personas/test-engineer.md and generalize.

**CORE STANCES (load-bearing — preserve these):**
- Favor real e2e tests. At the e2e layer, do not mock anything — real infrastructure, real processes, real network where applicable. E2e tests that mock the database or the HTTP layer are unit tests lying about their scope.
- Stubs over mocks at the unit layer. Extract interfaces at domain boundaries; write in-memory stubs that return canned values set up by the test. Stubs do not record call sequences.
- Contract tests between in-memory stubs and real backends — same suite runs against both, proving they are interchangeable. This is the discipline that keeps stubs honest.
- Zero mocks in integration tests. Use real git (temp dir with scrubbed env), real filesystem (t.TempDir), real database (temp file / temp container), real HTTP (local test server). The only approved fake is at the AI-provider (or equivalent third-party-SDK) boundary.
- Behavior over implementation — assert on observable state, not on call counts or invocation sequences.
- Deterministic factory functions / fakers over static JSON fixtures.
- Test names describe behavior ("creates invoice when all line items valid"), not positions ("test_create_invoice_3").
- Testing pyramid as a *shape guide* (more unit than integration than e2e), not as mandated ratios.
- **Performance measurements require a baseline and clear boundaries.** Any claim that something is "fast", "scales", or "improved performance" must include: (a) a numeric baseline to compare against (prior version, prior run, published SLO), (b) an explicit boundary — what unit of work is being measured, what environment, what is excluded (cold-start, warmup, network latency, etc.), (c) reproducible harness so the measurement can be re-run. "It feels fast" and "benchmarks look good" are not performance claims.

**STRIP (cargo-cult testing dogma — do NOT ship):**
- Hard coverage percentages as universal rules (">= 80% overall", "100% on critical paths"). Replace with: "measure coverage where the project tracks it; coverage is a signal, not a target".
- Specific timing budgets as universal rules ("unit < 10ms", "integration < 100ms"). Replace with: "tests should be fast enough that developers run them; the specific threshold is a project concern".
- Specific pyramid percentages ("60% unit / 25% integration / 10% contract / 5% e2e") — keep the shape, drop the numbers.
- TDD as the only valid approach. Test-first is one workflow; test-alongside is another; test-after for a specific reason can be defensible. Do not call non-TDD "worst practice".
- Tests-for-the-sake-of-tests (mirror-of-spec tests that add no signal, trivial getters/setters).
- Mutation testing as a quality requirement. Mention as an optional signal, not a bar to clear.
- Snapshot tests without a maintenance plan.
- Retry loops around flaky tests as "improvement" — a retry loop hides a bug; fix the flake.
- Branch coverage vs line coverage dogma — say "coverage metrics matter less than whether tests catch real bugs".

**STRIP (DDx-internal specifics):**
- References to cli/internal/agent/script.go (replace with generic "a deterministic script-like fake at the AI-provider boundary")
- Named DDx legacy offenders (orchTestGitOps, fakeExecuteBeadGit, gateTestGitOps, mockExecutor) — these belong in a helix-test-engineer plugin persona, not this generic base
- Authoritative-sources frontmatter section citing docs/helix/concerns.md and HELIX concern.md
- Specific paths like cli/internal/bead, cli/internal/agent

Ground in external canon:
- Kent Beck - Test-Driven Development: By Example
- Martin Fowler - "Mocks Arent Stubs" (canonical reference for why call-sequence assertions are a code smell)
- Brendan Gregg - Systems Performance methodology (for the baseline + boundary stance on perf measurement)
- Anthropic Prompt Library (if a testing entry exists)
- TDD Guard from anthropics/skills repo (if the automated-test-first-enforcement pattern is worth mentioning)

Body structure: Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources.

Frontmatter:
---
name: test-engineer
roles: [test-engineer, quality-analyst]
description: Testing specialist enforcing stubs-over-mocks, contract tests, real e2e, behavior-over-implementation, testing pyramid as shape not ratios, and baselined performance measurement
tags: [testing, quality, performance]
---

In-scope: library/personas/test-engineer.md (new)
Out-of-scope: deletion of test-engineer-tdd.md (deprecation bead); helix-test-engineer plugin persona (HELIX concern)
title: Generalize test-engineer persona — strip DDx-internal path references

acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer running `go test`, my test runs do not leave behind registered projects in my real `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`. When a machine has already been polluted, I can prune it with one command. The server sweep is aggressive enough that obvious test-dir pollution does not survive a restart.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Fix A — test isolation enforced by CI.**
   - Every existing test that instantiates `ServerState` or boots a server helper is migrated to an isolated state dir. No test writes to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`.
   - A lint test (Go or shell) parses `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` (and any other directories that wire up `ServerState`) and fails if it finds a construction that does not pass an explicit isolated state path or override `XDG_DATA_HOME` first. Test cases added for both the allowed-pattern and the disallowed-pattern.

2. **Fix B — sweep recognizes test-dir pollution.**
   - `ServerState.migrate` drops entries whose `Path` matches any of:
     - prefix `/tmp/`, `/private/tmp/`, `/var/folders/`
     - regex `/Test[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9_]*\d+/` anywhere in the path
   - Missing paths matching these patterns are dropped on first sweep (no 24h tombstone).
   - Reachable paths matching these patterns are still dropped (they are never real projects — the test dir may exist transiently but is never the user's registered project).
   - Unit test seeds a fixture state with one real project, one `/tmp/TestFoo123/001` missing, one `/tmp/TestBar456/002` still on disk, and one `/private/tmp/TestMac789/001`; asserts only the real project survives.

3. **Fix C — pruning CLI.**
   - `ddx server state prune [--dry-run] [--state <path>]` applies the Fix B rules to an on-disk state file.
   - Writes `server-state.json.bak-<YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS>` before overwriting.
   - Prints summary: `Pruned X of Y entries (Z kept)`. Dry-run prints the summary without writing.
   - Integration test uses a fixture state containing 10 real + 100 fake entries, runs the command, asserts 10 remain and a backup file with exactly 110 entries was written.

4. **Perf amplifier — regression test.**
   - A test asserts `GetBeadSnapshots` does **not** call `os.Stat` on any project already tombstoned or pattern-filtered. (Implementation detail: sweep on startup + skip tombstoned in the hot loop.)
   - If Fix A+B are in place, a fresh `go test ./...` run followed by a server restart produces a state file with ≤ 20 projects on a typical dev machine. Asserted via a CI job that runs the tests, starts the server briefly, inspects the state file, and fails if test-dir patterns are present.

5. **Backward compatibility.**
   - The prune CLI is safe to run on an existing polluted state file. The sweep upgrade is safe on restart — no data loss for real projects.
   - On upgrade, a note is logged: "Pruned X phantom test-dir projects from state file."

6. **Cross-reference.**
   - Bead notes link to `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) as the two surfaces whose perf problems are amplified by this bug. Those beads' fixture sizing assumptions were correct for a clean state file; the operator experience will still feel slow on a polluted machine until Fix C is run.
description:
## Observed

`~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json` on a developer machine contains **7,343 registered projects**. Composition:

- **6,936 named "001"** — all are Go test temp dirs like `/tmp/TestAgentCapabilitiesCommandJSON1451829170/001`, `/tmp/TestAgentCheckSuccess3666443068/001`.
- **344 named "002"** — same pattern in tests that use `002/`.
- **~60 real projects** (ddx, helix, axon, plus per-branch worktrees).
- Only 135 of the 6,936 are marked unreachable; the rest are "alive" because their `/tmp` dir still exists or because `LastSeen` is recent.
- State file size: 1.8 MB.

## Root cause

1. Go tests that exercise the server (or anything that wires up a `ServerState`) call `RegisterProject` with a temp-dir path. The state file they write to is **not isolated** — it defaults to the shared XDG location (`serverAddrDir()` at `cli/internal/server/server.go:67`) unless the test explicitly overrides `XDG_DATA_HOME` or constructs state via the `tc.StateDir` injection path.
2. There is no test-side deregistration on teardown. Temp dirs get cleaned up by `t.TempDir()`'s own finalizer, but the project entry in the production state file stays.
3. The reachability sweep (`ServerState.migrate` at `cli/internal/server/state.go:85-148`) tombstones missing-path entries and drops them 24h later. With thousands of entries being added per test run, the rate of addition outpaces the 24h GC.

## Perf amplifier (cross-cutting)

Both `GetBeadSnapshots` (`state_graphql.go:49-105`) and `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` (`state_graphql.go:476-489`) iterate every registered project per request. With N=7343 that's ~7k `os.Stat` + `store.ReadAll` attempts per bead-list or sessions query, mostly against dead paths. This is part of why `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) feel slow in practice even before their own N·M bugs are considered.

## Proposed direction

Three independently-landable fixes:

### Fix A — Test isolation (highest leverage)

- Audit every test that calls `ServerState` / `RegisterProject` / boots a server. Each one must point at an isolated state dir (`t.TempDir()` + `XDG_DATA_HOME` override, or direct `tc.StateDir` injection).
- Add a linting test: parse `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` for constructions of `ServerState` or HTTP server test helpers and assert each uses an isolated state dir. Fail CI otherwise.
- This is the real fix. The rest is damage mitigation.

### Fix B — Smarter sweep (defense in depth)

- Sweep: any path under `/tmp`, `/private/tmp`, `/var/folders/*/T/` (macOS test temp root), or matching `/tmp/Test*` should be treated as a **candidate test-dir project** and dropped immediately on first sweep that finds it missing — no 24h tombstone.
- Sweep should also drop entries where `Path` contains any `Test[A-Z]\w+\d+` segment (the Go testing convention), regardless of whether the dir currently exists. These are never real projects.
- Unit test: seed a state with mixed real + fake-test-dir entries, run sweep, assert only the real ones remain.

### Fix C — Manual cleanup CLI

- `ddx server state prune` — read state file, apply Fix B's rules, write backup to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json.bak-<timestamp>`, write cleaned state, print summary (X dropped, Y kept).
- Useful for recovery on machines that have already accumulated pollution — like the reporter's. Keep even after Fix A lands.
- Dry-run flag prints the summary without writing.

## Out of scope

- Moving state to SQLite.
- Per-user project management UI. (Follow-up if the projects list becomes a real operator surface.)
- Renaming "001" away from a confusing default. The name field is just `filepath.Base(path)`; fixing Fix A makes this question moot.

No-changes rationale for ddx-15f7ee0b attempt 20260423T040113-a89a35ab
======================================================================

Status: All acceptance criteria are already satisfied by commits already
present in the base branch (a101e215 and its ancestors). No additional
changes are required.

The previous REVIEW:BLOCK on this bead appears to have been a review-side
mis-targeting: the reviewer's diff covered the evidence-only commit
034fbf8f instead of the implementation commits af88d432 and 1f1b916c,
both of which are already merged into main and are ancestors of the
current base revision a101e215. The review notes themselves acknowledge
this possibility ("the actual implementation may have been merged already
but is not visible in this diff").

Verification of every AC item against the working-tree state:

AC §1 — Fix A: test isolation enforced by CI
  - cli/internal/server/test_isolation_lint_test.go provides
    TestTestsIsolateStateDir, which AST-walks every *_test.go in the
    server package and fails if a test wires up a server constructor
    without first isolating XDG_DATA_HOME via setupTestDir-family helpers
    or t.Setenv("XDG_DATA_HOME", ...).
  - TestTestIsolationLintCatchesBadCase exercises both the allowed-pattern
    (good source passes) and the disallowed-pattern (bad source flagged)
    arms of the lint, satisfying the "Test cases added for both ..."
    sub-clause of AC §1.
  - All existing tests pass the lint (verified by `go test`).

AC §2 — Fix B: sweep recognizes test-dir pollution
  - cli/internal/server/state.go exposes IsTestDirPath, which matches
    /tmp/, /private/tmp/, /var/folders/ prefixes and any path containing
    a /Test[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9_]*\d+/ segment (the goTestNameRegexp).
  - ServerState.migrate (state.go:158-229) drops matching entries on
    first sweep with no 24 h tombstone delay (Fix B item 1).
  - ServerState.SweepProjects (state.go:295-330) drops matching entries
    whether or not the path still exists on disk (Fix B item 2).
  - Unit test TestMigrateDropsTestDirPollution (state_test.go:90-172)
    seeds a fixture state containing one real project, one missing
    /tmp/TestFoo123/001 entry, one *existing* /tmp/Test... entry, and a
    /private/tmp/TestMac789/001 entry; asserts only the real project
    survives migration. This is exactly the seeded-fixture test specified
    in AC §2.

AC §3 — Fix C: pruning CLI
  - cli/cmd/server_state.go registers `ddx server state prune` with
    --dry-run and --state flags. Default --state resolves to
    server.DefaultStateFilePath() (XDG_DATA_HOME/ddx/server-state.json).
  - server.PruneStateFile (state.go:487-535) writes
    server-state.json.bak-<UTC YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS> beside the file before
    rewriting, prints "Pruned X of Y entries (Z kept)", and skips the
    write entirely when DryRun is true.
  - Integration test TestServerStatePruneDropsPhantomsKeepsRealProjects
    (cmd/server_state_test.go:22-130) seeds 10 real + 100 fake entries,
    runs the command, asserts 10 survive and a backup containing exactly
    110 entries was written.
  - TestServerStatePruneDryRun additionally verifies --dry-run leaves the
    file mtime unchanged and writes no backup.

AC §4 — Perf amplifier regression test
  - TestGetBeadSnapshotsSkipsTombstonedProjects
    (graphql_bead_perf_skip_phantom_test.go) installs a beadStoreOpenHook
    and asserts GetBeadSnapshots never opens a bead store for a project
    whose Unreachable=true. The unreachable filter happens inside
    GetProjects (state.go:335-347), which the bead path consumes before
    delegating to beadSnapshotsForProjectEntry.
  - The CI job "Guard server state against test pollution" added in
    .github/workflows/ci.yml (commit 1f1b916c) runs the full test suite
    under an isolated XDG_DATA_HOME, starts the server, and fails if the
    resulting state file contains more than 20 projects or any path
    matching the test-dir patterns.

AC §5 — Backward compatibility
  - loadServerState (state.go:113-143) emits "ddx-server: Pruned X
    phantom test-dir projects from state file" when migrate drops at
    least one entry, satisfying the upgrade-log requirement.
  - TestLoadServerStateLogsPhantomCleanup (state_test.go:205-239) seeds
    a state file with two phantom entries, redirects the stdlib logger,
    invokes loadServerState, and asserts the cleanup line appears in the
    captured output.
  - PruneStateFile is idempotent on already-pruned files (a clean state
    file produces Dropped=0 and no functional change beyond the backup).

AC §6 — Cross-reference
  - The bead's <notes> already contain the cross-reference paragraph
    linking ddx-9ce6842a (beads perf) and ddx-2ceb02fa (sessions feed)
    as the surfaces amplified by this pollution.
  - state.go:40-43 and state_graphql.go documentation comments name the
    same two beads in the source so future maintainers see the link.

Test runs (commands executed in this worktree):

  $ go test ./internal/server/ -count=1 \
        -run 'TestIsTestDirPath|TestMigrateDropsTestDirPollution|TestSweepProjectsDropsTestDirPollution|TestLoadServerStateLogsPhantomCleanup|TestTestsIsolateStateDir|TestTestIsolationLintCatchesBadCase|TestGetBeadSnapshotsSkipsTombstonedProjects'
  PASS
  ok   github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/server  0.034s

  $ go test ./cmd/ -count=1 -run 'TestServerStatePrune'
  PASS
  ok   github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/cmd  0.015s

  $ go test ./internal/server/ -count=1 -timeout 120s
  ok   github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/server  22.464s

  $ go build ./...
  (clean)

Pre-existing unrelated failures in ./cmd: TestInstallationPerformance and
the TestAcceptance_US028..US034 family fail because they expect a
pre-built `cli/build/ddx` binary that is not produced by the test
harness. These failures pre-date this bead (the test files were last
touched by commits 0a9c892d / d6dc8cdc / 62d1807d / d02fdea2, none of
which are related to ddx-15f7ee0b).

Conclusion: nothing to commit. The branch already meets the contract.
A bead-attempt commit would only add a no-op marker. Returning control
to the orchestrator without producing one.

    /tmp/, /private/tmp/, /var/folders/ prefixes and any path containing
    a /Test[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9_]*\d+/ segment (the goTestNameRegexp).
  - ServerState.migrate (state.go:158-229) drops matching entries on
    first sweep with no 24 h tombstone delay (Fix B item 1).
  - ServerState.SweepProjects (state.go:295-330) drops matching entries
    whether or not the path still exists on disk (Fix B item 2).
  - Unit test TestMigrateDropsTestDirPollution (state_test.go:90-172)
    seeds a fixture state containing one real project, one missing
    /tmp/TestFoo123/001 entry, one *existing* /tmp/Test... entry, and a
    /private/tmp/TestMac789/001 entry; asserts only the real project
    survives migration. This is exactly the seeded-fixture test specified
    in AC §2.

AC §3 — Fix C: pruning CLI
  - cli/cmd/server_state.go registers `ddx server state prune` with
    --dry-run and --state flags. Default --state resolves to
    server.DefaultStateFilePath() (XDG_DATA_HOME/ddx/server-state.json).
  - server.PruneStateFile (state.go:487-535) writes
    server-state.json.bak-<UTC YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS> beside the file before
    rewriting, prints "Pruned X of Y entries (Z kept)", and skips the
    write entirely when DryRun is true.
  - Integration test TestServerStatePruneDropsPhantomsKeepsRealProjects
    (cmd/server_state_test.go:22-130) seeds 10 real + 100 fake entries,
    runs the command, asserts 10 survive and a backup containing exactly
    110 entries was written.
  - TestServerStatePruneDryRun additionally verifies --dry-run leaves the
    file mtime unchanged and writes no backup.

AC §4 — Perf amplifier regression test
  - TestGetBeadSnapshotsSkipsTombstonedProjects
    (graphql_bead_perf_skip_phantom_test.go) installs a beadStoreOpenHook
    and asserts GetBeadSnapshots never opens a bead store for a project
    whose Unreachable=true. The unreachable filter happens inside
    GetProjects (state.go:335-347), which the bead path consumes before

  - The CI job "Guard server state against test pollution" added in

AC §5 — Backward compatibility
  - loadServerState (state.go:113-143) emits "ddx-server: Pruned X
    phantom test-dir projects from state file" when migrate drops at
    least one entry, satisfying the upgrade-log requirement.
  - TestLoadServerStateLogsPhantomCleanup (state_test.go:205-239) seeds
    a state file with two phantom entries, redirects the stdlib logger,
    invokes loadServerState, and asserts the cleanup line appears in the
    captured output.
  - PruneStateFile is idempotent on already-pruned files (a clean state
    file produces Dropped=0 and no functional change beyond the backup).

AC §6 — Cross-reference
  - The bead's <notes> already contain the cross-reference paragraph
    linking ddx-9ce6842a (beads perf) and ddx-2ceb02fa (sessions feed)

acceptance:
- [ ] After the fix, a session that performs zero actual compactions emits zero compaction.start/end pairs (or at most one compaction.skip per turn at low verbosity)
- [ ] When compaction does perform real work (messages_after < messages_before OR token count drops), events are still emitted with the same data shape they have today
- [ ] Operator-facing docs (if any) updated to reflect the new event semantics
- [ ] Regression test in the appropriate package asserts no-op compactions do not emit events at the default verbosity level
description:
Embedded agent emits a compaction.start + compaction.end event pair around EVERY turn (pre-turn check + post-turn check), even when no compaction is performed. Observed in session log .ddx/executions/20260418T191746-684f612a/embedded/agent-1776539866960442383.jsonl during a bragi qwen3.6-35b-a3b run on bead ddx-8610020e:

  {"type":"compaction.start","data":{"messages_before":1}}
  {"type":"compaction.end","data":{"messages_after":1,"messages_before":1,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
  {"type":"compaction.start","data":{"messages_before":6,"mid_turn":true}}
  {"type":"compaction.end","data":{"messages_after":6,"messages_before":6,"mid_turn":true,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
  ... and so on every turn

Both fields together (no_compaction:true, success:false) when messages_before == messages_after make this a NO-OP. The event is documenting that the compactor was asked, looked at the input, and decided nothing needed compacting. That is not interesting telemetry.

Real-world impact:
  - Misleads operators ("why is compaction firing constantly? — am I context-bound?") when the actual signal is "no compaction needed, all is well"
  - Inflates session-log size for no diagnostic value
  - Triggered the false hypothesis on 2026-04-18 that compaction-thrash was the bottleneck on cheap-tier beads (real bottleneck was per-turn latency)

Fix options:
  1. Suppress the start+end pair entirely when the compactor decides no_compaction. Only log when work was actually performed (messages_after < messages_before, OR success:true with measurable token reduction).
  2. Log a single compaction.skip event instead of the start+end pair, with reason ("below threshold", "nothing summarizable", etc.). Smaller but still findable for operators who want to confirm the check ran.
  3. Demote no-op events to a lower verbosity tier (only emit when a debug flag is set).

Likely lives in the ddx-agent compaction package upstream — but the symptom shows up in DDx session logs and operator UX, so DDx should own filing the upstream issue and consuming the fix when it lands. If the no-op log line is purely a DDx-side wrapper, fix here.
notes: Moved upstream to agent-22c95151 (compaction package lives in ddx-agent library, not in DDx). Closing here.
title: agent: suppress no-op compaction telemetry (compaction.start/end pair when nothing was compacted)

acceptance: 1. ddx agent usage JSON/CSV/table includes an explicit cost basis for subscription harnesses such as claude/codex indicating estimated value rather than direct spend. 2. ddx status Agent Usage output preserves the same distinction. 3. Focused cmd tests pass.
description: Claude and Codex subscription-backed harnesses may report non-zero cost-equivalent values, but those values are estimates/value received, not direct billed spend. DDx usage/status reporting must expose that distinction explicitly so operators do not treat subscription usage as cash burn. In-scope files: cli/cmd/agent_usage.go, cli/cmd/status.go, and focused tests. Out-of-scope: changing routing cost-cap behavior, which already excludes local/subscription providers from billed cost caps.
title: Mark subscription harness costs as estimated value

acceptance:
- [ ] Reproduce: invoke a tracker commit via ddx work --local in a detached environment (no /dev/pts/0) and trigger the false positive, OR demonstrate the failure mode in a unit/integration test
- [ ] Root cause documented (lefthook tty handling, {staged_files} expansion under no-tty, or other)
- [ ] Fix applied: rewrite the conflicts hook command to be tty-independent (e.g. wrap grep with explicit empty-input handling, or switch to a small script that reads staged file list from git diff --cached --name-only) — and/or have ddx work bypass lefthook for its own tracker commits with a documented rationale
- [ ] Regression test or runbook step that exercises the fix
- [ ] No reintroduction of the bug across at least one full ddx work --local drain pass
description:
During a `ddx work --local --provider bragi --model qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b` drain on 2026-04-18T19:17, the loop tried to commit a tracker update for ddx-91bc770a (no_changes outcome). lefthook pre-commit blocked it with:

  🥊 conflicts: Merge conflict markers found. Resolve conflicts before committing. (0.00 seconds): exit status 1

But:
  1. Verified no file in the repo matches lefthooks own regex `^<<<<<<<[ \t]|^=======$|^>>>>>>>[ \t]|^\|\|\|\|\|\|\|[ \t]` — including .ddx/beads.jsonl and the staged execution evidence dir.
  2. Every OTHER lefthook check (ddx-validate, large-files, secrets, resolution-path-citations, secrets) passed cleanly.
  3. lefthook output earlier in the run contained: `open /dev/pts/0: permission denied` — strongly suggests lefthook is mis-handling its absence of a controlling tty when ddx work runs detached as a background worker.
  4. The conflicts grep ran in 0.00s and returned exit 1 — too fast to be a real grep failure on real input. Probably a non-grep failure mode (maybe lefthook fallback path, or empty {staged_files} expansion combined with no stdin).

The hook command per lefthook.yml is:
  grep -E "^<<<<<<<[ \t]|^=======$|^>>>>>>>[ \t]|^\|\|\|\|\|\|\|[ \t]" {staged_files} 2>/dev/null && exit 1 || exit 0

This blocks execute-loop tracker commits non-deterministically and leaves beads in an inconsistent state (claimed/heartbeated but not closed/unclaimed). Must be either fixed in lefthook config (more robust hook), bypassed when ddx work owns the commit (the loop already validates its own tracker writes), or both.
title: lefthook conflicts hook false-positive when ddx work runs detached (tty unavailable)

acceptance: No-change execute-bead attempts are classified as non-success, do not close beads, and regression tests cover the reproduced `outcome=no-changes` / `status=success` bug from execute-loop evidence
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: execute-loop outcome classification. Evidence covers .ddx/executions/20260410T220647-5ad936b2/result.json, where bead ddx-40a722c3 recorded `outcome=no-changes`, `detail=agent made no commits`, and `result_rev==base_rev`, but the status was still `success` and the loop appended a closed tracker row.
</context-digest>
Fix execute-bead/execute-loop result classification so no-change attempts cannot be treated as successful landed work.

## Scope
- Reproduce the no-change-success path from execute-loop
- Ensure attempts with no commits / no diff / unchanged result_rev cannot produce `status=success` or close the bead by default
- Align loop close behavior with the result classification contract so only true successful work closes a bead
- Add regression coverage around no-change, preserved, and successful landed cases

## Boundaries
- Do not change the documented merge-by-default policy for real successful executions
- Keep this fix focused on outcome/close classification, not broader prompt-quality or routing issues
notes: Fixed in a2db669: no-change execute-bead attempts are no longer treated as success or bead-closing outcomes.
title: Do not classify no-change execute-loop attempts as success

acceptance:
1. On a host where ddx agent check reports at least one LM Studio provider
   (bragi, grendel, vidar, vidar-coder) connected, ddx agent execute-loop
   --once --json against a ready bead routes a cheap-tier attempt to a live
   provider without requiring --provider or --harness on the CLI.
2. The result.json for the cheap-tier attempt records status != "execution_failed"
   when the live provider accepts the request (status may be success, no_changes,
   or a non-dispatch failure downstream, but must NOT be "did not match any
   provider, catalog ref, or model-route" and must NOT be "harness lmstudio
   not available: not found in PATH").
3. New unit / integration test in the execute-bead routing package asserts:
   given a catalog entry cheap.embedded-openai: qwen3.5-27b and a discovered
   provider advertising qwen3.5-27b, the resolver returns that provider.
4. New unit / integration test asserts: the lmstudio harness is treated as
   HTTP-probeable, not PATH-probeable, so it survives harness availability

5. cd cli && go test ./... && go build .
6. ddx agent doctor and ddx agent check exit 0 on a host with live LM Studio

description:
Observed on 2026-04-17T02:39 during an execute-loop --once cycle against bead
helix-ede8085a (from /Users/erik/Projects/helix). Evidence preserved at
/Users/erik/Projects/helix/.ddx/executions/20260417T023917-dc4a49d3/result.json
and /Users/erik/Projects/helix/.ddx/executions/20260417T023921-e66e9375/result.json.

ddx agent check --verbose on this host reports live LM Studio providers:
bragi (10 models), grendel (3), vidar (11), vidar-coder (11), openrouter (345).
So model discovery IS working — the gap is in execute-bead's catalog→provider
resolution.

Symptom 1 — cheap tier / embedded-openai surface
  Routing decision: tier=cheap harness=agent model=qwen3.5-27b
  Failure: "model \"qwen3.5-27b\" did not match any provider, catalog ref, or
  model-route; specify --provider or --model-ref explicitly"
  The user's ~/.ddx/model-catalog.yaml maps cheap.embedded-openai → qwen3.5-27b,
  but the embedded ddx-agent harness has no provider registration that serves
  qwen3.5-27b. Meanwhile bragi / grendel / vidar all expose lmstudio-backed
  models and could plausibly serve it — but execute-bead never consults live
  provider discovery when a catalog model-ref fails to resolve.

Symptom 2 — lmstudio harness fallback
  Routing decision: harness=lmstudio model=minimax/minimax-m2.7
  Failure: "agent: harness lmstudio not available:  not found in PATH"
  lmstudio is an HTTP harness (ddx agent doctor reports it as "(http)" and OK),
  yet the execute-bead harness probe path-checks it like a binary CLI and
  rejects it before any HTTP probe runs.

Net effect: the auto-escalation ladder skips cheap and standard tiers entirely
on this machine and lands on smart-tier codex/gpt-5.4, defeating the
cost-tiered routing the parent epic (ddx-4b59b816) is designed to enable.
The operator should not have to pass --provider vidar or --harness codex to
route around this — that re-introduces the hyper-specification this epic is
trying to eliminate.

Root-cause candidates (pick one, not both):
  a) Extend execute-bead catalog resolution so that when a catalog surface
     maps to a model-ref the local harness cannot serve, it consults live
     provider discovery (the same discovery ddx agent check uses) to locate
     a reachable provider that advertises the model, and routes there.
  b) Teach ddx agent execute-bead that the lmstudio harness is HTTP-backed
     and probe it via the live-discovery path instead of PATH lookup, so the
     cheap-tier fallback from (qwen3.5-27b) → (minimax/minimax-m2.7 via
     lmstudio) actually reaches the bragi/grendel/vidar endpoints.

Both symptoms share a root: execute-bead's harness/model resolution has no
live-discovery hook. A fix should close that gap.
title: execute-bead cheap tier cannot resolve catalog models to live LM Studio providers

acceptance: `ddx agent execute-bead` resolves governing references for both doc-ID and repo-relative path-style `spec-id` values when the target file exists in the execution snapshot; the generated prompt/manifest include the resolved path reference instead of the `No governing references` fallback; and deterministic tests cover a bead whose `spec-id` is a repo-relative path such as `workflows/README.md`.
description:
<context-digest>
Alpha validation run from /Users/erik/Projects/helix using `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` against a live HELIX bead whose `spec-id` is the repo-relative path `workflows/README.md`. Evidence includes `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/prompt.md`, `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/manifest.json`, and `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go`.
</context-digest>

`ddx agent execute-bead` prompt synthesis failed to resolve a live path-style `spec-id` into governing references, so the generated execution prompt told the agent there were no governing references even though the referenced file exists in the execution snapshot.

## Reproduction
1. In `/Users/erik/Projects/helix`, inspect bead `helix-fef22846`; it carries `spec-id=workflows/README.md`.
2. Run `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` or `ddx agent execute-bead helix-fef22846 --json`.
3. Open `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/prompt.md`.
4. Observe the prompt includes `- spec-id: workflows/README.md` but the `## Governing References` section says `No governing references were resolved from the current execution snapshot.`

## Observed gap
`executeBeadResolveGoverningRefs()` builds the doc graph and looks up only by graph document ID. Path-like `spec-id` values that point to real files in the repo are dropped instead of being resolved as governing references.

## Why this matters
This is exactly the context the execution prompt tells the agent to read before changing code or docs. When DDx drops an existing repo-relative path reference, agent execution becomes less grounded and the artifact bundle misstates the available governing context.
title: Resolve path-style spec-id values in execute-bead governing references

acceptance: Project install materializes relative symlinks for skills declared in the plugin's package.yaml; re-install prunes removed symlinks; acceptance test covers both create and prune paths.

Current install.go lacks visible materialization; no stale-pruning scan.

acceptance: Backend Go test in cli/internal/server/server_test.go: (1) registers the same project path 5 times with t.Setenv-isolated state dir, asserts GET /api/projects returns exactly 1 project with last_seen = the most recent registration. (2) registers a path inside a linked worktree (via git worktree add fixture), asserts the stored path is the PRIMARY worktree, not the linked path. (3) registers a project, deletes the path on disk, runs the reachability sweep (expose a Server.SweepProjects helper for tests), asserts the project is marked unreachable with a tombstone timestamp, then asserts GET /api/projects with default params hides it and ?include_unreachable=true shows it. Migration test: writes a state file with 50 duplicate entries for the same canonical path, starts the server, asserts the post-startup state file has exactly 1 entry. Acceptance smoke: running the migration on the eitri server's current state file reduces the count from 4186 to single-digit, verifiable by curl https://127.0.0.1:7743/api/projects | jq length.
description:
GET /api/projects currently returns 4186 entries on the eitri node. The real number of projects on this machine is single-digit. Root cause: the per-CLI-invocation auto-registration in serverreg.TryRegisterAsync registers the project on EVERY ddx command run with no dedupe, AND historical bugs (CWD drift in linked worktrees, the stale beads-store-write bug fixed in 26c0266) caused thousands of phantom 'projects' to land with paths like /Users/erik/Projects/ddx/.claude/worktrees/agent-XYZ. The registry has no GC, no path canonicalization, no reachability check.

Fix scope:
1. Path canonicalization on register: filepath.Abs + EvalSymlinks before storing, so /tmp and /Users/erik/Projects/.... are normalized
2. Detect linked-worktree paths (via git rev-parse --git-common-dir) and store the PRIMARY worktree path, never the linked worktree path itself — this is the same fix we did for FindNearestDDxWorkspace in commit 26c0266 but applied to project registration
3. Dedupe on register: if a project with the same canonical path already exists, update its last_seen instead of creating a new entry
4. Reachability sweep: a periodic background goroutine (or a manual ddx server projects gc command) that stat()'s each registered project's path and either (a) marks it as 'unreachable' with a tombstone timestamp or (b) removes entries whose path has been gone for >24h
5. Migration: on server startup, run dedupe + path canonicalization + reachability sweep ONCE against the existing state file so the eitri 4186 entries collapse to the real count
6. GET /api/projects gains a ?include_unreachable= query (default false) so the UI can hide tombstones unless asked
title: Server: dedupe and garbage-collect the project registry; tombstone unreachable projects

acceptance:
**User story:** As an operator, I want a single view that shows every place my agents can run — endpoint providers and subprocess harnesses alike — with status, recent token usage, and quota utilization. When I click into one, I see a 7-day trend so I can tell if I'm about to run out.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Deliverable 1 — Unified view.**
   - New `harnessStatuses` GraphQL resolver returns subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini, agent) with: name, kind=`harness`, reachable, detail, lastCheckedAt, defaultForProfile.
   - Existing `providerStatuses` shape gains `kind=\"endpoint\"` and `lastCheckedAt`.
   - UI page loads within 500ms on a warm cache — it renders from last-known status **first**, then patches rows live as async probes return. Playwright asserts the table is interactive within 500ms of navigation and that row status patches within 5s of the probe completing.
   - Subprocess harnesses claude and codex **appear in the table** on a machine where their binaries are installed, with correct reachable status. Integration test.

2. **Deliverable 2 — Usage + quota fields (stubbable).**
   - Per-row fields `usage` and `quota` as described. All fields nullable.
   - For endpoint providers, `usage.tokensUsedLast24h` and `usage.tokensUsedLastHour` are computed from the sessions index (fixture: ≥1k session rows covering the last 24h). Unit test asserts correctness on a seeded window.
   - For subprocess harnesses that expose rate-limit headers (claude, codex), parsed headers populate `quota.ceilingTokens`, `quota.ceilingWindowSeconds`, `quota.remaining`, `quota.resetAt`. Parser tested with captured header fixtures from each harness.
   - For harnesses/providers that expose nothing, fields remain null and the UI renders `—` / "not reported"; no fabricated data.
   - Utilization bar renders when both `usage` and `quota.ceilingTokens` are present. Sparkline renders when ≥6 hourly buckets of usage are available.

3. **Deliverable 3 — Trend detail.**
   - Detail route `/nodes/.../providers/[name]` shows 7-day and 30-day tokens/hour + requests/hour time series, with quota ceiling overlay when known.
   - Buckets computed server-side from the sessions index; the client receives ≤ 200 data points per series.
   - "Projected to hit quota in ~X" callout uses last-24h slope only. Renders only when `quota.ceilingTokens` is known and the last-24h slope is non-zero positive; otherwise absent.
   - Playwright asserts the detail route renders with seeded usage and the projection text appears for a fixture that has a ceiling + ascending usage.

4. **Perf posture.**
   - Fixture for perf test: ≥1,000 session index rows covering the last 30 days with usage distributed across ≥5 endpoints/harnesses. Per standing directive in `ddx-9ce6842a`.
   - Targets: unified list p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP (excluding async probes); trend detail p95 ≤ 400ms HTTP on the fixture.
   - If in-place aggregation cannot hit the detail-view p95 on a 10,000-row stretch fixture, pause and raise the DB-substrate decision with the user per the standing directive — do not silently switch.

5. **Design note.**
   - One-pager in `docs/helix/02-design/` before implementation recording the page-rename decision, the row model (common fields, kind-specific fields), async-probe strategy, and the quota source per harness/provider. PR links to it.

6. **No regressions.**
   - `ddx agent providers`, `ddx agent doctor`, `ddx agent list`, `ddx agent check` CLI commands continue to work unchanged.
   - `defaultRouteStatus` query remains.

7. **Cross-references.**
   - Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for usage aggregation. If that bead is not yet landed when this work starts, ship Deliverable 1 standalone with usage/quota stubbed as "not available," and re-open the remaining deliverables when the index is ready.
description:
## Observed

Three related issues on `/nodes/.../providers`:

1. **Loads slowly.** The page fires two GraphQL queries on mount — `providerStatuses` (which probes every configured provider for live connectivity) and `defaultRouteStatus` (which resolves the default route). Both are synchronous and serialized only by `Promise.all`. Each provider probe is a real network call.
2. **Missing harnesses.** The pane shows API-endpoint providers (qwen, minimax, etc. — anything wired through the service with an endpoint). It does not show subprocess-invoked harnesses like `claude` (Claude Code) or `codex`. That is accurate to the current `providerStatuses` resolver (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_providers.go:15-50`, which calls `svc.ListProviders`) but not what an operator wants: the operator thinks of "where their agent work can run," which includes both endpoint providers AND subprocess harnesses.
3. **No quota visibility.** Operator need: see current token utilization rate + quota utilization per harness/provider, trend it, and use the trend to decide whether to throttle before hitting a cap.

## Root cause by area

- **Slow load:** `svc.ListProviders` probes every configured provider for liveness. Probes are serial within the service call, and the whole query blocks page render.
- **Missing harnesses:** Subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini) are first-class in the CLI (`ddx agent list`, `ddx agent doctor`, `ddx agent check`) but are not surfaced via `providerStatuses`. They have their own status concepts — binary installed, credentials configured, rate-limit headroom — that don't map cleanly onto the endpoint-provider model.
- **No quota:** No existing data source captures rolling token usage vs quota. Quota information varies wildly by harness: Claude Code has message / session headroom concepts from response headers; codex has rate-limit headers; endpoint providers have API quota per key. There is no unified representation today.

## Proposed direction

Three deliverables, sequenced from easy to hard.

### Deliverable 1 — Unified "Agent endpoints" view (renames/rescopes the page)

- Rename the surface from "Providers" to **"Agent endpoints"** (or similar — confirm copy in the design note). The domain object is "a place where agent work can run," not "an API provider."
- Merge the data: `providerStatuses` stays as the source for endpoint providers. A new `harnessStatuses` resolver (read from `svc.ListHarnesses` / existing `ddx agent list` + `ddx agent doctor` machinery) surfaces subprocess harnesses. Page renders both in one table with a `kind` column (`endpoint` | `harness`) — or two tables if design prefers.
- Status fields common to both: name, kind, reachable (bool), detail (text), last-checked-at, default-for-profile (set of profile names).
- Asynchronous liveness. The page renders an instant first paint from cached status; liveness probes fire in background and patch rows as they return. No synchronous probe-wall on load.

### Deliverable 2 — Token & quota fields

- Extend the status payload (per row) with: `usage { tokensUsedLastHour, tokensUsedLast24h, requestsLastHour, requestsLast24h }` and `quota { ceilingTokens?, ceilingWindowSeconds?, remaining?, resetAt? }`. Any field may be null if the provider/harness doesn't expose it.
- Data sources:
  - **Endpoint providers:** aggregate from the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) filtered to the last 1h / 24h, grouped by provider. Quota ceilings come from any headers we capture during calls, plus optional static config.
  - **Subprocess harnesses:** same sessions-index aggregation for usage. Quota — parse rate-limit headers from the harness output where available (Claude Code, codex both emit them). For harnesses that don't, leave quota null and render "not reported."
- Row gains two small inline displays: utilization bar (used / ceiling for the quota window) and a 7-day sparkline of tokens/hour.

### Deliverable 3 — Trend view

- Click into a row → detail page showing: 7-day and 30-day time-series of tokens/hour and requests/hour, with the quota ceiling overlaid. Aggregations come from the sessions index, bucketed server-side to avoid shipping raw rows.
- Simple "projected to hit quota in ~X hours/days" callout computed from the slope of the last 24h — enough to answer "are we on a trend to run out?" without building a forecasting system.

## Cross-bead dependencies

- Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for any usage-rate data. If that bead isn't landed when this work starts, the usage/quota features stub to "not available" and ship purely with the unified-view rename + async liveness.
- Fixture / perf guidance: per the standing directive recorded in `ddx-9ce6842a`, any aggregation here is tested against ≥1k session records. If in-place aggregation can't hit perf targets, bring the DB-substrate question to the user before picking one.

acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer on any project sub-page, I can always return to the project overview with a single click — either from a sidebar entry or from the DDx brand in the header.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. Sidebar has an "Overview" (or equivalent) entry at the top of the `pages` list, routing to `/nodes/<nodeId>/projects/<projectId>/`.
2. The entry is the active/highlighted one when the current URL matches the project-home route exactly (no trailing segment after `projectId`).
3. DDx brand in the header is an anchor. When a project is selected, it routes to that project's home; otherwise routes to `/`.
4. Playwright: navigate into `/beads`, click the Overview sidebar entry, assert URL is project home and queue summary renders. Then navigate into `/sessions`, click the DDx brand, assert URL is project home.
5. No regression to any existing sidebar entry.
description: ## Observed The project home page (`/nodes/.../projects//`) renders a useful overview — queue summary (ready/blocked/in-progress) plus three action cards (drain queue, re-align specs, run checks). User feedback: "I see it when I navigate to a new project, then it disappears." ## Root cause `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/components/NavShell.svelte:26-36` — the sidebar's `pages` array contains: beads, documents, graph, workers, sessions, personas, plugins, commits, efficacy. **There is no entry for the project home/overview.** Once a user clicks any sidebar link, there is no nav-level path back to the home page — only clicking the project in the `ProjectPicker` again, or editing the URL. Adjacent: the `DDx` brand in the header (`NavShell.svelte:61`) is not a link — common affordance for "go home" is missing. ## Proposed direction 1. Prepend a "Overview" (or "Home") entry to `pages` in `NavShell.svelte`, routing to `/nodes//projects//` (empty path segment after the project id). Use the `LayoutDashboard` icon that's already imported, or pick a dedicated home icon — design call. 2. Wrap the `DDx` brand in an `` that routes to `/` (node selector) or to the current project's home if one is selected. Pick one and stay consistent. Trivial change; the biggest decision is whether the brand link goes to node-root or project-home. Recommend project-home when a project is selected (less jarring) and node-root otherwise.

acceptance: catalog update populates costs for gpt-5.4 and other models; routing uses cost as tiebreaker when benchmarks unavailable; newer model versions preferred within same family
description:
OpenRouter exposes pricing for all models including subscription-tier ones (gpt-5.4, etc). We should:
1. Fetch ALL model prices from OpenRouter during catalog update, cross-reference by ID
2. For models not directly matchable by ID, store OpenRouter reference price separately as a capability signal
3. Use cost as directional capability indicator in routing when benchmark scores are unavailable: higher cost = likely higher capability
4. Add version ordering: within same model family, prefer newer version (opus-4.6 > opus-4.5) all else equal
5. Add ModelVersion field to ModelEntryYAML to encode family + version for comparison
6. Subscription/local harnesses still take priority (sunk cost), but when routing to pay-per-token (openrouter), use actual catalog prices for cost estimation
title: Use OpenRouter pricing as capability proxy and populate costs for all models
