acceptance: New file cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_integration_test.go contains at minimum the 9 test cases enumerated in the description. Shared helper newScriptHarnessRepo lives in cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go. Every test uses the script harness (not virtual, not fakeAgentRunner) and a real temp git repo created via git init with scrubbed GIT_* env. Every test verifies its assertion by running real git commands on the temp repo (git log, git rev-list, git for-each-ref) — no mock git. The suite runs under `go test ./internal/agent -run Integration -race -count=1` in under 60 seconds with all cases passing. Test 5 (FiveConcurrentBeads) proves linear history invariant that ddx-6aa50e57 acceptance demands. Test 6 (TwoWorkersSameBead) proves atomic claim invariant that ddx-3315dce2 acceptance demands. Test 7 (RebaseConflict) depends on the land coordinator from ddx-8746d8a6 being in place — it may be a t.Skip if the coordinator hasn't landed yet, with a TODO comment and a bead reference.
description:
Build the integration test suite for execute-loop orchestration using the script harness + real git temp repos. This is the test gap the opus test-plan review identified as the most critical coverage hole: every "concurrency" or "merge race" test today uses in-memory fakes with per-worker orchestrators, so the shared-writer invariant the meta-bead design depends on is never actually exercised.

## Test infrastructure helper

Build a shared helper `newScriptHarnessRepo(t *testing.T) (projectRoot, initialRev string)` in a new file `cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go` that:

1. Creates a temp dir via t.TempDir()
2. Runs `git init` with scrubbed GIT_* env (so the test's own pre-commit hook env doesn't leak)
3. Creates and commits an initial seed commit on `main`
4. Seeds .ddx/beads.jsonl with N ready beads (parameterized by test)
5. Returns projectRoot + the initial SHA

The helper must NOT inherit any GIT_DIR from the parent test environment.

## Test cases

Create cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_integration_test.go with these cases. Each runs against the script harness and real git:

1. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_SingleBead_AppendLine_Merged** — one bead, directive appends a line and commits, asserts git log main shows +1 commit, worktree is cleaned up, bead closed with closing_commit_sha.

2. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_NoOp_ClassifiedAsNoChanges** — directive no-op, asserts outcome=no-changes, bead stays open (below adjudication threshold), no commit on main.

3. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_DirtyWorktreeSynthesized** — directive creates files but omits explicit commit, asserts SynthesizeCommit fires, outcome=merged, single synthetic commit on main with the expected files.

4. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_FailedExit_WithCommits_Preserved** — directive commits then set-exit 1, asserts outcome=preserved, refs/ddx/iterations/<bead>/<ts>-<sha> exists, main unchanged.

5. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_FiveConcurrentBeads_LinearHistory** — seed 5 distinct beads, spawn 5 workers concurrently (single store, single projectRoot), assert `git log --merges main | wc -l` is 0, `git rev-list main ^initial | wc -l` is 5, every bead closed. **This is the ddx-6aa50e57 acceptance test that cannot be written today.**

6. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_TwoWorkersSameBead_ClaimedOnce** — seed 1 bead, spawn 2 workers against the same store, assert exactly one executes and the other reports no_ready_work. **This is the ddx-3315dce2 acceptance test.**

7. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_RebaseConflict_Preserved** — two beads both modify the same line in the same file, workers start from the same base, first lands cleanly, second gets rebase conflict and is preserved under refs/ddx/iterations/. Main has exactly +1 commit, ref exists for bead 2. (Only passes once the land coordinator from ddx-8746d8a6 is in place.)

8. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_ContextCancelBetweenIterations** — two beads, cancel context after first completes, assert second bead is not claimed. **This is the regression for commit 21d16e6.**

9. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_NoChangesRationale_ClosesBeadFast** — directive writes a no_changes_rationale.txt citing a commit SHA, asserts the bead closes as already_satisfied without the 3-strike cooldown wait.

Each test runs in isolation, cleans up its temp dir, and completes in under 10s. The full suite runs in under 60s. Use `-race` to catch any coordinator goroutine races.

## Out of scope

- Server-layer wiring (that's bead C)
- Real-AI acceptance tests (gated tier 4)
- Multi-project isolation (a separate bead, not filed here)
- Cross-machine scenarios (multi-machine topology bead)
title: Integration tests for execute-loop orchestration using script harness + real git

description:
## Observed

The graph view at `/nodes/.../projects/.../graph` renders a long flat list of amber-styled warnings, currently dominated by `duplicate document id "AC-AGENT-001" in "/Users/erik/Projects/ddx/.claude/worktrees/agent-a2818c5c/docs/resources/agent-harness-ac.md"` and similar entries. Two distinct concerns:

1. **Input noise** — most duplicates today come from `.claude/worktrees/<agent>/...` copies of the repo being indexed (tracked in `ddx-12cae4dd`). That fix will eliminate a large class of false positives but will not make the warning surface usable.
2. **Brittle degradation** — when documents have real problems (duplicate IDs in the canonical tree, broken frontmatter, missing `depends_on` targets, `id_to_path` mismatches), the system doesn't just warn — downstream consumers (documents list, staleness, UI routing) misbehave. And there is no workflow for a user to triage, diff, or fix the offending files.

## Current state of warnings

- `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/graph/+page.svelte:22-30` dumps `graph.warnings: string[]` into a flat amber block. No grouping, no categorization, no links to the offending file, no way to dismiss or act.
- Warning strings are generated in `cli/internal/docgraph/docgraph.go` (duplicate id, parse failure, required-root-missing, id_to_path mismatch/missing, cascade unknowns) as unstructured `fmt.Sprintf` output.
- No stale-document-pruning: if a `.md` is deleted on disk, any id_to_path entry pointing at it yields a warning instead of being auto-cleaned.

## Scope of this bead (deliberately not overlapping ddx-12cae4dd)

Assume `.claude/worktrees` noise is gone. This bead delivers the **integrity surface** that remains valuable: a way to see, categorize, and act on real document problems.

### Deliverables

1. **Structured warnings** — extend `docgraph.Graph.Warnings` from `[]string` to `[]GraphIssue{kind, path, id, message, relatedPath?}` and plumb through GraphQL. Keep a `messageLines(): []string` shim for any caller still using the old shape during the grace period.
2. **Integrity panel on /graph** — replace the flat amber block with a collapsible panel grouped by issue kind (Duplicate ID, Missing dep target, Broken id_to_path, Parse error, Required root missing). Each group shows count + expandable list; each row shows file path (clickable → document viewer), the offending ID, and a copyable message.
3. **Summary badge** — small badge next to the page title showing total issue count, so a clean graph reads clean.
4. **Audit GraphQL query** — `query { docGraphIssues { kind, path, id, message, relatedPath } }` usable independently of the full graph for future dashboard use. Reuses the same structured slice.
5. **Repair affordances (minimum viable)** — for two common cases:
   - Duplicate ID: show both file paths side-by-side and a "copy suggested unique ID" button (deterministic suggestion, e.g., append a short hash of the path). No auto-write.
   - Missing dep target: show the document declaring the missing dep, and a copy-to-clipboard snippet of the frontmatter line to remove. No auto-write.
   Auto-repair is explicitly out of scope until we trust the detection.
6. **CLI parity** — `ddx doctor` (or `ddx docs audit`) prints the same structured issues in a terminal-friendly form, so headless users and CI can surface problems.

### Out of scope

- Fixing `.claude/worktrees` indexing (owned by `ddx-12cae4dd`).
- Auto-rewrite of source files.
- Cross-project graph integrity (today's graph is per-project).

post-merge review: REQUEST_CHANGES
**AC 7 — command path**: The command is registered as `ddx doc audit` (`cli/cmd/doc.go:59`), but the acceptance criteria specifies `ddx docs audit` (or `ddx doctor docs`). If `doc` is the existing noun, this is arguably fine, but the AC should be reconciled. The `Aliases: []string{"integrity"}` alias doesn't cover the spec'd names.
**AC 7 — `--json` exit code**: `cli/cmd/doc.go:79-83` — when `--json` is set, the command encodes issues and returns `nil` unconditionally. The AC requires exit 1 when issues are present regardless of output format, so CI pipelines using `ddx doc audit --json` would incorrectly report success on a broken graph. The test `TestDocAuditCommand_JSONOutput` even documents this as intentional ("--json always succeeds (exit 0) so piping into jq works"), which contradicts the AC. Either the AC should be amended or the `--json` path should also return `ExitError` when issues exist (with a `--exit-zero` escape hatch for pipe-friendly use).

The test `TestDocAuditCommand_JSONOutput` even documents this as intentional ("--json always succeeds (exit 0) so piping into jq works"), which contradicts the AC. Either the AC should be amended or the `--json` path should also return `ExitError` when issues exist (with a `--exit-zero` escape hatch for pipe-friendly use).

`cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/graph.spec.ts:177` — AC 8 asks for a Playwright e2e on a fixture repo with one seeded duplicate-id pair and one missing-dep target. The test directly injects `duplicate_id` and `missing_dep` objects through a mocked `/graphql` response, so it does not exercise docgraph detection, GraphQL resolver plumbing, or project fixture routing end-to-end.
title: Document graph needs an integrity audit/repair surface for duplicate IDs, missing deps, and orphaned entries

acceptance:
## Mode 2: Explicit model (--model X)

1. ddx work --model qwen3.6 discovers providers advertising qwen3.6-35b-a3b (prefix match) and routes there without --harness or --provider.
2. When a provider advertises both qwen3.6 and qwen3.6-35b-a3b, --model qwen3.6 selects qwen3.6 (exact match wins over prefix).
3. When only prefix matches exist, shortest suffix wins: qwen3.6-27b (suffix len 4) beats qwen3.6-35b-a3b (suffix len 8).
4. On equal suffix length, catalog entries preferred over discovered-only, then alphabetical tiebreak.
5. Fuzzy match prints: model "qwen3.6" resolved to "qwen3.6-27b" (fuzzy match).
6. ddx work --model nonexistent-model-xyz errors: "model X is not in the catalog and no discovered provider serves it".
7. ddx work --model <resolved-but-no-provider> errors: "model X resolved but no available provider serves it".

## Mode 2 + provider modifier

8. ddx work --model qwen3.6 --provider vidar prefers vidar if it has a match; ignores vidar and routes to bragi if vidar lacks it.
9. ddx work --model qwen3.6 --provider claude treats claude as soft preference (not harness override) because --model is set.

## Mode 5: Provider targeting (--provider P alone)

10. ddx work --provider vidar discovers vidar's models and selects the best cataloged one.
11. ddx work --provider nonexistent errors: "unknown provider P".
12. ddx work --provider vidar when vidar is unreachable errors: "provider P has no available models".

## Mode 3: Profile routing exclusion

13. Profile routing (--profile cheap) does NOT auto-select uncataloged discovered models.

## Discovery infrastructure

14. Partial discovery failure: when some providers time out, routing proceeds with responding providers.
15. Discovery cache: repeated routing calls within 30s reuse cached /v1/models results.

## Unit tests (in cli/internal/agent/)

T1. Prefix matching: qwen3.6 resolves to qwen3.6-35b-a3b when only prefix match available.
T2. Exact beats prefix: qwen3.6 resolves to qwen3.6 (not qwen3.6-35b-a3b) when both exist.
T3. Shortest suffix tiebreak: qwen3.6-27b wins over qwen3.6-35b-a3b on equal prefix.
T4. Catalog preferred over discovered on equal suffix length.
T5. Provider affinity is soft: preferred provider ignored when it lacks the model.
T6. No match returns clear error (not silent fallback).
T7. Resolved model but no provider returns distinct error.
T8. Provider alone selects best cataloged model from that provider.
T9. Unknown provider returns clear error.
T10. Partial discovery failure: routing succeeds with subset of providers.
T11. Profile routing does not select uncataloged discovered models.

## Build gate

acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer opening a bead with a long id, the id truncates cleanly without overlapping any action button, the full id is discoverable on hover and copyable with one click, and the header buttons remain fully clickable.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. The bead-id span in `BeadDetail.svelte` uses `truncate min-w-0` (or equivalent) instead of `shrink-0`. The parent flex container keeps `min-w-0`.
2. The span has `title={bead.id}` so hovering reveals the full value.
3. A copy-to-clipboard icon button sits immediately after the id. Clicking copies the full id to the clipboard and surfaces a subtle confirmation (existing toast / inline check mark — whichever matches project conventions).
4. Action buttons (Claim/Unclaim, Edit, Delete, Close) remain fully visible and clickable regardless of id length. Status pill and owner chip remain visible; they may wrap below the id on narrow viewports but never overlap the action group.
5. Playwright e2e:
   - Seeds a bead with a 60-char id (e.g. `.execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-526efaf1`) and opens its detail panel.
   - Asserts the id element has a truncation class and that its rendered width is ≤ the parent container width minus the action-button group width.
   - Asserts the Delete button is clickable (`.click()` does not throw) and that its bounding rect does not intersect the id span's bounding rect.
   - Asserts hovering the id shows a tooltip (or reveals `title` attribute) with the full value, and clicking the copy button writes the full id to the clipboard.
6. Unit/visual: at `max-w-xl` width the header renders without horizontal scroll and without clipped action buttons for ids of length 8, 32, 60, and 120.
description:
## Observed On the bead detail panel, a long bead id overflows and visually overlaps the Claim/Unclaim/Edit/Delete/Close buttons on the right. Reporter example: `.execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-526efaf1` (~60 chars). ## Root cause `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/components/BeadDetail.svelte:211-221` — ```svelte
{bead.id} ← shrink-0 prevents truncation {bead.status} {#if bead.owner} @ {bead.owner} {/if}
[Claim/Unclaim] [Edit] [Delete] [Close]
``` The bead-id span has `shrink-0`, so it never truncates regardless of length. The outer panel is `max-w-xl` (576px). A 60-char monospace id occupies roughly 400-500px on its own; combined with the status pill, owner chip, and the action-button group (also `shrink-0`), it pushes past the available width. Flex layout collapses by overlapping rather than wrapping, so the id draws on top of the buttons. ## Proposed direction 1. **Replace `shrink-0` on the bead-id span with `truncate min-w-0`.** The parent `
` already establishes a shrinkable container. 2. **Add `title={bead.id}` so hovering shows the full value** (important — truncating monospace ids from the right loses the distinguishing suffix). 3. **Add a copy-to-clipboard icon button** next to the id so operators can grab the full value with one click when the id is truncated. Small icon button, no extra visual weight. 4. **Sanity check:** the reporter's example starts with `.` and has the shape of an execute-bead worktree name. Worth briefly checking whether something is producing such a value as a bead id (worktree name leaking into bead creation?) — but that's a separate investigation. The UI still needs to degrade gracefully regardless. ## Out of scope - Changing bead id generation or enforcing max length on ids. - Redesign of the detail-panel header layout.

description:
Wire /v1/models discovery from configured providers into BuildCandidatePlans
so that --model with an exact model ID routes to a discovered provider
without requiring --harness or --provider.

This is the foundation layer — fuzzy matching (bead B) and routing mode
cleanup (bead C) depend on this.

Implementation:
- Add a DiscoverModels function that loads agentconfig, probes each provider's
  /v1/models endpoint (reusing providerstatus.Probe), and returns a map of
  provider→[]modelID.
- Cache results per-process with a 30s TTL.
- On partial failure (some providers timeout), proceed with responding providers.
- In BuildCandidatePlans (or a new variant), when processing a ModelPin that
  no harness can serve via catalog, consult discovered models to find providers
  that advertise the pin. Create candidate plans for those provider+model pairs
  mapped to the appropriate harness surface.
- Remove or bypass ValidateOrphanModel for models found via discovery.
- Discovered-only models are assumed high-cost/low-performance for scoring
  (they don't win profile routing, only explicit --model).
title: Wire live provider discovery into candidate planning

acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer reviewing what my agents have done, I open the Executions view, filter to a specific bead or verdict or harness, click into one, and see the prompt it received, the verdict it produced, the session it corresponds to (with cost + tokens), and every tool call it made — all without leaving the browser.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Sidebar entry.** "Executions" added to the project sidebar between "Sessions" and "Personas" (or wherever fits the existing visual order — design call; captured in the PR description). Active-highlight works.

2. **List route.** `/executions` renders a paginated, filterable table with columns: timestamp, bead (id + title as link), harness/model, verdict (color-coded pass/block/error/other), duration, cost. Default sort: newest first. Filters: verdict, harness, bead, since/until date, free-text search over prompt first ~200 chars.

3. **Detail route.** `/executions/<id>` shows five tabs as described (Manifest, Prompt, Result, Session, Tool calls). Tab switching doesn't re-fetch already-loaded data.

4. **Tool calls pane.** Virtualized list. First 50 calls load with the page; subsequent pages load on scroll. Each entry shows tool name + collapsed input/output, expandable on click. Links to the raw agent-log file for operators who want to diff.

5. **GraphQL completeness.** `executions`, `execution`, `executionToolCalls` resolvers exist and pass feat-008-style integration tests. Schema documented in `schema.graphql`.

6. **Cross-links present.**
   - Bead detail renders an "Executions" section listing linked executions with verdict + timestamp, each clicking into the detail route.
   - Session detail shows "Execution" link when the session has one.
   - Commits page shows execution link when commit sha == execution.result_rev.

7. **Fixture + perf.**
   - Seeded fixture: ≥1,000 executions, ≥100 with seeded tool-call streams (≥50 calls each).
   - List view on this fixture: p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP.
   - Detail view (no tool calls): p95 ≤ 150ms HTTP.
   - Tool-call page 1 (first 50 entries): p95 ≤ 300ms HTTP.
   - If any target cannot be met in-place, raise DB-substrate decision with user before switching.

8. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds a project with 3 executions across 2 beads with mixed verdicts.
   - Navigates to `/executions`, asserts the table renders 3 rows and filter-by-verdict narrows correctly.
   - Clicks into one execution, asserts all 5 tabs exist and the Prompt tab shows the seeded prompt.
   - Opens the Tool calls tab, expands an entry, asserts input/output rendering.
   - Clicks the linked bead from the detail, asserts navigation to bead detail and that the Executions section on that bead lists this execution.

9. **Depends on.**
   - Soft dependency on `ddx-2ceb02fa` for the Session tab's cost/token numbers. If not yet landed, render "not available" for those fields and wire them in after.
   - Adjacent: `ddx-6904a90b` (billing mode) — Session tab's cost shows the correct bucket once that lands.
description:
## Observed / Motivation `.ddx/executions/-/{manifest.json, prompt.md, result.json}` bundles are already being produced per `ddx agent execute-bead` attempt — 469 of them on the current repo. They contain the canonical record of what an agent was asked to do and what verdict the attempt produced. **Nothing in the web UI surfaces them today.** A user who wants to ask "why did this bead end up in this state?" has to drop to the filesystem. Sessions (`sessions.jsonl` / sharded index per `ddx-2ceb02fa`) carry the harness/cost/token telemetry but not the verdict and not the bead linkage. Agent log streams (`agent-*.jsonl`) carry the tool-call trace but are scattered across hundreds of files. **Executions are the natural join** — they connect a bead, a session, a prompt, a verdict, and a tool-call stream. ## Ground truth Per-execution on disk: ``` .ddx/executions/-/ manifest.json { harness, model, base_rev, result_rev, verdict, bead_id, execution_dir } prompt.md the prompt that was sent result.json { verdict, rationale, ... } ``` Relations: - `manifest.bead_id` → bead - a session id is recorded in bead events (`kind:cost`, `kind:routing`) and in the agent-log stream; both can map back to an execution via correlation metadata - tool calls live in `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` (and `agent-s-*.jsonl`), keyed by session id ## Scope ### Part 1 — GraphQL surface - `executions(projectId, first, after, beadId?, since?, until?, verdict?, harness?)` — paginated list of execution bundles. - `execution(id)` — one bundle, fully materialized: manifest fields, prompt markdown, result JSON, linked bead (id + title), linked session (id + harness + model + cost + token totals), tool-call stream pointer. - `executionToolCalls(id, first, after)` — paginated tool-call stream for the execution, read lazily from the agent-log file. Matches the event shape used by the workers detail page. Executions source of truth: directory scan of `.ddx/executions/` filtered/paginated at the resolver. Shard discipline: follow the sessions-index monthly shard pattern (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) if listing all executions grows beyond tens of thousands — per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`, raise the storage decision with the user before switching substrate. ### Part 2 — Sidebar + routes - Add "Executions" to the project sidebar (`NavShell.svelte`). - Routes: - `/nodes//projects/

/executions` — list view with filters (verdict, harness, bead, date range, free-text over prompt). Table columns: timestamp, bead, harness/model, verdict (pass/block/error), duration, cost. - `/nodes//projects/

/executions/` — detail view with five tabs: **Manifest**, **Prompt** (rendered markdown), **Result** (rationale + raw JSON toggle), **Session** (harness, model, cost, token totals, link to `/sessions/`), **Tool calls** (virtualized lazy-scroll list of tool_use + tool_result pairs with expandable payloads). ### Part 3 — Cross-links (make the graph real) - **Bead detail:** new "Executions" section listing all executions for this bead with verdict + timestamp + link. Already have data: manifest.bead_id. - **Session detail:** new "Execution" link pointing to the execution this session ran for, when the session has a linked execution. - **Commits page:** already shows commits; if a commit was produced by an execution (result_rev == commit sha), surface the execution link. - **Graph/Docs pages:** out of scope for this bead. ### Part 4 — Perf - Fixture: ≥1,000 executions, per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`. Tool-call streams seeded for 10% of them. - Targets: list view p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP, detail view without tool calls p95 ≤ 150ms HTTP, tool-call page-1 (first 50) p95 ≤ 300ms HTTP. - If these can't be met with filesystem scan + in-memory filter, raise DB-substrate decision with user per standing directive. ## Out of scope - Writing new execution bundles (that's already happening). - Executions from other projects/nodes (keep per-project for v1). - Tool-call payload redaction / privacy. File a follow-up if relevant. - `.claude/worktrees/` worktree lifecycle tracking — covered by companion bead per user's split request.

acceptance:
1. Execute-loop cannot close a bead after a reviewer error, crash, or unparseable verdict. On any reviewer terminal failure the loop records a retryable failure event and leaves the bead open (not closed) with a clear status such as review_failed.
2. Repro fixture bead-closed-no-evidence.jsonl (from ddx-004eccbc) replays through the execute-loop close path and is rejected — the bead does not transition to closed.
3. Unit test covers the three terminal failure modes: reviewer exit non-zero, reviewer output empty, reviewer output unparseable.
4. No new invariant machinery here — the closure invariant bead (ddx-e30e60a9) owns cross-path enforcement; this bead owns the execute-loop symptom repro.
description:
Reporter: nexiq+axon 2026-04-18/19/20. Symptom 3 of review-malfunction report. Most damaging of the three report findings because it hides incomplete work.

Observed on axon `axon-c5cc071a` (RBAC field/row/entity spec, ~2000-word AC) 2026-04-19:

```json
{
  "id": "axon-c5cc071a",
  "claimed-at": "2026-04-19T23:20:45Z",
  "claimed-machine": "sindri",
  "claimed-pid": "3479503",
  "execute-loop-heartbeat-at": "2026-04-19T23:20:45.364638776Z",
  "updated_at": "2026-04-19T23:25:18.98959532Z",
  "status": "closed",
  "events": []
}
```

4m33s from claim to closed, `events: []`, no execution bundle under `.ddx/executions/` referencing the bead, no git commit referencing the id, no branch, no notes. Reopened 2026-04-20 as `axon-c5cc071a`. Evidence this is worker-side: no other actor claimed the bead, `claimed-pid` matches execute-loop PID pattern, `execute-loop-heartbeat-at` was set at claim — a manual `ddx bead update` would not leave `events: []` while setting `status: closed`.

Root-cause hypothesis from report: the worker's cleanup path closes the bead on primary-task success even when the reviewer sub-step errors out without returning a parseable verdict (e.g. context-overflow `n_keep: 1,868,459 >= n_ctx: 196,608` seen in reporter's LLM server logs, or any exception in the review state-machine path). A `bead.result` event that never gets emitted still leaves the bead closed if the exit path is not gated on verdict presence.

Closure contract should be: a bead transitions to `status=closed` only when a valid terminal review verdict (APPROVE / REQUEST_CHANGES / BLOCK / SKIP) is recorded alongside an execution bundle and at least one bead event. Worker crash, malformed reviewer output, or context-overflow during review must transition the bead back to `in_progress` with a retry marker — never to `closed`.

Prior related bead `ddx-b57be3ab` (closed) addressed BLOCK-with-empty-rationale but treated the rationale-missing case as `review_malfunction` without closing; this report shows a separate path where the closure itself happens despite reviewer failure.
title: exec: bead closes without valid reviewer verdict — silent false-closure hides unfinished work

events.body:
Bead ddx-75b2f5e9 is obsolete — do not execute. Close as superseded.

## What the bead asked for
Bump cli/go.mod from ddx-agent v0.3.14 to v0.4.0-pre.<sha>, the pre-release
tag that was intended to ship the new DdxAgent contract side-by-side with
legacy agentlib.Run. Pre-condition was agent-33ef96d9 ("tag agent v0.4.0-pre
in agent repo").

## Why it cannot be completed as specified
1. v0.4.0-pre was never published. `go list -m -versions
   github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` returns:
     v0.0.1 ... v0.3.14 v0.5.0
   There is no v0.4.0, no v0.4.0-pre, no v0.4.x at all. The planned
   intermediate release was skipped.

2. cli/go.mod is already past the target. Line 93 reads:
     github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent v0.5.0
   Commit d5ebab2b ("feat: cut DDx over to agent v0.5.0; drop local replace
   directive [ddx-5fdbc69d ddx-b34dd585]") migrated DDx directly from a
   local-replace scaffold (commit 38a0e62c, marked "partial:ddx-75b2f5e9") to
   the final v0.5.0 tag, bypassing the side-by-side pre-release entirely.

3. The parent epic ddx-ac5c7fdb and its sibling beads have already moved
   callers onto the v0.5.0 surface (e.g. ddx-f5a29d82 closed:
   cmd/agent_models.go now uses service.ListModels; ddx-f31ee3d2 closed:
   providerstatus/ deleted). Downgrading to a non-existent v0.4.0-pre would
   break those consumers.

## Current repo state
- cli/go.mod: github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent v0.5.0
- `go build ./...` from cli/ → clean (verified this pass)
- No local replace directive remains (dropped in d5ebab2b)

## Acceptance criteria status
- [ ] cli/go.mod shows agent v0.4.0-pre.<sha>
      IMPOSSIBLE — tag does not exist; repo is already at v0.5.0 which is
      strictly newer.
- [x] go mod tidy clean (already)
- [x] cd cli && make test passes (build verified; tests not re-run since no
      edit was made)
- [x] No new compile warnings (none introduced; legacy Deprecated surface
      was removed rather than left side-by-side)

## Recommendation
Close ddx-75b2f5e9 as superseded by d5ebab2b / ddx-5fdbc69d / ddx-b34dd585.
A stronger model re-running this bead will reach the same conclusion — the
side-by-side migration path this bead was designed around was abandoned in
favor of a direct v0.3.14 → v0.5.0 cutover.

acceptance: A worker claiming a bead updates execute-loop-heartbeat-at every HeartbeatInterval while the bead is in_progress. A second worker scanning the queue reclaims a bead whose heartbeat is older than HeartbeatTTL and resets it to open before claiming. A bead with no heartbeat and claimed-at older than ClaimTTL is also reclaimable. Conditional write prevents TOCTOU race between two workers both detecting the same stale bead. A structured log entry is written when a stale bead is reclaimed. Existing in_progress beads written before heartbeat was introduced are reclaimable via the ClaimTTL fallback. Tests: (1) worker goroutine killed mid-execution leaves heartbeat timestamp in the past; second worker reclaims it; (2) two workers simultaneously detect a stale bead; only one successfully reclaims it; (3) active worker heartbeat prevents reclaim by another worker.
description:
When an execute-loop worker crashes mid-execution, the bead it was working on is left in status=in_progress indefinitely. The only recovery today is manual: ddx bead unclaim <id>. This blocks the queue from making progress on that bead.

Root cause: cli/internal/bead/store.go Claim() writes claimed-at (RFC3339) and claimed-pid once at claim time and never updates them. There is no heartbeat, no TTL check, and ReadyExecution() in execute_bead_loop.go skips in_progress beads unconditionally.

## Required changes

### 1. Heartbeat writes (cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go)
While a bead is in_progress, the owning worker goroutine must periodically update a heartbeat field in bead.Extra. Recommended field name: execute-loop-heartbeat-at (RFC3339). Write interval: every 30s (configurable via HeartbeatInterval on the loop config struct). The heartbeat goroutine runs alongside the agent executor and is cancelled when the bead completes or errors.

### 2. Stale claim detection (cli/internal/bead/store.go or ReadyExecution)
ReadyExecution() currently skips all in_progress beads. Change it to also yield beads where:
  - status == in_progress
  - execute-loop-heartbeat-at is set AND is older than HeartbeatTTL (default: 3 * HeartbeatInterval = 90s)
  - OR claimed-at is older than ClaimTTL (default: 10 minutes) AND execute-loop-heartbeat-at is absent (supports beads claimed before heartbeat was introduced)

When a stale bead is yielded, the claiming worker should first reset status to open via a conditional write (matching the stale heartbeat timestamp to guard against a TOCTOU race) before claiming it as its own. If the conditional write fails, skip and continue scanning.

### 3. Stale-claim log entry
When a worker reclaims a stale bead, write a structured log entry to the worker log: { event: 'stale-claim-reclaimed', bead_id, previous_claimed_machine, previous_claimed_pid, previous_heartbeat_at, stale_for_seconds }.

### 4. Config surface
Expose HeartbeatInterval and ClaimTTL on the loop config. For --local workers, read from .ddx/execute-loop.toml if present. CLI flags: --heartbeat-interval (default 30s) and --claim-ttl (default 10m).

## Key files
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go — main loop, claim call at line 124, add heartbeat goroutine
- cli/internal/bead/store.go — Claim() at line 439, ReadyExecution() stale check
- cli/internal/bead/types.go — StatusInProgress constant, Extra field conventions
title: Worker heartbeat and stalled-bead TTL reclaim for execute-loop

acceptance: ddx persona list shows the 5 dropped personas with a (deprecated) suffix. ddx persona show reliability-guardian prints a warning banner to stderr pointing to the replacement or README. ddx agent run --persona reliability-guardian emits a stderr warning but still runs. Setting persona_bindings: code-reviewer: reliability-guardian in .ddx/config.yaml emits a stderr warning at load time. All deprecation warnings are stderr, not stdout. cd cli && go test ./... passes with updated persona tests.
description:
Before the 5 dropped personas are physically deleted (reliability-guardian, simplicity-architect, data-driven-optimizer, product-discovery-analyst, product-manager-minimalist), emit deprecation warnings so users who have them bound in .ddx/config.yaml get a clear signal to migrate.

Changes:
1. In the persona loader (cli/internal/persona/ or cli/cmd/persona.go), maintain an explicit deprecation list — persona names that still exist on disk but are scheduled for removal.
2. ddx persona list: mark deprecated personas with a (deprecated) suffix in the listing.
3. ddx persona show <name>: if <name> is deprecated, prepend a warning banner explaining the persona is scheduled for removal and pointing to a surviving persona (or to library/personas/README.md) as a migration target.
4. ddx agent run --persona <name>: if <name> is deprecated, emit a stderr warning (non-fatal) with the migration pointer. Do not error — users migrate on their own timeline.
5. Resolver for persona_bindings in .ddx/config.yaml: same stderr warning when a deprecated persona is bound. Do not error.

The warnings must be stderr, not stdout, so they don't poison piped output. The warning is non-fatal — the deprecated persona still loads and runs normally during the deprecation window.

One release later, the 'Delete dropped personas' bead removes the files; by then users have seen the warnings.

In-scope:
- cli/internal/persona/ (whichever file does loading/listing)
- cli/cmd/persona.go (the CLI subcommands)
- A small data structure (map or slice) of deprecated persona names with suggested replacements

Out-of-scope:
- Actually deleting the deprecated files (separate bead)
- Test fixtures — update as needed but keep minimal

Prerequisite: audit bead (ddx-5241bb19) so we know which bindings reference dropped personas.
title: Add deprecation warning for dropped personas in ddx persona list/show

acceptance:
1. Execute-bead first attempt uses cheap or standard with reasoning off by default.
2. Retryable first-pass failures trigger one smart retry and preserve both attempts as execution evidence.
3. Non-retryable setup/config/worktree failures do not trigger smart retry.
4. Cost cap applies across both attempts.
5. Tests cover retryable failure, non-retryable failure, and evidence preservation.
6. `go test ./...` passes in the DDx repo.
description:
Implement the consumer-side execution policy for bead work once DocumentDrivenDX/agent exposes the public `Reasoning` contract.

Agent-side tracking:
- agent repo epic: agent-c1e0b0af
- service contract bead: agent-b278411e
- execution-policy spec bead: agent-aa95cdd5

Required policy:
- First implementation attempt for a bead should use `profile=cheap` or `profile=standard` with `reasoning=off`.
- If the first pass fails due to model capability, reasoning quality, test failure after implementation attempt, or explicit agent failure, the orchestrator may retry with `profile=smart` and the smart catalog/default reasoning setting.
- Retrying smart must preserve first-pass logs/evidence and associate both attempts with the same bead.
- Do not retry smart for deterministic setup failures: dirty worktree conflict, missing dependencies, invalid bead metadata, unavailable repo checkout, command-not-found harness setup, or config parse errors.
- Cost cap and determinism controls must apply across both passes, not reset between attempts.

In-scope files:
- cli/internal/agent or execution-loop implementation files that select profile/harness/model for execute-bead
- execution evidence/session logging code needed to preserve both attempts
- tests for retryable vs non-retryable failure classification

Out-of-scope:
- Implementing the agent-side `Reasoning` API. This bead consumes it after the agent repo lands the contract.
- Provider-level reasoning/thinking serialization.
implementation_note: Implemented in e75a7766: (1) IsInfrastructureFailure pattern detection in escalation package — defers bead with retry-after on 502/503/connection-refused/no-such-host/context-deadline/command-not-found/auth/rate-limit failures rather than burning escalation budget. (2) CostCapTracker with --max-cost flag (default ) — accumulates across attempts, only counts billed providers (not subscription/local). NOT implemented (deferred to follow-on after agent ships Reasoning surface): per-tier Reasoning plumbing (cheap/standard → off, smart → default). Filed agent-4c482f60 for the agent-side routing intelligence (quota-trending bias + local/subscription preference within tier).
title: agent-run: retry bead execution with smart after cheap/standard reasoning-off failure

description:
The ddx-bead skill guides bead creation but underspecifies what makes a bead *execution-ready* for automated execute-bead runs. Observed during axon ADR-018 execute-loop driving: beads that technically met the current skill's requirements (title, AC, labels, description pointing at spec-id) still produced bad execute-bead outcomes because the description lacked the context the agent needed to ship correct code in one pass.

## Gaps in the current skill

1. **Description quality is underspecified.** The skill gives AC quality rules but the description field just says 'per SD-004 Section 2' is OK. In practice, agents need:
   - Exact file paths to touch (e.g., 'crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs')
   - Cross-bead context when dependencies are listed (e.g., 'A1 landed the credential_revocations table in crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs — query it via SELECT 1')
   - Inline spec excerpts, NOT just doc-id references. If the spec is ADR-018 §4's failure table, paste the table into the description. Don't say 'see ADR-018 §4'.
   - Files NOT to touch (scope negation)

2. **spec-id and the doc graph are unreliable for execution context.** The current skill implies spec-id linkage is sufficient. It is not — spec-id drives docgraph.BuildGraphWithConfig() which only resolves HELIX-shaped doc references. Generic beads on non-HELIX projects have no equivalent. AND even when resolution works, the agent only gets a file path, not the relevant section. The bead description is the only place that carries content the agent can rely on.

3. **No guidance on cross-bead context.** When bead B depends on bead A, B's description should describe what A landed (file paths, type names, key APIs) — not assume the agent will reopen A's history. Automated execute-bead does not have that context; each bead is read cold.

4. **No guidance on verification commands in AC.** AC items like 'unit tests green' are too vague. Should be 'cargo test -p axon-server green' or 'bun test passes in sdk/typescript'. Named commands.

5. **No guidance on bead size.** A bead should be one unit of testable work, sized so a single agent pass can complete it. Too small is overhead; too large defeats review. Heuristic: if the AC lists more than ~12 checkboxes, consider splitting.

6. **No guidance on incorporating review findings on reopen.** When a bead is reopened after a REQUEST_CHANGES review, the author should either (a) fold the review findings into the description for the next attempt to see, or (b) rely on the tool to thread review events into the prompt (a separate ddx bead — see ddx-a317208c). The skill is silent on this.

7. **No template for description.** A recommended description template (Scope / Files to touch / Cross-bead context / Verification / Quality bar) would make good beads cheaper to author.

## Scope

Update skills/ddx-bead/SKILL.md to add:

- A 'What makes a bead execution-ready' section that codifies the points above
- A 'Description template' with recommended sections: Scope, Files to touch, Files NOT to touch, Cross-bead context, Verification commands, Spec excerpts inline
- A 'Bead sizing' section with the ~12-AC heuristic
- A 'Review-driven reopen' section that tells authors to update the description in place when a bead is reopened after a review rejection
- A 'Common anti-patterns' section enumerating the mistakes observed during the axon execute-loop:
  - Listing doc IDs without inlining the relevant text
  - Not naming file paths in the description
  - AC items that are not verifiable by a named command
  - Depending on unmerged work without explaining what it landed

Keep the existing required-fields and label-conventions sections — they are correct.

## Context

Observed during axon ADR-018 execute-loop:
- Bead A1 was well-sized but initially had scope creep in its description (audit_retention_policies, which belonged to F1). Agent added the scope-creep table faithfully.
- Bead A2's initial description said 'UserIdentity { provider, subject, user_id }' — a typo carried over from an earlier ADR revision. Agent implemented 'subject' faithfully even though ADR-018 §3 said 'external_id'. The description was the contract the agent followed.
- Bead B1 is being re-authored right now with a richer description (inline ADR-018 §4 failure table, exact file paths, cross-bead context about A1 schema and A2 types) — this is the experiment to validate the authoring changes proposed in this bead.

## Acceptance

- [ ] skills/ddx-bead/SKILL.md gains 'What makes a bead execution-ready' section
- [ ] Description template added with the 6 recommended sections
- [ ] Bead sizing heuristic documented
- [ ] Review-driven reopen guidance added
- [ ] Common anti-patterns enumerated
- [ ] Skill still lints against any existing skill-format CI check
title: skill: improve ddx-bead SKILL.md with authoring discipline for execution-ready beads

The polling alternative (GET /api/projects/:project/workers/:id with recent_phases) must also be served.

acceptance: The design stack defines native `.agent/config.yaml` as the authoritative embedded-agent config source, documents precedence with environment / `.ddx` orchestration config / CLI overrides, and queues implementation work to remove DDx's lossy embedded-agent config mirror while preserving OpenRouter/native runtime compatibility
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: embedded-agent configuration ownership. Evidence covers ddx's partial `agent.agent_runner` mirror in `.ddx/config.yaml`, the native `ddx-agent` config model in `.agent/config.yaml`, and the need to support existing provider/backends such as OpenRouter without duplicating or losing configuration surface.
</context-digest>
Replace DDx's lossy embedded-agent config mirror with native `ddx-agent` configuration loading.

## Goals
- Make `.agent/config.yaml` the authoritative config source for the embedded `agent` harness
- Define precedence between native agent config, environment variables, DDx orchestration config, and CLI overrides
- Remove or narrow DDx-owned embedded-agent config fields that duplicate native runtime config
- Ensure DDx `agent run`, `execute-bead`, and `execute-loop` can consume existing OpenRouter/native agent setups without bespoke DDx translation
- Add regression coverage for embedded-agent config resolution and override semantics
title: Use native ddx-agent config for the embedded harness

acceptance: DDx docs or CLI help state which `execute-bead` statuses `execute-loop` consumes, that `success` closes the bead with recorded session/commit evidence, that non-success statuses leave the bead open and unclaimed, and what loop-level observability or event metadata operators can expect after each attempt.
description: The shipped `ddx agent execute-loop` close behavior is clear in code but not in user-facing docs. Operators should not need to read `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go` to learn that success closes a bead with evidence, non-success unclaims it, and loop events are recorded with the execute-bead status and preserve metadata when present. Clarify the documented result surface and the exact close/unclaim behavior for each status.

acceptance:
**User story:** As an operator, I can see every execute-bead worktree on my project, understand which ones are active vs completed vs abandoned, and clean up abandoned ones with confirmation without shelling out to git commands.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Inventory resolver.** `executeBeadWorktrees(projectId)` returns structured entries as described. Unit test covers: (a) active worktree with live worker, (b) completed worktree with matching execution bundle and head==result_rev, (c) abandoned worktree past the threshold, (d) orphan (path without git registration).

2. **State classification.** `state` is computed deterministically from inputs (workers list, execution bundles, git worktree list, filesystem stat, configured threshold). Classifier is a single pure function with table-driven tests covering all four states and at least two edge cases per state.

3. **UI — list view.** `/worktrees` route renders a table with columns path/branch/bead/execution/state/created/last-activity/disk. Sortable by any column. Filters: state, bead. Default sort: newest `last activity` first.

4. **UI — actions.** Per-row: Remove (confirm-gated), Force-remove (for orphans, extra-confirm-gated with typed confirmation of the path). Both actions call through to git + filesystem via a mutation; neither is performed client-side.

5. **CLI — prune.** `ddx server state worktree-prune [--dry-run] [--project <path>]` lists abandoned+orphan worktrees and prompts for removal (unless `--yes`). Dry-run prints the list without touching anything. Integration test runs against a fixture repo with one of each state and asserts only the expected ones are targeted.

6. **Sweep + notice.** Server background sweep runs at a configurable interval (default 15m), logs a summary, and exposes a GraphQL counter (`executeBeadWorktreeCounters { total, active, abandoned, orphan }`) for dashboard rollup. Sweep never deletes unless `worktrees.auto_prune: true` is set in config.

7. **Cross-links.**
   - From a worktree row, click execution → navigates to `/executions/<id>` (depends on `ddx-726bc5f2`).
   - From an execution detail page, a "Worktree" section shows the worktree (if still present) with the same state badge.

8. **Fixture + perf.** Per standing directive in `ddx-9ce6842a`, seed a fixture with ≥100 worktrees (mix of states). List view p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP. This is small-n territory; should be well within filesystem-scan limits, no DB substrate expected.

9. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds fixture with one worktree of each of the four states.
   - Navigates to `/worktrees`, asserts all four rows with correct state badges.
   - Clicks Remove on an abandoned worktree, confirms, asserts the row disappears within 2s and the worktree is gone from `git worktree list`.
   - Attempts Remove on an active worktree, asserts the confirm dialog warns and the action is gated.

10. **Path convention decision note.** Before Part 4 lands, a one-page note in `docs/helix/02-design/` records the canonical location choice and the migration plan. Parts 1-3 may ship without this note; Part 4 requires it.

11. **Cross-references.** Notes link to: `ddx-12cae4dd` (documents noise), `ddx-71fbe968` (long id overlap — same worktree-name leak into UI), `ddx-726bc5f2` (executions UI — worktrees complement it), `ddx-15f7ee0b` (project registry cleanup pattern reused here).
description:
## Observed `git worktree list` on the current ddx repo shows **39 worktrees**: - 1 main worktree (the clone itself). - ~37 under `.claude/worktrees/agent-/` from execute-bead runs. Branch names `worktree-agent-`. - At least 1 under `/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx---` (detached HEAD) — different path convention, different semantics. There is no UI visibility into any of this. A user cannot see which worktrees exist, what bead each one belongs to, whether each is active/abandoned/completed, or clean them up short of `git worktree prune` + `rm -rf`. The `.claude/worktrees/` entries also leak into other surfaces (already tracked): - Documents view noise (`ddx-12cae4dd`). - One was surviving in the project registry cleanup earlier today (`proj-4c1ca90e: agent-a7bf6d44`). ## Why the two path conventions? - `.claude/worktrees/agent-` — created with a branch (`worktree-agent-`), likely by interactive execute-bead runs or an earlier worktree convention. - `/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx---` — detached HEAD, under `/tmp`, convention that ships with current execute-bead (this is what's producing the long "bead id"-shaped names that leaked into UI in `ddx-71fbe968`). Two conventions is itself a smell: inconsistent path discipline makes cleanup harder and makes cross-referencing an execution bundle → worktree unreliable. ## What's needed ### Part 1 — Inventory resolver - GraphQL: `executeBeadWorktrees(projectId)` → list of `{path, branch, beadId?, executionId?, baseRev, headRev, state, createdAt, lastActivityAt}`. - Source: `git worktree list --porcelain` + filesystem stat + manifest lookup for executions whose `execution_dir` matches or whose path correlates. - `state` ∈ `{active, completed, abandoned, orphan}`: - **active** — an execute-bead session is currently running against this worktree (cross-reference workers / sessions). - **completed** — associated execution bundle exists with a verdict; head matches `result_rev`. - **abandoned** — no recent activity (configurable, default 24h), no matching running worker, but worktree still present. - **orphan** — path on disk but `git worktree list` does not know about it (or vice versa). ### Part 2 — Sidebar + route - New "Worktrees" view, or slot this into the Executions detail (`ddx-726bc5f2`) as a sidebar panel since every worktree is for some execution. Design call; recommend a dedicated view so orphans (which have no execution) are still visible. - Route: `/nodes//projects/

/worktrees`. - Columns: path, branch, bead (link), execution (link), state, created, last activity, disk usage. - Row actions: **Open execution**, **Remove worktree** (confirm-gated; calls `git worktree remove` and cleans up `.claude/worktrees/`), **Force remove** (for orphans; `-f`). ### Part 3 — Policy + cleanup - Background sweep (server-side, already has a sweep pattern from project registry): every N minutes, list worktrees, detect abandoned state, emit a notice (but **never auto-delete** — user-confirmed cleanup only by default). - `ddx server state worktree-prune [--dry-run]` CLI command that enumerates and optionally removes abandoned worktrees. Same deletion path as the UI action. - Config: `.ddx/config.yaml: worktrees.abandoned_after: 24h`, `worktrees.auto_prune: false`. Opt-in auto-prune for teams that want it. ### Part 4 — Path convention unification - Decide a single canonical location for execute-bead worktrees. Recommend `.claude/worktrees/--/` or a sibling `.ddx/worktrees/` — **pick one and stick with it**. - Migrate existing paths lazily: on first sweep after upgrade, record the path as-is and leave it; new worktrees use the new convention. Old ones phase out as they complete or get cleaned. - This is a separable sub-effort; acceptable to land Parts 1-3 against the current mixed reality and do Part 4 afterward. ## Out of scope - Changing how execute-bead creates worktrees (beyond the canonical-location choice in Part 4). - Documents-view noise — covered by `ddx-12cae4dd`. - Long-id display overlap — covered by `ddx-71fbe968`.

acceptance: (1) cli/go.mod pins the agent release containing agent-32e8ff5e, -f0bc5467, -b896b406, -bcd2ff47, -1a0c0502, -81830379. (2) Consumer-side stand-ins removed: no local ServiceEvent payload struct copies, no provider-native file parsers, no GlobalProviderHealth shadow, no ~/.config/agent/models.yaml reads, no RunViaService virtual/script carve-out. (3) Reviewer verdict extraction consumes normalized final text only; no raw stream frame inspection. (4) ddx work --once drains one ready bead end-to-end with the new surface. (5) go -C cli build ./... and go -C cli test ./... green.
description:
Future ddx-agent release (post-v0.7.0; v0.8.0 shipped 2026-04-20 with all six upstream deliverables). Module bump landed in 05dfbbc2.

## Progress

- ✅ **agent-32e8ff5e normalizer wiring** (ba3f8125): FinalText from upstream ServiceFinalData flows through drainServiceEvents into result.Output. Reviewer verdict extractor now consumes normalized text; per-harness extractors (extractOutputCodex/Claude/PiGemini in runner.go) are legacy-only paths. Completes the scope:partial clause of ddx-f7ae036f.
- ✅ **agent-f0bc5467 typed event payloads** (fa31b917): serviceFinalData, serviceFinalUsage, serviceRoutingActual, serviceToolCallData, serviceToolResultData are now type aliases to upstream agentlib types. -47 lines; eliminates the shadow-struct maintenance burden.
- ✅ **agent-81830379 virtual/script closed as won't-fix** (de77aa41): DDx's virtual and script harnesses are different products from upstream's same-named stubs — DDx is a content-addressed record/replay dictionary (PromptHash → .ddx/agent-dictionary/<hash>.json) + NormalizePrompt regex substitutions + --record mode; upstream is per-call metadata stuffing. Renamed runDDxOnlyHarnessViaRunner → runFixtureHarnessViaRunner with clarifying comment.

## Remaining streams

### Atomic preferred: parser-removal + GlobalProviderHealth removal (agent-b896b406 + agent-bcd2ff47)

These two streams can land together in one PR — that is the cleanest option because the alternative (parsers first, then GlobalProviderHealth) introduces a window where operator-facing surfaces (doctor, /api/providers, MCP tools, agent usage) read from different sources than the execute-loop. If operational constraints force a split, land parsers first; shipping GlobalProviderHealth removal first would leave execute-loop reading svc.RecordRouteAttempt / RouteStatus while doctor / REST / MCP still read local parser+cache state.

**Files to delete / rewrite** (all must land together so doctor, REST, MCP, and usage stay consistent):

- `cli/internal/agent/session_evidence.go` (357 LOC): Codex native session JSONL, Claude stats/cache, OpenRouter /auth/key, LM Studio /v1/models.
- `cli/internal/agent/routing_signals.go` (~450 LOC Runner-method variant; loadCodex/Claude/LMStudio helpers) — parallel to session_evidence.go.
- `cli/internal/agent/routing_signal_adapters.go`, `routing_signal_http.go`.
- `cli/internal/agent/claude_quota_cache.go` — the entire `ReadClaudeQuotaSnapshot` / `WriteClaudeQuotaSnapshot` / `resolveClaudeQuotaSnapshotPath` cache subsystem.
- `cli/internal/agent/state.go` — `ReadClaudeQuotaRoutingDecision` and `ClaudeQuotaDecision` type (cache read surface).
- `cli/internal/escalation/GlobalProviderHealth` singleton + ProviderHealthTracker.
- Call sites: workers.go:511 (IsHealthy) / :593 (Mark); agent_cmd.go:1702 (IsHealthy) / :1791 (Mark); agent_cmd.go:680 (`routing_signal.CurrentQuota.State == "blocked"`); agent_cmd.go:704 (`ReadClaudeQuotaRoutingDecision`).

**Migration target**: `svc.ListHarnesses` (HarnessInfo.Quota / UsageWindows / Account), `svc.ListProviders` (ProviderInfo.EndpointStatus), `svc.RouteStatus` (RouteStatusReport with RouteCandidateStatus.Healthy), `svc.RecordRouteAttempt` (RouteAttempt with status/reason).

**Vocabulary translation shim** in buildProviderSummary / buildProviderDetail: maps current "fresh/stale" to upstream's "ok/stale/unavailable". Not an upstream API change.

**Consumers of the write-side cache** (removed when parsers drop — no JSON Schema contract to break, but behavior changes observable):

- `ddx agent doctor --routing --json` (state.claude_quota_decision field disappears).
- `ddx agent doctor --routing` human text (claude-quota-cache line).
- `ProbeHarnessState` via state.go.
- `ddx agent usage` enrichment.
- REST `/api/providers`.
- MCP `ddx_provider_list` / `ddx_provider_show`.

Not affected: SvelteKit frontend providers page queries GraphQL `providerStatuses` only (name / type / baseURL / model / status / modelCount / isDefault / cooldownUntil) — no cache fields.

### agent-1a0c0502 models.yaml parser removal (independent; any order)

- `cli/internal/agent/agent_catalog_shared.go` reads ~/.config/agent/models.yaml directly and hard-codes surface translations ("agent.openai" → "embedded-openai"). Migrate to `svc.ListProfiles` / `svc.ResolveProfile` / `svc.ProfileAliases`.

## Blocks

- ddx-ac5c7fdb (thin-consumer epic)
- ddx-c0f6b19c, ddx-ea7f041c, ddx-f0cab7b3, ddx-4e3e149f, ddx-1cc9933c (Phase 5 deletes)
- ddx-bbb65768 (profile-automation default)
- ddx-4b59b816 (cost-tiered measurement epic)

<acceptance-check>

(2) All consumer-side stand-ins removed:

    - Provider-native parsers: session_evidence.go, routing_signals.go, routing_signal_adapters.go, routing_signal_http.go, claude_quota_cache.go all deleted (6fbe1a9d).

acceptance: execute-bead resolves applicable graph-authored execution documents from the governing snapshot, runs required post-run gates before landing, preserves iterations when required executions or ratchets fail, records gate outcomes through existing result/evidence surfaces, and automated coverage proves merge-by-default still works when no required gates fail
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead post-run gate enforcement. Evidence covers cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go landing behavior, FEAT-006's required execution and ratchet language, FEAT-010 execute-bead compatibility requirements, and the need to consume authored execution documents rather than hidden workflow policy.
</context-digest>
Implement the documented post-run merge-gate behavior in execute-bead.

## Goals
- Resolve applicable graph-authored execution documents from the governing snapshot used for the iteration
- Run the required execution documents after the agent completes and before land
- Preserve instead of land when a required execution fails or a ratchet gate blocks the iteration
- Record gate outcomes in the execute-bead result/evidence surface and through the existing exec history/runtime surfaces
- Add automated coverage for merge-by-default, required gate failure, and successful gate-passing land behavior
notes: evaluateRequiredGates() is implemented and called. Gap: automated test coverage for the gate-fail → preserve path and for merge-by-default when no gates fail. Focus on tests, not reimplementation.
title: Implement execute-bead required execution gate enforcement

acceptance: Web UI route /nodes/:nodeId/agents (or /workers) lists all workers from /api/agent/workers with auto-refresh every ~2s. A worker row links to /nodes/:nodeId/agents/:workerId with three panels: prompt, log stream (structured events rendered with FormatSessionLogLines symbols), utilization (tokens/cost/duration/attempts). Both pages work against the alpha14 server without any additional backend work beyond optionally exposing the prompt content. Playwright test covers: list-shows-running-worker, list-refreshes-on-state-change, detail-renders-prompt-log-and-utilization.
description:
The server knows everything about running workers (state, harness, model, bead, attempts, prompt file, structured event log, token/cost/duration) via /api/agent/workers endpoints but the web UI has no live workers view. When an operator selects the eitri node they should land on (or reach in one click) an Agents page that lists every running worker from /api/agent/workers with bead-id, harness, model, state, elapsed, and latest status line. Clicking a row should open a worker detail page showing: (1) the rendered bead prompt from .ddx/executions/<attempt>/prompt.md, (2) the structured session log stream from .ddx/agent-logs/agent-loop-<session>.jsonl rendered via FormatSessionLogLines (so tool calls, turn counts, and timings are readable), (3) a session utilization panel with input/output/total tokens, cost-if-available, duration, attempts and successes/failures from the worker record, and (4) the base-rev and result-rev link once the worker has made progress. Polling at ~2s is fine for v1; no WebSocket required.

Backing endpoints that already exist:
- GET /api/agent/workers -> list
- GET /api/agent/workers/:id -> detail
- GET /api/agent/workers/:id/log -> stdout+stderr streams

Missing backing endpoints (if not already there): GET /api/agent/workers/:id/prompt to serve prompt.md content without requiring the UI to know the execution bundle path; optionally /api/agent/workers/:id/events to serve the structured JSONL stream if /log doesn't already cover it.
title: Dashboard UI: live workers page with per-worker prompt, log, and utilization detail

acceptance: A shipped single-project loop monitors one project for execution-ready beads, validates the selected candidate with the generic execution-readiness surface, executes it via ddx agent execute-bead, runs the required post-run checks, preserves failed or conflicted attempts without mutating the target branch, lands successful attempts by rebase plus fast-forward, resets to the updated branch tip, and continues scanning; automated coverage exercises at least success, preserved failure, land-conflict, and no-ready-work cases
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: single-project execute-bead automation. Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, SD-012, API-001, the closed single-project loop design bead ddx-8bb9af7c, and the requirement to ship a tested single-project execution loop before multi-project or multi-host expansion.
</context-digest>
Implement the first shipped single-project loop that continuously works one project's executable-bead queue using the explicit execute-bead contract.

## Scope
- Monitor one project queue for execution-ready beads using the documented queue-ordering contract and the generic execution validator as a gate
- Run `ddx agent execute-bead` for the selected bead from the project root; do not introduce a second worktree lifecycle around the command
- Run required post-run validity checks and execution checks before classifying the attempt
- On execution failure, validation failure, or land conflict, preserve the iteration and evidence exactly as the execute-bead contract specifies, leave the target branch untouched, record the non-landed result, and continue scanning
- On success, land by rebase plus fast-forward, reset loop state to the updated branch tip, and continue scanning for the next executable bead
- Support a minimal control surface suitable for standalone use now and ddx-server hosting later without pulling in multi-project scheduling

## Boundaries
- Single-project only for the first release
- No inline PTY policy or hidden HELIX scheduling heuristics
- Multi-project worker pools and multi-host control planes remain follow-on work
title: implement: single-project execute-bead loop

acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer on the plugins page, I want clicking Update or Install to give me continuous feedback until the operation completes, and I want the card to reflect the new installed state automatically — without a page reload.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Per-card in-flight tracking.** Clicking Update/Install records the workerId under the plugin's name in a `Map<string, string>`. The same plugin cannot be dispatched again while it is in flight (button disabled; tooltip shows the worker link). A different plugin can be dispatched concurrently and tracks its own worker.

2. **Worker-driven refresh.** For each in-flight plugin, the page subscribes to that worker's progress via the existing `subscribeWorkerProgress` channel. On a terminal event, the page refetches the plugins list and clears that plugin's entry from the in-flight map. Within 2s of worker terminal event, the card reflects the new status (e.g., `update-available` → `installed`, new `installedVersion`).

3. **Fallback polling.** If no terminal event arrives within 30s of dispatch, the page begins polling `plugins { name status installedVersion }` every 2s until the card's status or installedVersion changes, then stops. Integration test simulates a stream dropout and asserts the card eventually refreshes via the poll path.

4. **In-flight visual state.** While a card is in flight, its status badge is replaced by a spinner + "Updating…" or "Installing…" text. The action button is disabled with a tooltip pointing to the worker.

5. **Failure surfacing.** If the worker terminates in a failure phase, the card shows an error badge + tooltip "Update failed — view worker" linking to the worker page. The card's status/version remains whatever it was before dispatch (no false success). The global `dispatchError` stays for synchronous mutation errors only.

6. **Concurrent dispatch.** Dispatching Update on two different cards at once shows two active worker links (a row or small stack in the header instead of the single slot), and each card tracks its own state. Playwright asserts.

7. **No extra refetch overhead.** Refetch is scoped to the plugins list, not the full page data. Manual verification is enough; no specific perf test required.

8. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds a fixture with one outdated plugin.
   - Clicks Update.
   - Asserts the card enters the in-flight visual state and the action button is disabled.
   - Simulates the worker completing successfully.
   - Asserts within 2s the card shows `installed` and a newer `installedVersion`.
   - Runs a second scenario where the simulated worker fails; asserts the failure badge and that status stays `update-available`.

9. **No regressions.** Install-from-registry flow (the ConfirmDialog path) adopts the same in-flight tracking; existing Playwright install flow still passes.
description:
## Observed

On `/nodes/.../projects/.../plugins`, clicking Update on the outdated `helix` card:

1. Fires the mutation — works.
2. Shows a link to the dispatched worker — helpful.
3. Worker completes and the plugin is actually updated on disk — verified.
4. **Card still shows `update-available`.** The UI never closes the loop.

The user has no in-UI signal that the update succeeded. They must reload the page (or navigate away and back) to see the new state. Worse, nothing prevents them from clicking the button again during the gap — firing a second update.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/plugins/+page.svelte:74-89` —

```js
async function dispatchPlugin(name, action, scope = 'project') {
  const result = await client.request(PLUGIN_DISPATCH_MUTATION, { name, action, scope });
  workerId = result.pluginDispatch.id;
}
```

That is the entire post-dispatch behavior. `data.plugins` is the snapshot from `+page.ts` at load time. There is no:

- subscription to the dispatched worker's progress,
- polling of the plugin list,
- refetch on worker terminal event,
- optimistic card state while the worker runs,
- per-card in-flight tracking (only a single `workerId: string | null` at module scope, which means dispatching a second plugin overwrites the first worker link).

The workers layout already has `subscribeWorkerProgress(workerID, cb)` (`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte:22-30`) — the plugins page just doesn't use it.

## Proposed direction

Two changes, smallest first:

### Fix A — Close the loop on the dispatched worker.

- Per-card in-flight tracking: replace the singleton `workerId` with a `Map<pluginName, workerId>`. Each card shows its own worker link + in-flight state; clicking Install/Update is disabled while that plugin is in flight.
- Subscribe to `subscribeWorkerProgress` for each in-flight plugin. On a terminal phase event (`completed`, `failed`, etc. — whatever the existing worker event vocabulary uses), refetch the plugins list and clear that plugin's in-flight entry.
- If the existing worker-progress stream doesn't emit a terminal event with enough detail, fall back to polling `plugins { name status installedVersion }` every 2s while any card is in flight, stopping when none are.

### Fix B — Optimistic card state (optional polish).

- While a card is in-flight, show "Updating…" / "Installing…" with a spinner in place of the status badge, and disable the action button. This is orthogonal to Fix A and can ship with it.

### Fix C — Persistent failure surfacing.

- Currently `dispatchError` is a single string. If the worker terminates with a failure phase, surface that per-card (error badge + tooltip with detail link to the worker page) rather than relying on `dispatchError` which only covers the synchronous mutation error.

## Out of scope

- Designing new worker terminal events. Use whatever the workers layout already consumes.
- Cancelling an in-flight plugin update — follow-up if needed.
- Bulk update ("update all outdated"). Follow-up.

acceptance: (1) New cli/internal/server/graphql/cursors.go with encodeStableCursor and decodeStableCursor helpers that encode/decode a stable string key (not slice index). (2) resolver_node.go and resolver_documents.go both use the new helpers; old encodeCursor(i int) is deleted. (3) NewResolver constructor exists, rejects nil State and empty WorkingDir with a clear error. All "if r.State == nil" / "if r.WorkingDir == empty" guards removed from individual resolvers. (4) ProjectSnapshot removed — resolver_node.go returns docgraph or server domain types directly, matching the pattern in resolver_documents.go. (5) All existing integration tests (TestGraphQLNodeInfo/TestGraphQLProjects/TestGraphQLNodeByProjectID/TestGraphQLDocuments/TestGraphQLDocGraph) still pass. (6) go build ./... clean.
description:
Follow-up from Stage 2.4 opus review: the resolver pattern established in Stage 2.2 (node/projects) and extended in Stage 2.4 (documents/docGraph) has 3 load-bearing shape issues that should be fixed before Stage 2.5+ compounds them across 7 more resolver beads.

1. Stable-key cursors. Current: resolver_node.go defines encodeCursor(i int) / decodeCursor(s string) that base64-wrap "cursor:<slice-index>". Slice-index cursors break when the underlying collection reorders between pages. Fine for projects (rarely mutate) but broken for beads, documents, commits, sessions, workers (mutated or resorted per query). Replace with ID-based or (sort-key, ID)-based stable cursors. New file: cli/internal/server/graphql/cursors.go with encodeStableCursor(id string) / decodeStableCursor(s string).

2. Constructor-enforced non-nil state. Current: every resolver has "if r.State == nil { return }" or "if r.WorkingDir == empty { return }" early returns. As Stage 2 grows this becomes 50+ copy-pasted guards. Replace with a NewResolver(state StateProvider, workingDir string) *Resolver constructor that rejects nil/empty and returns an error, drop the runtime checks.

3. Snapshot struct vs direct domain types. Current inconsistency: resolver_node.go uses ProjectSnapshot / NodeStateSnapshot (adapter structs) while resolver_documents.go uses docgraph.Document directly. Pick ONE approach and migrate the other. Recommendation: direct domain types (simpler, less boilerplate). Migrate resolver_node.go to drop ProjectSnapshot.

Not in scope: the prefix-registry for node(id) dispatch (concern 5 from the Stage 2.3 note) — keep that for when bead/doc/worker prefixes are added. Not in scope: splitting StateProvider into sub-interfaces (concern 1) — decide that lazily when the interface actually grows.

File: cli/internal/server/graphql/{cursors.go,resolver.go,resolver_node.go,resolver_documents.go,state_graphql.go}

Stage 2.3 opus review flagged two items that belong in this refactor bead alongside the original 3:

ADDITION 4: node(id) must resolve bead- prefixes.
Current state: cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_node.go:74-88 handles node-/proj- via strings.HasPrefix if-chain. The bead- prefix is not handled. Node(id: "bead-xxx") returns (nil, nil) even though beads exist as first-class objects in the schema.
Required fix: add "bead-" branch (or migrate the dispatch to a prefix registry per the original shape concern 5).

ADDITION 5: Strengthen TestGraphQLBeadsPagination to actually cover cursor stability.
Current state: graphql_beads_test.go creates 3 beads, queries first 2, queries next 1, asserts no duplicates. That test passes trivially on any cursor implementation including broken slice-index.
Required fix: create >= 10 beads, query first 5, mutate the dataset (delete 1 bead at index 2, create 1 new bead), query next page via the cursor from step 1, assert no duplicates AND no skipped beads. This test fails on slice-index cursors and passes on stable-key cursors — exactly the property we want to lock in.

Migrate cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go TestExecuteBeadHeadRevFailure() from fakeExecuteBeadGit + fakeAgentRunner to real git + script harness.

Pattern:
- Use cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go newScriptHarnessRepo(t, beadCount) to build an isolated repo with real git.
- Use runGitInteg (from cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go) for any supplemental git invocations, so GIT_* env is scrubbed.
- Replace fakeExecuteBeadGit with the RealLandingGitOps flow used in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land_test.go.
- Replace fakeAgentRunner with the script harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go) driven by a per-attempt directive file. When the test needs to simulate 'agent emits no commits' or 'agent emits a dirty worktree', encode that as script directives rather than a canned Result struct.
- Preserve every assertion — the point is that the test now exercises real git + real merge/preserve semantics, not that the surface shrinks.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadHeadRevFailure$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.

acceptance: Both reasoningByteLimit and reasoningStallTimeout are configurable via ddx-agent config.yaml with raised defaults (>=256KB, >=300s). Setting either to 0 disables the check. Error messages include the model name and current threshold. Existing tests updated, new tests cover custom thresholds and disabled checks.
description:
The ddx-agent stream consumer has two hardcoded constants that abort model responses prematurely:

- `reasoningByteLimit = 32 * 1024` (32KB) — aborts if pure reasoning_content exceeds 32K chars before any content delta
- `reasoningStallTimeout = 120 * time.Second` — aborts if only reasoning tokens arrive for 2 minutes

Both are in `stream_consume.go` (github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent v0.3.5, lines 13 and 18).

## Observed
- google/gemma-4-31b on bragi: reasoning overflow at 32KB on ddx-e14c86ef (P3, small bead)
- qwen3.5-27b on bragi: same reasoning stall on the same bead
- Both models use extended thinking by default; coding tasks routinely produce >32KB of reasoning before the first content token
- qwen3-coder-next on vidar does NOT hit this (different reasoning behavior — produces content tokens earlier)

The 120s stall timeout is particularly problematic: local models generating at 5-15 tok/s can easily spend 2+ minutes reasoning through a complex prompt before producing output. This is normal behavior, not a stall.

## Required changes
1. Make both thresholds configurable via ddx-agent config.yaml (e.g. `reasoning_byte_limit`, `reasoning_stall_timeout`)
2. Raise defaults: at least 256KB byte limit and 300s stall timeout
3. Allow 0 = unlimited for both (same pattern as max_iterations)
4. When the threshold fires, include the model name and configured limit in the error message so operators can tune

## Scope
- stream_consume.go: replace const with config-driven values
- config.go: add fields to Config struct
- Defaults() should set the raised defaults
- Tests: verify configurable thresholds, verify 0 = unlimited
title: Raise and make configurable reasoning stall/overflow thresholds in ddx-agent
