acceptance:
- [ ] Runner struct + NewRunner deleted from cli/internal/agent/runner.go (or runner.go deleted entirely)
- [ ] Zero references to agent.Runner / agent.NewRunner / Runner.Run* in cli/
- [ ] All listed callers migrated
- [ ] DDx builds + tests pass
- [ ] After this lands, the 5 file-delete beads (ddx-f0cab7b3, ddx-ea7f041c, ddx-70c48576, ddx-1bf6c0df, ddx-2912e7cd) execute cleanly
description:
Discovered while preparing the file-delete beads (ddx-f0cab7b3, ddx-ea7f041c, ddx-70c48576, ddx-1bf6c0df, ddx-2912e7cd) — the Runner struct in cli/internal/agent/runner.go is widely used across DDx and references types from the to-be-deleted files (Registry, DiscoveryResult, recordRoutingOutcome, etc.).

Original bead breakdown assumed deleting the underlying production files would cascade cleanly. It does not — Runner.Run, Runner.ValidateOrphanModel, Runner.RunCompare/RunQuorum/RunBenchmark, etc. all use those types.

Callers found via grep (cli/ directory):
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go (Runner construction + execute path)
- cli/cmd/agent_usage.go (Runner.* method calls)
- cli/internal/server/workers.go (Runner-based execute-loop)
- cli/internal/server/server.go (Runner field on server struct)
- cli/internal/server/providers.go (Runner-based provider probing)
- cli/internal/server/providers_test.go + workers_test.go (test scaffolding)
- cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_test.go + agent_runner_service_test.go (test scaffolding)
- cli/internal/agent/compare_adapter.go (RunCompare/RunQuorum/RunBenchmark shim from ddx-1d2c2e7f)
- cli/internal/agent/omlx_e2e_test.go
- cli/internal/agent/runner_context_test.go + script_test.go

The right fix: replace every Runner consumer with direct service.Execute / service.RouteStatus / service.ListX calls. Delete Runner struct. Then the 5 file-delete beads become trivial.

This is a substantial migration that should be sliced into smaller beads (per consumer file or coherent file group). Recommended approach:
  1. Replace server-side Runner usage (workers.go, providers.go, server.go) — these are the highest-impact consumers
  2. Replace agent_cmd.go + agent_usage.go (user-facing CLI commands)
  3. Migrate the test scaffolding files
  4. Delete Runner struct + its dependencies (the original 5 file-delete beads)
  5. Delete compare_adapter.go shim (no longer needed once Runner is gone)

acceptance:
- [ ] New function gitPushLanded(projectRoot, defaultBranch) called after successful CloseWithEvidence in execute_bead_loop.go
- [ ] Push uses --ff-only; never --force or --force-with-lease
- [ ] Push rejection triggers land_conflict + unclaim + refs/ddx/iterations/ preservation + stop-the-loop
- [ ] Network failure triggers 'push_pending' attempt marker but does NOT fail the bead; subsequent workers on the same branch see the pending state and halt
- [ ] Unit tests: successful push, rejected push, network failure
- [ ] Integration test: two workers racing, one wins the push, the other is rejected and unclaims its bead
- [ ] Integration test: repeated loops push each successful bead immediately (origin advances after every success)
- [ ] Docs updated: success path includes push; land_conflict path is the canonical 'two machines raced' outcome
description:
Second child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. When a worker successfully lands a bead (status = success), the new commit sits on the local branch forever. There is no git push. The source in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_commit.go has ZERO references to push/fetch/origin (grep verified). Result: successful work on one machine is invisible to every other machine, and the local branch grows indefinitely with unsynced commits.

## Evidence

cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_commit.go defines the commit step but never calls git push. The only mention of 'git' in that file is a comment about trailers.

cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go at the success branch (around line 343):

    if report.Status == ExecuteBeadStatusSuccess {
        if err := w.Store.CloseWithEvidence(candidate.ID, report.SessionID, report.ResultRev); err != nil {
            return result, err
        }
        result.Successes++
        result.LastSuccessAt = now().UTC()
    }

The tracker is updated (CloseWithEvidence writes to beads.jsonl) but the working tree / commits are never pushed to origin.

## Required behavior

After a successful landing (merge outcome = 'merged' AND bead status = success), the worker must:

1. Run `git push --ff-only origin <default-branch>`
2. On rejection:
   - Classify as ExecuteBeadStatusLandConflict (this status already exists in execute_bead_status.go)
   - Preserve the commits under refs/ddx/iterations/ (the infrastructure already exists for the preserved-result case)
   - Unclaim the bead so a later attempt can pick it up again AFTER divergence is resolved
   - Emit a 'push_rejected' event with the local HEAD and the remote tip
   - Stop the worker loop (divergence requires human attention)
3. On network failure: similar to fetch — log a warning, mark the attempt with a 'push_pending' flag, continue
4. Pair this push with the fetch-first bead (sibling ddx-bd42f640) so the loop is: fetch → verify ancestry → run bead → push — a closed consistency loop

## Why --ff-only

Force-with-lease is tempting but wrong here — if push is rejected, it means another machine pushed in the meantime, and we want to detect that and reconcile, not overwrite. --ff-only ensures we only ever advance origin, never rewrite it. A rejection is a signal, not a problem to work around.

## Test cases

- Fresh clone, worker lands a bead, push succeeds, origin advances to new HEAD
- Another machine pushed between fetch and commit (race): second worker lands, push is rejected, bead is unclaimed and marked land_conflict, loop stops
- Network unreachable: push is deferred, attempt records 'push_pending', worker continues but subsequent workers halt on the same branch
- Default branch is configurable
title: execute-loop: push to origin on successful bead landing

Pattern:
- Use cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go newScriptHarnessRepo(t, beadCount) to build an isolated repo with real git.
- Use runGitInteg (from cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go) for any supplemental git invocations, so GIT_* env is scrubbed.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadInvalidBeadID$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.

acceptance:
1. types.go:RouteRequest has a Provider field with documented semantics.
2. NormalizeRouteRequest captures flags.Provider into req.Provider in all cases (including when it doesn't name a harness).
3. scoreCandidate applies a soft-preference bonus when candidate's discovered provider matches req.Provider. Regression test asserts the ranking flip.
4. 'ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx' emits a routing event on the bead recording {resolved_provider: 'vidar-omlx', route_reason: 'provider-affinity', ...} — proving provider preference flows end-to-end.
5. cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/ passes.

6. **--harness × --provider interaction** (per codex + opus fresh-eyes review). NormalizeRouteRequest MUST spec the precedence explicitly:
   - --harness alone: pin harness. Provider ignored.
   - --provider alone: pin provider; routing picks best harness whose candidate plan includes that provider.
   - --harness X --provider Y where X's surface is compatible with Y: pin BOTH. (Compatible = Y is a discovered provider whose /v1/models or equivalent serves a model usable by X.)
   - --harness X --provider Y where Y is NOT compatible with X (e.g., --harness codex --provider vidar-omlx — codex is a cloud subscription harness, vidar-omlx is a local openai-compat endpoint): fail fast at normalization with a clear diagnostic ('harness X does not dispatch to provider Y; --harness and --provider are incompatible for this combination'). Do NOT silently drop one flag.
   - Regression test covers all four cases plus the incompatible-combo fail-fast path.

acceptance: execute-loop never selects `script` or `virtual` harness via tier routing (cheap/standard/smart). Explicit `--harness script` still works. Regression test covers the fallback-when-real-harnesses-fail case.
description:
## Problem

The `script` harness is a test-only sentinel (`cli/internal/agent/registry.go:127` — comment says "Binary: ddx-script-agent // sentinel — never actually exec'd") that interprets directive files for deterministic test execution. It has no LLM and cannot produce real work.

Despite being test-only, it shows up in production tier fallback chains. Observed 2026-04-17 on bead agent-ecc79bf0:

```
22:28:39  tier=cheap      harness=lmstudio  model=qwen3.5-27b    execution_failed (exec: no command)
22:28:43  tier=standard   harness=script    model=<prompt.md>    execution_failed (unknown directive "<execute-bead>")
22:28:47  tier=smart      harness=codex     model=gpt-5.4        (succeeded)
```

The `<execute-bead>` XML prompt that execute-bead emits is obviously not a valid script directive file — script's parser choked on it on the first line.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/agent/registry.go:125-132` registers `script` with:
- `CostClass: "local"`
- `IsLocal: true`

`cli/internal/agent/routing.go:scoreCandidate` rewards any `local` harness with +25 (standard profile) or +40 (cheap profile). Because script is registered as local AND always "available" (it doesn't need an LLM or network), it scores highly whenever real local harnesses (LM Studio, ollama, embedded agent) are missing or failed.

There is no filter in the routing logic that excludes `script` (or `virtual`) from execute-bead candidate selection.

## Expected behavior

`script` and `virtual` should be test-only harnesses, never selected by execute-bead / execute-loop production routing. They should only be reachable via an explicit `--harness script` / `--harness virtual` override, not via tier-based profile routing.

## Proposed fix

1. Add a `TestOnly bool` field on the registry struct (or reuse an existing negative marker).
2. Filter test-only harnesses out of the candidate list in routing.go before scoring.
3. Keep explicit opt-in (`--harness script`) working so the existing script tests still pass.
4. Add a regression test that execute-loop never selects script/virtual when real harnesses are unavailable.

## Files likely touched
- cli/internal/agent/registry.go — add TestOnly flag
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — filter TestOnly before scoring
- cli/internal/agent/routing_test.go — regression test
title: bug: script test-harness leaks into production tier fallback chain

acceptance: A single per-project land coordinator goroutine owns all writes to target refs. Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/ (or cli/internal/server/) prove: (1) happy path — worker submits base=A result=B, coordinator fetches current tip X, rebases B onto X, fast-forwards, asserts the new tip is the rebased commit and no merge commits exist in the history between A and the new tip (use git log --merges --count to assert 0); (2) rebase conflict path — worker submits a commit that cannot rebase cleanly, coordinator preserves the submission under refs/ddx/iterations/<bead-id>/<attempt-id> and returns a "preserved" status, target branch is unchanged; (3) serialization path — 5 concurrent submissions against the same project against base=A produce 5 sequential rebased commits on the target branch with linear history (git log --merges returns 0), and the coordinator processes them in order; (4) server runWorker integration — a server-managed worker whose task produces a commit B actually advances the target branch to a rebased B, via the coordinator path, visible in git log main -1 after the worker exits; (5) --local mode integration — the --local execute-loop path runs a process-local coordinator that provides the same semantics. Secondary acceptance: RealOrchestratorGitOps.Merge is deleted from execute_bead_orchestrator.go OR clearly marked as the legacy path to remove; no production code path calls git merge --no-edit on the target branch; FEAT-006, API-001, FEAT-012 still describe the same contract (rebase + ff-only, linear history). Operator-visible smoke: running ddx agent execute-loop --harness claude --model sonnet against a seeded queue produces a linear history on main with no merge commits between the run's first and last commit.
description:
Today's execute-loop orchestration is a pile of patches on a flawed mental model. The user's target model is "human picks PR off the queue → rebases onto current tip → merges → next worker starts from the new tip." The code implements "worker branches off whatever main was when it started → runs task → git merge --no-edit that result back → 2-parent merge commit fan." The two models are incompatible; no patch to the second model gets you to the first.

Evidence the current model is wrong:

1. FEAT-006 §"Landing" (line 414), API-001 §"Landing & Preservation" (lines 217-222), and FEAT-012 §"Execute-Bead Git Operations" (line 413) all explicitly require rebase + fast-forward with linear history. The code in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go:61 Merge() runs `git merge --no-edit` with a defensive `chore: checkpoint before merge` commit — zero rebase, zero ff-only, zero fetch, zero push. Three spec files aligned on one model; code implements a different one.

2. cli/internal/server/workers.go:runWorker (around line 237) calls agent.ExecuteBead via an inline ExecuteBeadExecutorFunc closure at workers.go:248 and never calls LandBeadResult. LandBeadResult is only wired into two call sites: cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1372 (the `--local` execute-loop path) and cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go:134 (the interactive single-bead CLI). The default `ddx agent execute-loop` path (which submits to the server) produces worker commits that are NEVER landed on main. Commits sit in worktree refs and the loop reports "success" to the tracker anyway.

3. The "worker simplification" commit 49c3d2b that closed ddx-679d2fc7 moved Merge/UpdateRef out of execute_bead.go's GitOps interface into a new OrchestratorGitOps in execute_bead_orchestrator.go. That is a code-level function split, not a process-level split. The "parent orchestrator" runs synchronously in the worker process from whichever call site reaches it first. Two workers racing on the same projectRoot will both call LandBeadResult with stale baseRev assumptions; the current Merge implementation papers over conflicts with ort-strategy merge commits. The user was explicit in their feedback: "we may not be separating concerns appropriately — we have pushed too much functionality into execute-loop and it's causing us issues; it needs to be very simple: 'I've been handed a worktree and a task, I will execute that task and make commits, produce progress output, notify done.'" The current code does not reflect that intent.

4. Six open child beads of ddx-b7ee0d6f (bd42f640 fetch-before-claim, 6aa50e57 rebase-instead-of-merge, 83ec1430 push-on-land, 2791dc4f tracker concurrency, 42dd982c divergence monitoring, b0374901 multi-machine docs) each correctly identify one piece of the gap but do not compose into a coherent end-to-end workflow. The fix is not "implement all six"; the fix is to adopt the human-PR model as the single source of truth and then let those beads fall out as sub-tasks of a coherent design.

## Target design

The human-PR mental model, encoded as code:

1. **Worker.** Accepts a pre-made worktree and a prompt. Runs the agent harness. Emits structured progress events. Produces zero or more commits in the worktree. Exits with a simple terminal status (task_succeeded / task_failed / task_no_changes) and reports its worktree branch + commit SHAs to the server. The worker NEVER fetches, rebases, merges, pushes, or writes a ref. The worker NEVER interacts with target refs on the shared project repo. The worker does not care what main is doing.

2. **Land coordinator.** A single goroutine per project (hosted on the ddx server) that owns all writes to the project's target refs. Workers submit their completed worktree branch SHAs to the coordinator via a server endpoint. The coordinator, for each submission, in order: (a) `git fetch origin <target-branch>` to refresh the target tip; (b) `git rebase --onto <target-tip> <base-rev> <result-rev>` to replay the worker's commits onto the latest tip; (c) if the rebase is clean, `git push --ff-only origin <target-branch>` (or equivalent local update-ref if no remote); (d) on rebase conflict or push rejection, move the submission to `refs/ddx/iterations/<bead-id>/<attempt-id>` and mark the bead as needing human review. The coordinator handles one submission at a time. No locks needed internally because there's only one goroutine.

3. **Worker-to-coordinator handoff.** Workers submit to a new `POST /api/agent/workers/:id/land-submission` endpoint with {worktree_branch, base_rev, result_rev, bead_id}. The endpoint enqueues the submission onto the land coordinator's channel and returns 202 Accepted. Workers block (via long-polling GET, SSE, or channel-await) for the land result — success (pushed to target), preserved (moved to iteration ref), or failed (rejected, worker should retry from new tip).

4. **Worker isolation.** Workers run in /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/ worktrees per the alpha14 worktree-relocation fix. They fetch their starting base from the coordinator ("what is the current tip?"), branch off it, work, and submit. They never touch the shared project .git beyond their own worktree's read-fetch.

5. **Tracker (beads.jsonl) coordination.** Separate concern from the git target refs. ddx-2791dc4f stays as its own bead because the tracker is a second shared resource with its own write-serialization requirement. The land coordinator and the tracker lock must both be respected, but they are orthogonal locks.

6. **Multi-machine.** When the server grows to multi-machine topology, each machine runs its own land coordinator against its local clone of the project. Coordinators push to a common origin. The origin becomes the single source of truth for the target branch. Rebase-then-push gives the per-machine coordinators a clean serialization point via origin's ref update (push is atomic at the remote).

## What this replaces

- ddx-bd42f640 (fetch+ancestry check before claim) → subsumed. The land coordinator owns the fetch; workers don't need to check ancestry because they're handed a branch from the coordinator.
- ddx-6aa50e57 (rebase instead of merge) → subsumed. The coordinator's rebase step is the canonical implementation.
- ddx-83ec1430 (push-to-origin on landing) → subsumed. Push is part of the coordinator's sequence.
- ddx-42dd982c (divergence monitoring) → reframed. Becomes monitoring of the coordinator's rejection rate and conflict rate.
- ddx-b0374901 (multi-machine topology docs) → reframed. Document the per-machine coordinator + push-to-origin model.
- The missing "server runWorker never lands" bug (new P0 child, filed alongside) → subsumed. The new model HAS a land step by design.

What this does NOT replace:

- ddx-2791dc4f (beads.jsonl concurrent-write safety) — separate concern, separate lock.
- ddx-679d2fc7 (simplify worker to task-only) — this bead is nominally closed by commit 49c3d2b, but that commit did a cosmetic function split, not the architectural process split. Re-open or file a follow-up that cleans up the nomenclature ("OrchestratorGitOps" → "CoordinatorGitOps" scoped to the single coordinator goroutine) and deletes Merge() in favor of Land(). OR leave it closed and track that cleanup under this meta-bead.

## Why this is better

The core insight: **target-ref writes must be serialized through a single writer that always sees the current tip.** That is how every real PR queue works (GitHub merge queue, gerrit, Zuul). The current design has every worker trying to be its own merge-master; the fix is to have exactly one merge-master per project and let workers be contributors.

## Rollout

This is a redesign, not a patch. Rollout steps:

1. Land a new Land(projectRoot, baseRev, resultRev, targetBranch) coordinator function that implements fetch → rebase → ff-push → preserve-on-fail. Unit tests that cover the happy path, the rebase conflict path, and the concurrent-submission serialization path.
2. Add POST /api/agent/workers/:id/land-submission + the land coordinator goroutine + per-project queue channel to cli/internal/server/workers.go.
3. Rewrite workers.go:runWorker to have the worker's executor closure submit to the local coordinator (via channel; no HTTP round-trip needed since it's in the same process) instead of calling LandBeadResult inline.
4. Delete or mothball the current LandBeadResult and its "orchestrator" naming. The new coordinator replaces it.
5. Leave --local mode working by having it instantiate a process-local single-goroutine coordinator at startup. --local remains useful for single-bead debugging.
6. Add a --coordinator-only server mode for the multi-machine eventual topology (out of scope for the first cut; reference ddx-e2f497c3 for the design).

Out of scope for this bead (but related):

- Interactive conflict resolution UI. The meta-bead says "preserve under refs/ddx/iterations/" and move on; a separate UI flow for human merge-conflict resolution is its own work.
- Cross-machine coordinators talking to each other. Out of scope; origin push suffices.
- PR approval / review gates before the coordinator lands. Separate feature.
title: execute-loop: adopt human-PR landing model end-to-end (meta redesign)

description:
Surfaced by ddx-bc1e99ec (commit 280f58e0). The observability_workdir.go wrappers currently build a minimal Runner via NewRunner(cfg) and call its observability methods (ProbeHarnessState, LoadRoutingSignalSnapshot, RefreshClaudeQuotaViaTmux, RefreshCodexQuotaViaTmux). This keeps Runner alive as an internal implementation detail.

Goal: lift the file-reading logic OUT of Runner methods into free functions (or just inline into observability_workdir.go). Then Runner can finally die, and the 5 file-delete beads cascade.

Key insight: the observability data DDx surfaces lives in DDx-managed paths (session JSONLs in cfg.SessionLogDir, quota caches in agent module - need to call into agent for quota refresh). These are file-read operations, not Runner-state operations.

Files involved:
- cli/internal/agent/observability_workdir.go — currently uses Runner-build pattern; rewrite to direct file reads
- cli/internal/agent/runner.go — extract file-reading logic from methods then delete those methods (Runner.ProbeHarnessState, Runner.LoadRoutingSignalSnapshot, Runner.RefreshClaudeQuotaViaTmux, Runner.RefreshCodexQuotaViaTmux). The methods themselves are likely just thin wrappers around Runner.Config + free helpers — extract the helpers, drop the methods.
- For tmux quota refresh: call agent module helpers directly (internal/harnesses/{claude,codex}/quota_tmux.go via the existing tmux probe ENTRY POINTS; the agent has these functions exported, e.g. ReadClaudeQuotaViaTmux from internal/harnesses/claude — check)

After this lands:
- Runner public methods all gone
- Runner struct can delete (Phase 5g — separate bead OR fold here)
- The 5 file-delete beads (ddx-f0cab7b3 state+registry, ddx-ea7f041c routing.go, ddx-70c48576 routing observability, ddx-1bf6c0df tier_escalation, ddx-2912e7cd discovery+deadline+probe) cascade cleanly

acceptance:
**User story:** As an operator using a browser — on a machine without a terminal, or a phone, or a laptop away from my dev box — I can open my project's home page, type a prompt into a chat pane, and see the DDx agent work on it in real time. Tool calls stream as they happen, costs accrue visibly, and the conversation persists so I can come back to it.

**Acceptance criteria (written for Option A; rewrite if design note chooses B/C/D):**

1. **Design note landed.** One-pager in `docs/helix/02-design/` records the chosen option, the answers to the six subsidiary questions, and the message/thread schema. Implementation PR links to it.

2. **Thread model.** Chat threads persist under `.ddx/chat/<thread-id>/{manifest.json,messages.jsonl}`. One thread per project per operator by default; operators can create named threads. Messages.jsonl is append-only, one JSON object per message (user or assistant), with `sessionId` linking each assistant turn back to an entry in the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`).

3. **GraphQL surface.** New resolvers:
   - Query: `chatThreads(projectId)`, `chatThread(id)`, `chatMessages(threadId, since?)`.
   - Mutation: `chatThreadCreate(projectId, title?)`, `chatSend(threadId, message, harness?, profile?)` → kicks off a session via `service.Execute`.
   - Subscription: `chatStream(threadId)` → emits assistant message chunks + tool call events (same event vocabulary as `workers.recentEvents`).

4. **Home page UI.** Project home page gains a chat pane below the actions section. Pane shows thread list, active thread messages, input box, harness/profile picker. Tool calls render inline within assistant messages. Streaming updates as chunks arrive.

5. **Session linkage.** Each assistant turn creates one session in the sessions index. Clicking an assistant message shows the session id and a link to the session detail (`/sessions/<id>`). Chat thread header shows cumulative session count + total cost for the thread.

6. **Concurrency.** Two browsers open on the same thread see each other's messages within 2s (subscription-driven). Two operators on different threads in the same project do not interfere.

7. **Interrupt.** The in-flight assistant turn can be cancelled via a Stop button; server sends the harness the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop <id>` for the in-flight session. The thread records a cancelled turn, does not try to auto-resume.

8. **Cost guardrail (soft).** If the cumulative thread cost passes a configurable ceiling (`.ddx/config.yaml: chat.cost_ceiling_usd`, default unset), the UI warns but does not block. Hard block is a follow-up.

9. **Auth posture.**
   - Per design note. If the chosen posture is "single-operator localhost / tsnet only for v1," the chat routes are served unauthenticated behind tsnet and the design note records the gap to close before broader deployment.
   - A small middleware gate is in place even for v1 so adding auth later is schema-local, not route-local.

10. **Playwright e2e.**
    - Seeds a project with zero chat threads.
    - Navigates to project home, asserts the chat pane renders with "Start a new thread" affordance.
    - Starts a thread, sends a short prompt, simulates a streamed session response with one tool call, asserts chunks appear in order and the session id link is present.
    - Opens a second browser context on the same thread, asserts both see new messages within 2s.
    - Cancels an in-flight turn, asserts the cancelled state is persisted.

11. **Depends on.**
    - `ddx-2513f08c` (home page reachable from sidebar) — hard dependency.
    - `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) — hard dependency for the session linkage + cost rollup.

12. **Explicitly deferred.** PTY integration (Option B / pop-out terminal from Option C) is its own bead, filed only if the Option A rollout surfaces real interactive-steering needs.
description:
## Observed / Motivation

User wants to "lift work out of the TUI entirely and allow non-tmux users to work remotely." Today the only way to prompt the DDx agent stack interactively is a terminal session. The web UI is read-mostly: dispatch three actions (drain/align/checks) and review results. There is no surface that lets an operator say "go do X" against the project in natural language from a browser.

Related UX: the project home page already exists (`/nodes/.../projects/<id>/`), renders queue summary + actions, and is the natural host for a chat affordance. First the home page needs to be reachable from the sidebar (`ddx-2513f08c`) — this bead depends on that work.

## The tension

The DDx harness abstraction (`cli/internal/agent/service_run.go`, `service.Execute`) is designed for **non-interactive** use: prompt in, structured result out, optional streaming. This is the right shape for queue-draining, quorum, evaluation, and automation. It is **not** the shape of a TUI conversation with tab completion, mid-run steering, and back-channel signals.

An operator-facing chat can land on either side of that line. The bead records all four options so the design phase can pick one deliberately.

## Options (design phase decides, recommended path noted)

### Option A — Chat over the harness abstraction (RECOMMENDED starting point)

- Chat UI posts a prompt → server dispatches a session via `service.Execute` (or a wrapper) → streams tool calls + response chunks back over a GraphQL subscription.
- Message history is persisted as a thread; each user turn = one new session. Responses reuse the existing `recentEvents` stream shape already used on the workers detail page.
- Works with every harness today because the harness abstraction is what it is. claude, codex, agent (embedded) all participate.
- **Trade-off:** no mid-run steering, no tab completion, no live back-and-forth inside one agent invocation.
- Matches the user's stated target audience (non-TUI users who want remote browser access). Those users aren't trying to steer a TUI — they're trying to leave it behind.

### Option B — PTY gateway per harness

- Server owns a PTY pool. Each chat thread attaches to one harness process (claude/codex/etc.) over a WebSocket; browser renders xterm.js.
- Full interactivity, including TUI steering.
- **Trade-off:** PTY lifecycle, auth, resource limits, tab-close-loses-session semantics, multi-user access control — all real. The harness abstraction is bypassed for this mode, which creates a second execution path to maintain.

### Option C — Hybrid (chat-first + pop-out terminal)

- Option A is the primary surface. When a chat reaches a point that needs interactive steering, a "Open terminal" escape hatch attaches a PTY to the current harness session.
- **Trade-off:** more surface area, but honest about both use cases. Most sessions stay on the cheap path.

### Option D — Web-tmux / ttyd wrapper

- Embed the existing terminal tooling directly: ttyd or xterm.js over a tmux socket owned by the server.
- **Trade-off:** fastest to ship, zero harness work, but the UX is a terminal in a browser — not "chat." Requires tmux. Doesn't serve the stated "non-tmux users" audience.

### Recommendation

Start with **A**. If the design note documents real interactive-steering needs we can't paper over, the right follow-up is **C** (A + PTY escape hatch), not B (PTY-only).

## Scope of this bead

- Design note in `docs/helix/02-design/` locking the option, the message schema, persistence model, and auth posture. Implementation PR links to it.
- First implementation pass: Option A.
- Home page gains a chat pane as its primary interaction surface, alongside the existing queue summary + actions (which stay — they're quick-dispatch affordances, not replaced by chat).

## Subsidiary questions the design note must answer

1. **Message persistence.** Are chat threads per-project or per-user? Stored where (`.ddx/chat/<thread-id>/` or elsewhere)? Is history shared across operators on the same project, or isolated per operator identity?
2. **Harness selection.** Does the chat pick a harness per-thread (picker in the UI) or per-message (re-route each turn)? Default to profile-based selection?
3. **Cost accountability.** Every chat message spawns a session. Sessions are already tracked in the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). The chat thread should surface cumulative cost to the operator so they don't blow budget unknowingly.
4. **Tool execution scope.** Chat prompts will trigger agent tool calls that read/write the project tree. Is the chat session bounded to the same permissions as `ddx agent run`? Does it run in the project worktree, a worktree per thread, a scratch dir? Default safe answer: same worktree as `ddx agent run` (user trusts ddx running anyway), but design note records it.
5. **Concurrency.** What happens if two operators have the chat open on the same project and both send messages? Default: independent threads, each with its own session — no shared state.
6. **Auth.** Today the server assumes single-operator localhost. Remote use implies some identity concept. Design note picks a posture: delegate to reverse proxy, ship a simple token, or punt and require tsnet + single-operator for v1.

## Out of scope

- Full PTY implementation (Option B). Follow-up bead if A+hybrid doesn't suffice.
- Cross-project chat.
- Voice input.
title: Add chat interface to project home page: prompt ddx remotely without the TUI

acceptance: Child design beads define the one-machine multi-project server module, the server-managed execute-bead supervisor contract, and the future service-backed multi-node topology with clear stage boundaries
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: ddx server topology beyond the initial execute-bead release lane. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-013, SD-013, the single-project server architecture, and the requirement to defer multi-project and multi-host topology work until the single-project executable-bead flow is tested and released.
</context-digest>
Evolve `ddx server` beyond the initial single-project execute-bead release lane in staged steps.

## Scope
- Stage 2: one-machine, multi-project `ddx server` with project-scoped APIs, UI, and worker control after the single-project execute-bead loop is tested and released
- Stage 3: service-backed storage/control plane with a central web UI and multiple headless node gateways, each serving multiple projects and workers

## Boundaries
- Preserve local-first, git-authoritative project state for code/docs/beads
- Do not compete with the immediate FEAT-006 lane to ship a trustworthy single-project executable-bead loop
- Keep DDx primitives explicit so HELIX and other workflow tools can compose scheduling policy on top

acceptance: The design stack defines a single-project loop that watches execution-ready beads, consumes the generic DDx execution validator, executes beads in isolated worktrees, runs required post-run checks, preserves failed iterations with explicit bead/result state, lands successful runs by rebase+fast-forward, and is explicitly scoped for first release without depending on multi-project or multi-host server behavior
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead automation and single-project worker supervision. Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, SD-012, the execute-bead contract bead ddx-5448b39c, and the requirement to ship a tested single-project loop before broader multi-project or multi-host server work.
</context-digest>
Design the long-running single-project loop that watches for execution-ready beads and drives `ddx agent execute-bead` safely.

## Goals
1. Define a single-project loop that monitors one project queue for execution-ready beads using the generic DDx execution validator rather than HELIX-specific hidden policy
2. Define the exact state machine: claim/select bead, validate structural executability, execute in an isolated worktree, run post-run validity checks / required executions, classify outcome, and continue scanning
3. Make success and failure outcomes explicit: on failed execution or failed post-run validity checks, mark the bead/result accordingly and preserve the iteration/evidence exactly as the execute-bead contract specifies; on successful validation, land by rebase + fast-forward, reset the worker worktree to the updated branch tip, and continue
4. Keep the first shipped loop single-project scoped; do not require multi-project discovery, cross-project scheduling, or multi-host coordination in this bead
5. Define the minimal control surface for running the loop standalone or under `ddx server`, without coupling the design to later multi-project topology decisions

## Required outputs
- Single-project loop and worker state machine
- Failure/preservation semantics and retry surfaces
- Post-run validation / required-execution integration
- Minimal operator control surface and observability for the shipped loop
title: Design the single-project execute-bead loop and supervisor contract

acceptance: Invoking `ddx bead` commands anywhere inside a repo subtree that contains a local `.ddx/` resolves to that repo's tracker; installed and source-built binaries behave the same; and regression tests cover repo root and nested subdirectory invocation without silent success against the wrong tracker
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: tracker context resolution for ddx bead commands. Evidence covers the repo-local tracker contract in FEAT-004, the CLI project-root resolution rules, and the observed failure where an installed ddx binary invoked from /Users/erik/Projects/ddx returned bead IDs from `ddx bead create` but did not persist them into this repo's .ddx/beads.jsonl.
</context-digest>
Make bead commands resolve their tracker context deterministically from the nearest parent repository containing a .ddx directory, rather than drifting to another context or reporting success without updating the live tracker.

## Scope
- Reproduce and fix the context-resolution bug for `ddx bead create` and the rest of the bead command family
- Nail down the repository-selection rule: nearest parent repo with a local `.ddx/` wins
- Ensure installed and source-built binaries resolve the same tracker context when invoked from the same repo subtree
- Add regression coverage for repo-root, subdirectory, and nested-worktree invocation paths

## Boundaries
- Do not change the rule that bead state is maintained via `ddx bead` commands rather than direct JSONL edits
- Do not introduce hidden cross-project tracker selection heuristics

acceptance: (1) sessions/+page.svelte uses $derived.by (not $derived of a function literal). (2) Consumers access summary.totalCost not summary().totalCost. (3) bun run check green.
description:
Opus Stage 4 batch review (2026-04-15): sessions/+page.svelte:21 uses $derived(() => { ... }) where the body returns a function. Accessed via summary().totalCost. This defeats memoization — the body re-runs on every read (6× per render here).

Fix: switch to $derived.by(() => ...) returning the object directly, so consumers read summary.totalCost and memoization kicks in.
title: refactor(svelte): sessions page $derived returns function — defeats memoization

acceptance: (1) bun run check shows 0 warnings for BeadForm.svelte. (2) Switching the bead prop on a mounted form updates all fields. (3) Existing form submission flows still work.
description:
Opus Stage 4 batch review (2026-04-15): BeadForm.svelte:41-51 captures 10 prop values at mount (const isUpdate = bead != null; let title = $state(bead?.title ?? ''); ...). svelte-check emits 10 state_referenced_locally warnings.

The form is currently always mounted fresh per modal open, but if anyone tries to reuse a single form with a changing bead prop, every field silently freezes. Switch initializers to $derived or add {#key bead?.id} where the form is rendered (call sites include beads/[projectId]/beads/+layout.svelte and BeadDetail.svelte).
title: refactor(svelte): BeadForm 10 stale-initializer warnings — use $derived or {#key}

acceptance: ddx agent workers prints a table with STATE, BEAD ID, TITLE, HARNESS, MODEL, ELAPSED, ATTEMPTS columns. Server-submitted workers show state from WorkerManager. Local --local workers are detected via manifest scan of .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories. --json flag emits raw JSON. --watch re-renders every 2s. A worker executing bead axon-abc123 shows that bead ID and its title (truncated). An idle system (no active workers) prints 'no active workers' rather than an empty table. No dependency on worktrunk or any external binary beyond git.
description:
There is no CLI command to see which workers are running and which bead each is executing. The WorkerManager in cli/internal/server/workers.go already tracks this (WorkerRecord.CurrentBead at line 51, WorkerRecord.State, WorkerRecord.Harness, WorkerRecord.Model, WorkerRecord.Attempts/Successes/Failures) but it is only accessible through the server API. Local --local workers are not tracked by the server at all.

This bead adds ddx agent workers as a top-level CLI subcommand. It must not depend on worktrunk (wt) or any external tool.

## Required changes

### 1. New command: ddx agent workers (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)
Register alongside the existing ddx agent log command. Flags:
  --json         emit raw JSON array
  --watch        re-render every 2s (like watch -n2) until Ctrl-C
  --project      filter to a specific project root (default: CWD project)

### 2. Data sources (two paths)
A. Server-submitted workers: GET http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/agent/workers — deserialize []WorkerRecord from the server. Already implemented in cli/internal/server/workers.go List() / HTTP handler.
B. Local --local workers: the server does not track these. Detect them by:
   1. Reading .ddx/workers/*/status.json files in the project root (server writes these even for local workers when they self-register — confirm in workers.go; if not, fall back to scanning active execute-bead worktrees)
   2. Scanning .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories (from ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go line 403) and reading the manifest.json inside each to extract bead_id, attempt_id, harness, model, started_at

### 3. Local worker detection via manifest scan
For each directory matching .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-*:
  - Read .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/manifest.json (path encoded in worktree name suffix)
  - Check if worktree is still active (git worktree list --porcelain)
  - Read execute-loop-heartbeat-at from bead.Extra (requires ddx-7651506a) if available
  - Derive synthetic WorkerRecord from manifest fields

### 4. Table output (default)
Columns: STATE | BEAD | TITLE (truncated 40 chars) | HARNESS | MODEL | ELAPSED | ATTEMPTS
- STATE: running (green), exited (dim), failed (red)
- ELAPSED: human-readable (e.g., 4m32s)
- If current bead title unavailable (local worker pre-manifest), show bead ID

### 5. Merge and deduplicate
When both server and manifest sources are available, deduplicate by worker_id or attempt_id. Server record takes precedence over manifest-derived record for the same worker.

## Key files
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — add newAgentWorkersCommand(), register in newAgentCommand()
- cli/internal/server/workers.go — WorkerRecord struct (lines 31-54), List API
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant (line 403), manifest shape (lines 726-771)
- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry Correlation map (bead_id field)
title: Add ddx agent workers command — native live worker status with current bead

acceptance: 1) TestExecuteBeadNoGatesWhenNoChanges no longer references fakeExecuteBeadGit or fakeAgentRunner. 2) Test dispatches through RealLandingGitOps + newScriptHarnessRepo (or equivalent real-git harness). 3) cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadNoGatesWhenNoChanges$' -count=1 green. 4) No other test in agent_execute_bead_test.go is modified.
description:
Migrate cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go TestExecuteBeadNoGatesWhenNoChanges() from fakeExecuteBeadGit + fakeAgentRunner to real git + script harness.

Pattern:
- Use cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go newScriptHarnessRepo(t, beadCount) to build an isolated repo with real git.
- Use runGitInteg (from cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go) for any supplemental git invocations, so GIT_* env is scrubbed.
- Replace fakeExecuteBeadGit with the RealLandingGitOps flow used in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land_test.go.
- Replace fakeAgentRunner with the script harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go) driven by a per-attempt directive file. When the test needs to simulate 'agent emits no commits' or 'agent emits a dirty worktree', encode that as script directives rather than a canned Result struct.
- Preserve every assertion — the point is that the test now exercises real git + real merge/preserve semantics, not that the surface shrinks.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadNoGatesWhenNoChanges$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.
title: test: migrate TestExecuteBeadNoGatesWhenNoChanges to real git + script harness

3. Re-run 'ddx work --no-adaptive-min-tier --min-tier cheap --max-tier cheap' against the 32 ready fake-migration beads; expect them to resolve 'qwen3.6' → whatever live endpoint serves it and actually execute.

acceptance:
- [ ] When the primary project tree is dirty before managed execute-bead/execute-loop starts, DDx records a deterministic pre-run checkpoint or equivalent recoverable reference before creating the worker worktree
- [ ] The checkpoint/ref is surfaced in execution evidence so operators can recover the exact pre-run local state later
- [ ] Clean parent trees do not create redundant checkpoint artifacts
- [ ] Regression tests cover dirty and clean parent-tree cases for both single-bead and execute-loop entrypoints
- [ ] Existing landing behavior and isolated-worktree cleanup remain intact
description:
CONTRACT-001 assigns dirty-tree checkpointing before managed execution to DDx, but current execute-bead/execute-loop flows create isolated worktrees from HEAD without automatically checkpointing pre-existing dirty parent-tree state. DDx has a manual `ddx checkpoint` command, yet managed execution does not create or reference a pre-run checkpoint artifact.

Add deterministic dirty-parent-tree checkpointing so a managed run can always point to the pre-execution state it started from.

spec_grounding: Per FEAT-012 §22 ('Checkpoint a dirty caller worktree before execution — creates a commit, does not discard changes') and FEAT-012 US-126 AC#1 ('DDx creates a checkpoint commit from those changes and uses it as the effective base revision'). Implementation: a real git commit on the current branch via the existing SynthesizeCommit helper before the worker worktree is created. NOT a stash, NOT a hidden ref, NOT an evidence file, NOT a new ExecuteBeadResult field. The checkpoint commit IS the artifact.
title: Checkpoint dirty parent trees before managed bead execution

description:
<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead merge gating. Evidence covers FEAT-006 merge-by-default landing semantics, FEAT-007 graph-authored execution documents with ddx.execution.required=true, FEAT-010 execute-bead compatibility rules, and the current implementation gap where execute-bead lands successful runs without enforcing documented required execution gates.
</context-digest>
Close the gap between the documented execute-bead merge-gate model and the shipped implementation.

## Scope
- Make the merge-default contract explicit: successful execute-bead runs land unless an explicit gate blocks landing or --no-merge is set
- Keep merge gates in the existing executable-bead shape: graph-authored execution documents linked through governing artifacts, not new bead fields
- Separate structural execution-readiness validation from post-run merge-gate evaluation
- Dry-run the intended model by authoring real execution-gate metadata for the current execute-loop/docs lane

acceptance: FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 define a structured embedded-agent progress contract for managed runs; the contract names the required phases and fields, distinguishes runtime-only progress from tracked execution evidence, and defines one shared read model for CLI and server/UI consumers
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: embedded ddx-agent runtime observability and live worker progress. Evidence covers FEAT-006 execution/evidence ownership, FEAT-002 server activity surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI expectations, execute-loop worker supervision, and the need for DDx-owned progress telemetry beyond coarse session logs when DDx runs the embedded harness.
</context-digest>
Define the progress-event contract for managed embedded-agent runs so DDx can surface meaningful live execution status in the CLI and server/UI.

## Goals
- Define structured phase/heartbeat output for embedded-agent runs (queueing, launch, running, post-checks, landing, preserved, done)
- Define the minimal per-event fields needed for operator visibility and later reporting: worker/project/bead/run identity, phase, elapsed/heartbeat timing, harness/provider/model/profile, and token/cost deltas when available
- Clarify which live progress artifacts remain runtime-only and which summaries/indexes are exposed through DDx-owned APIs and UI surfaces
- Keep provider-native transcript ownership external; DDx progress output should be normalized runtime telemetry, not transcript duplication

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 so embedded-agent progress visibility is an explicit contract rather than an implementation detail
- Clarify how progress events relate to tracked execution artifacts, session logs, and server-managed worker state

## Required implementation planning
- Define the server/API/UI read model for live worker progress and recent phase history
- Define how CLI and UI consume the same structured progress feed without introducing hidden workflow policy

Review area: DDx-owned routing metrics implementation. Evidence covers the metrics spike outcome, TD-006 activity semantics, and the embedded ddx-agent telemetry boundary.
</context-digest>
Implement the minimal DDx-owned routing metrics/activity layer.

## Scope
- Persist only routing-relevant metrics and invocation metadata
- Store native session and trace references where available
- Persist async quota snapshot history for any live-probe quota source
- Compute the inputs needed for relative subscription-burn estimation
- Do not duplicate external provider prompt/response bodies
- Preserve backward compatibility for existing activity/session rows
title: Implement minimal DDx-owned routing metrics, snapshots, and activity records

Each persona has a Sources section citing at least one entry from the external canon. grep -rE 'cli/internal/|docs/helix/' library/personas/*.md returns no matches (no DDx-internal path references). library/personas/README.md documents the quality bar and cross-harness consumption contract. No shipped workflow binds to a dropped persona name without a deprecation note.
description:
Trim the current 10-persona roster to 5 role-focused personas, generalize to remove DDx-internal path references, and anchor each in external canon (Anthropic Prompt Library, OpenAI Cookbook: Prompt Personalities, Superpowers, fspec) so the provenance of each stance is explicit.

Governing spec: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md (Personas section, newly added in spec-alignment pass).

Current roster (10): architect-systems, data-driven-optimizer, pragmatic-implementer, product-discovery-analyst, product-manager-minimalist, reliability-guardian, simplicity-architect, specification-enforcer, strict-code-reviewer, test-engineer-tdd.

Target roster (5): code-reviewer (from strict-code-reviewer), test-engineer (from test-engineer-tdd, decoupled from DDx-internal paths), implementer (from pragmatic-implementer), architect (from architect-systems), specification-enforcer (kept; rewritten grounded in fspec).

Dropped (merge into survivors or move to plugins): reliability-guardian, simplicity-architect, data-driven-optimizer, product-discovery-analyst, product-manager-minimalist.

Blocked on Phase 1 epic because the new unified ddx skill references library/personas/ content via reference/agents.md; rewriting personas before the new skill surface is stable just means double-rewriting.

acceptance: 1) TestExecuteBeadModelFlagPassthrough no longer references fakeExecuteBeadGit or fakeAgentRunner. 2) Test dispatches through RealLandingGitOps + newScriptHarnessRepo (or equivalent real-git harness). 3) cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadModelFlagPassthrough$' -count=1 green. 4) No other test in agent_execute_bead_test.go is modified.
description:
Migrate cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go TestExecuteBeadModelFlagPassthrough() from fakeExecuteBeadGit + fakeAgentRunner to real git + script harness.

Pattern:
- Use cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go newScriptHarnessRepo(t, beadCount) to build an isolated repo with real git.
- Use runGitInteg (from cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go) for any supplemental git invocations, so GIT_* env is scrubbed.
- Replace fakeExecuteBeadGit with the RealLandingGitOps flow used in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land_test.go.
- Replace fakeAgentRunner with the script harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go) driven by a per-attempt directive file. When the test needs to simulate 'agent emits no commits' or 'agent emits a dirty worktree', encode that as script directives rather than a canned Result struct.
- Preserve every assertion — the point is that the test now exercises real git + real merge/preserve semantics, not that the surface shrinks.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadModelFlagPassthrough$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.

description:
Update cli/cmd/init.go to handle both fresh projects and upgrades from older DDx versions.

Changes required:
1. Copy skills/ddx/ (whole directory tree — SKILL.md + reference/ + evals/) from the embedded FS into the target project:
     .claude/skills/ddx/ (for Claude Code)
     .agents/skills/ddx/ (for Codex and other agentskills.io consumers)
     .ddx/skills/ddx/    (project-local copy)
   Real files, not symlinks — symlinks break after git clone on a fresh machine.
2. Remove stale ddx-prefixed skill directories from prior DDx versions before copying the new one. Known stale dirs: ddx-bead, ddx-run, ddx-agent, ddx-review, ddx-status, ddx-doctor, ddx-install, ddx-release. Check each of .claude/skills/, .agents/skills/, .ddx/skills/. Do NOT touch third-party skills — only the known ddx-prefixed allowlist.
3. AGENTS.md: merge, do not clobber. Use marker-delimited injection like the existing CLAUDE.md meta-prompt injector (see cli/internal/metaprompt/). Wrap DDx content in:
     <!-- DDX-AGENTS:START -->
     (DDx block)

DDx is a toolkit for AI-assisted development that helps you:

• Share templates, prompts, and patterns across projects
• Maintain consistent development practices
• Integrate AI tooling seamlessly
• Contribute improvements back to the community

Get started:
  ddx init          Initialize DDx in your project

  agent       Invoke AI agents with harness dispatch, quorum, and session logging

  list        List available DDx resources
  log         Show DDX asset history

description:
Surfaced by ddx-c0f6b19c. The original ddx-70c48576 said "delete routing_signal_*.go because moved upstream." But these are DDx-side evidence primitives:
- routing_signal_adapters.go — ProbeOpenRouterBalance, ReadCodexNativeSignals (DDx native CLI session reading)
- routing_signal_http.go — provider HTTP probes for DDx evidence
- routing_signal_tmux.go — DDx-side tmux scrape (the AGENT also has its own tmux scrape in internal/harnesses; the DDx-side variant reads native CLI logs from DDx-managed paths)
- observability_workdir.go (kept) depends on ~25 free functions from these files

Re-scope ddx-70c48576: KEEP these files in DDx as evidence-collection primitives (parallel to the kept routing_metrics.go which writes .ddx/agent-logs/routing-outcomes.jsonl). Only delete routing_signals.go ITSELF if its top-level types (RoutingSignalSnapshot, RoutingOutcome, etc.) are no longer referenced.

Outcome of this bead: ddx-70c48576 notes updated; clear scope going forward.
notes:
INVENTORY (per codex review):

Signal-package files in cli/internal/agent/ (production callers in parens):
- routing_signals.go — defines RoutingSignalSnapshot, RoutingOutcome, source kind constants. Used by: cmd/agent_usage.go (RoutingOutcome aggregation), server/providers.go (RoutingSignalSnapshot fan-out), server/workers.go (RoutingOutcome write).
- routing_signal_adapters.go — ProbeOpenRouterBalance, ReadCodexNativeSignals, source-kind constants. Used by: routing_signals.go internally. DDx-evidence reading.
- routing_signal_http.go — provider HTTP probes. Used by: routing_signal_adapters.go internally.
- routing_signal_tmux.go — DDx-side tmux scrape (parallel to agents internal/harnesses/{claude,codex}/quota_tmux.go). Used by: routing_signals.go internally + observability_workdir.go RefreshXQuotaViaTmuxForWorkDir wrappers. SUBJECT TO ddx-f49d7d1a tmux-ownership decision.

DECISION:
- routing_signals.go, routing_signal_adapters.go, routing_signal_http.go: KEEP in DDx as evidence-collection primitives (parallel to routing_metrics.go which the cleanup explicitly preserves). They read DDx-managed data (provider native CLI logs, OpenRouter balance, etc.).
- routing_signal_tmux.go: PER ddx-f49d7d1a — agent owns tmux scrape; DELETE from DDx after observability_workdir.go retires the RefreshXQuotaViaTmuxForWorkDir wrappers.
- routing_metrics.go (separate file, already kept): stays. DDx evidence storage.

CONSEQUENCES:
- ddx-70c48576 RE-SCOPED: only routing_signal_tmux.go + routing_signal_tmux_test.go delete. The other signal_* files stay.
- routing_signals.go + RoutingSignalSnapshot type STAYS as DDx-internal type. server/providers.go consumers (ddx-c7fe4337) just need to drop the agent.NewRegistry usage; RoutingSignalSnapshot import stays.
title: Re-scope ddx-70c48576: keep routing_signal_*.go as DDx evidence; only delete routing_signals.go orphans

acceptance:
- [ ] cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go does not import agent/config
- [ ] All cmd/ + server/ calls to NewServiceFromWorkDir continue to work
- [ ] cd cli && go build ./... clean; tests pass
description:
cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go currently loads agent config in DDx (agentconfig.Load) and wraps it in ServiceConfigAdapter to satisfy agentlib.ServiceConfig interface. This pattern was a workaround until ConfigPath was plumbed.

Per CONTRACT-003 ServiceOptions: "ConfigPath string — optional override; default $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/ddx-agent/config.yaml". Agent loads its own config from the path. Verify this works end-to-end and migrate.

Two-step verification:
1. Confirm agentlib.New(ServiceOptions{ConfigPath: ...}) actually loads config from the given path. If yes:
2. Replace NewServiceFromWorkDir to pass ConfigPath instead of constructing ServiceConfigAdapter.

If agent does NOT yet support the ConfigPath load pattern, file an agent-side bead for it (small).

After this lands, serviceconfig.go either (a) deletes entirely, or (b) shrinks to a small workdir-derives-config-path helper.
notes: Pre-condition: agent-99cb5545 (service.New loads ServiceConfig from ConfigPath). Codex review confirmed agent does NOT yet implement this; the agent-side bead is required before this DDx-side migration is feasible.
title: CRITICAL: replace serviceconfig.go ServiceConfigAdapter with Options.ConfigPath plumbing

acceptance: (1) All 5 resolver integration test functions listed above exist and PASS. (2) go test ./internal/server/... -run "TestGraphQLWorkers|TestGraphQLAgentSessions|TestGraphQLExecutions|TestGraphQLPersonas|TestGraphQLCoordinators" -count=1 passes with all tests green. (3) Each test uses real temp server state, not mocks. (4) The test file(s) follow the existing graphql_node_test.go pattern — POST /graphql via httptest, decode JSON response, assert connection shape and a representative data item. (5) Opus review of the added tests confirms they would catch a broken resolver (not just test that the resolver exists).
description:
Systematic acceptance drift: Stage 2.5 (commits), 2.6 (workers+sessions+executions), and 2.7 (personas+coordinatorMetrics) all landed with real resolver implementations but zero resolver-level integration tests added, despite each bead explicitly listing "integration tests against real <source>" as an acceptance criterion. Opus reviewer flagged this consistently.

Why it was allowed to land: underlying stores (workers_test.go, ddxexec, persona loader, land coordinator) have their own unit tests, so go build and go test ./internal/server/... pass cleanly. The resolver-to-store path is unverified but not broken.

Scope: add end-to-end integration tests (same pattern as graphql_node_test.go and graphql_beads_test.go) that spin up server.New with real state and POST GraphQL queries, for:
- TestGraphQLWorkers (ddxgraphql.Workers + WorkersByProject + Worker(id))
- TestGraphQLAgentSessions (ddxgraphql.AgentSessions + AgentSession(id))
- TestGraphQLExecutions (ddxgraphql.ExecDefinitions + ExecRuns + ExecDefinition(id) + ExecRun(id))
- TestGraphQLPersonas (ddxgraphql.Personas + Persona(id) + PersonaByRole + PersonaLoader wiring via direct call not StateProvider)
- TestGraphQLCoordinators (ddxgraphql.Coordinators + CoordinatorMetricsByProject)
- TestGraphQLCommitsWithMutatingDataset (strengthens the existing TestGraphQLCommits to cover mutation-during-pagination, matching the cursor stability concern)

Each test: real temp server, real stores, real GraphQL POST via httptest, assert Relay connection shape and a representative data item. Tests should match the pattern in cli/internal/server/graphql_node_test.go (TestGraphQLNodeInfo) and graphql_beads_test.go (TestGraphQLBeadsPagination).

File: new cli/internal/server/graphql_backfill_test.go or split per-resolver files matching the existing convention.
title: test(graphql): backfill resolver integration tests for Stage 2.5–2.7

acceptance: The DDx pre-push `no-debug` hook runs without regex parse errors in a default ripgrep environment; the debug-scan command fails closed when the scan itself errors; and the hook still blocks actual debug statements it is meant to catch.
description:
<context-digest>
Observed during `git push` in /Users/erik/Projects/ddx after landing tracker commits for execute-loop alpha findings. The DDx pre-push hook `no-debug` invoked `rg` with a look-ahead pattern and emitted a regex parse error, but the hook summary still reported success and the push continued.
</context-digest>

The DDx pre-push `no-debug` hook uses an `rg` pattern that requires PCRE2 look-around support, but it invokes plain `rg` without `--pcre2`. In the current environment that produces a parse error while the hook still exits successfully.

## Reproduction
1. In `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx`, run `git push`.
2. Observe pre-push hook output from `no-debug` containing:
   `rg: regex parse error: ... look-around ... is not supported`
3. Observe the hook summary still marks `no-debug` as passed and the push continues.

## Why this matters
The hook currently gives a false sense of enforcement. A broken regex should either be written in ripgrep-compatible syntax or use `--pcre2`, and failures in the debug scan should not be silently treated as success.
title: Fix pre-push no-debug hook regex compatibility and failure semantics

acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer using the web UI on a real-world-sized workspace (tens of projects × hundreds of beads each), the beads list opens in under a second and the cross-project all-beads view stays usable. Before any perf fix lands, we can reproduce the problem and measure improvement — not guess.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Perf harness exists and is reusable.**
   - Fixture builder generates `N × M` beads across projects with configurable shape (status mix, label density, dep count). Reuses the real `bead.Store` write path (no fake writer).
   - Benchmark covers at minimum: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject`, `beads` (cross-project), `docGraph`, `sessions`. Adding a new target is a single-table-row change.
   - Each target produces p50/p95/p99 for in-process calls and end-to-end HTTP. Results write to a human-readable report (markdown or JSON) plus a machine-readable artifact suitable for CI diffing later.

2. **Baseline recorded.** First run of the harness on the current `main` binary is stored in the bead notes and in `docs/helix/04-observe/perf/YYYY-MM-DD-baseline.md` (or equivalent existing location). Numbers include hardware + go version footer.

3. **BeadsByProject scope fix.** `BeadsByProject` calls a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, …)` that opens exactly one project's store. Unit test asserts only that project's store is opened (no iteration over other projects).

4. **Filter pushdown.** Status/label/projectID filters apply at the per-file / per-entry parse boundary, not post-materialization. Benchmark captures before/after for the common filtered case (e.g., `status=open`).

5. **Perf targets on fixture (10 projects × 500 beads = 5000 beads total):**
   - `bead(id:)` p95 ≤ 50ms in-process / ≤ 200ms HTTP (already covered by `ddx-ad0db8fd`; this bead may inherit the work).
   - `beadsByProject(first=50)` p95 ≤ 75ms in-process / ≤ 250ms HTTP.
   - `beads(first=50, no projectID)` p95 ≤ 200ms in-process / ≤ 500ms HTTP.
   These are targets for the **first** pass (scope + pushdown). If they are not met with (1)+(2), escalate to the in-memory index (step 3 of Part 2 in the description) and update these targets with the new baseline.

6. **Stale-read safety.** Regardless of fix chosen, a test writes a bead via `ddx bead update`, then queries it via GraphQL, then asserts the new value is visible on the very next request. Works under concurrency: N concurrent readers + writers interleaved without torn reads or panics.

7. **CI posture (soft).** The harness is runnable via `make bench-graphql` (or the project's equivalent). CI wiring to fail on >25% regression is a follow-up bead — recorded in this bead's notes with the proposed threshold but not implemented here.

8. **Playwright smoke.** Opening `/beads` on the fixture project returns an interactive (clickable) list within 1s. Opening the cross-project `/beads` page returns within 2s. These are ceilings, not p95 targets — guards against outright regression, not fine-grained perf.

9. **Cross-reference.** This bead's notes list `ddx-ad0db8fd` (bead detail) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions shard/index) as the other two bead/session perf threads that should inherit this harness.
description:
## Observed

Per-project `/beads` page is noticeably slow; the cross-project "all beads" view is substantially slower. Related: `ddx-ad0db8fd` already identified the single-bead-detail slowness caused by the same storage pattern.

## Root cause (same pattern, different call site)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_beads.go:22,40` —

- `Beads` (cross-project) and `BeadsByProject` (single-project) both call `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots(status, label, projectID, search)`.
- `GetBeadSnapshots` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105`) iterates every registered project, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), filters in Go. Cost is `O(projects × beads_per_project)` on every call, independent of the filter/page size.
- All filtering (status, label, project, search) is Go-side, post-read. The page-size window is applied in `beadConnectionFromSnapshots` after the full corpus is already materialized.
- Cross-project "all beads" = same loop, just without the projectID filter. It is the worst case by design.

This is the same shape that's breaking the Sessions feed (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) and single-bead detail (`ddx-ad0db8fd`). A point fix here is insufficient — we need a measurement surface first so we stop relying on anecdote.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Perf harness (foundational; enables everything else)

Introduce a reusable GraphQL perf harness under `cli/internal/server/perf/` or `cli/internal/server/graphql/bench_test.go`:

- Seeded fixture builder: generates N projects × M beads with configurable distribution of status/labels/dependencies. Reuses existing store primitives (no fake data layer).
- Go benchmark + scriptable HTTP runner (wrapper around an already-running server) reporting p50/p95/p99 per query shape.
- Targets covered on day one: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject(projectID, first, after, status, label, search)`, `beads(projectID=nil, first, after, status, label)`, `docGraph`, `sessions` (so the sessions shard work `ddx-2ceb02fa` inherits this harness). New query targets are added by writing a single table row.
- Thresholds are **recorded, not asserted**, in the first pass. CI surfaces a regression if a threshold is breached beyond a configured delta (e.g., +25% p95). Initial pass is "land the harness and record baseline"; a follow-up wires it into CI.

### Part 2 — Fix the scan pattern

Minimum viable, in priority order:

1. **Scope by project when the query is scoped.** `BeadsByProject` should call a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, filters…)` that only opens that one project's store. Trivial change; eliminates the cross-project factor for the common case.
2. **Early filter pushdown.** `GetBeadSnapshots` should apply status/label/projectID filters at the file-parse boundary where cheap, not after materializing every bead. (Still reads every file — deeper fix below.)
3. **Lightweight in-memory index with invalidation.** After (1) and (2), if "all beads" or search is still slow, introduce an index keyed `{projectID, beadID}` with basic metadata (status, labels, title, updatedAt). Refresh on bead write + debounced filesystem watch. Full-body reads stay lazy — the index never holds bodies. This is the same storage discipline we adopted for sessions in `ddx-2ceb02fa`.

The harness from Part 1 is the gate for choosing between (2) and (3); don't commit to the index without numbers.

## Out of scope

- Full-text search infrastructure (tracked separately if needed).
- Changing the on-disk bead format.
- Cross-node federation performance. Current concern is a single server reading a single workspace.

notes:
## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.
**AC §9 — Bead notes cross-reference**: The acceptance criteria require this bead's notes to list `ddx-ad0db8fd` and `ddx-2ceb02fa`. The baseline `.md` and code comments mention them, but no `ddx bead update` call or notes update appears in the diff. If the notes were updated via a separate mechanism not captured in this branch, this finding can be dismissed.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline and perf-thread cross-reference

Baseline recorded in `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and JSON sibling from the 10 projects x 500 beads fixture (5,000 beads total, 1,000 session rows). Environment: `go=go1.26.2 goos=linux goarch=arm64 cpu-cores=12`.

Key p95 results: `bead` 6.72ms in-process / 6.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 8.84ms in-process / 9.29ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.16ms in-process / 75.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.45ms in-process / 6.87ms HTTP.

Cross-reference for inherited harness work: `ddx-ad0db8fd` covers single-bead detail lookup; `ddx-2ceb02fa` covers sessions shard/index work. Both should use/extend this GraphQL perf harness.

Review follow-up: AC §8 cross-project Playwright smoke now navigates to `/nodes/{nodeId}/beads` and exercises the `beads` GraphQL query instead of the project-scoped `beadsByProject` path. Proposed CI posture remains a follow-up +25% p95 regression gate, not implemented in this bead.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline refresh after session-index fixture alignment

Re-ran `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` after updating the perf fixture to seed current pointer-only session-index shards instead of legacy `sessions.jsonl`. Refreshed artifacts: `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and `.json`.

Refreshed p95 results: `bead` 7.23ms in-process / 7.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 7.18ms in-process / 7.83ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.49ms in-process / 67.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.74ms in-process / 7.73ms HTTP; `agentSessions` 13.45ms in-process / 14.19ms HTTP.

## 2026-04-23 — Final `make bench-graphql` verification

After fixing `cli/Makefile` to pass an absolute `DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DIR`, `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` writes to the intended repo docs path. Final p95 results from that run: `bead` 6.76ms in-process / 7.20ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 6.88ms in-process / 7.85ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 63.22ms in-process / 71.82ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.75ms in-process / 7.56ms HTTP; `agentSessions` 13.24ms in-process / 13.64ms HTTP.
